docs.title: D1.1 — ROADMAP
docs.content:
# D1.1 — ROADMAP
**Autonomous AI Organization — Master Execution Spine**
_Last updated: 2026-03-16 | Status: Active_

> ⚠️ **SUPERSEDED ASSUMPTIONS (2026-03-16):** Prior versions of this document used the codenames
> `bridge`, `brain`, and `forge` for the three crew agents. Those names are retired.
> The canonical crew model is now **Nami / Franky / Chopper**. All references below have been updated.
> Phase 1–2 bootstrap is complete. Current active phase is **Phase A — Infrastructure**.

---

## 1. Mission

Build and operate an autonomous organization of AI agents that produces real value 24 hours a day without requiring constant human attention. The human role is architect, director, and judge — not operator.

The system must be:
- **Durable** — survives reboots, outages, and drift without manual recovery
- **Legible** — every agent, role, and decision can be read, traced, and audited
- **Incrementally delegated** — autonomy is earned per task type, not granted wholesale
- **Self-improving** — the system gets better over time through deliberate research and integration

---

## 2. Current Reality

### Hardware (as of 2026-03-16)

| Machine | Crew Name | Role | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mac Mini (Apple M4, 16 GB RAM) | **Nami** | Chief of Staff / Navigator / Orchestrator | **Active** — OpenClaw 2026.3.13, gateway running on launchd, IP: 10.0.0.152 (Ethernet, DHCP reserved) |
| Desktop | **Franky** | Chief Engineer / Builder | **Active** — OpenClaw running, IP: 10.0.0.251, SSH confirmed, passwordless access from Nami |
| GPU3060 Laptop | **Chopper** | QA / Health / Security Manager | **Active** — OpenClaw running, IP: 10.0.0.16, SSH confirmed, passwordless access from Nami |
| MacBook Air | Cihan (Human) | Architect, Director, Judge | Active — remote access, primary control hub |

> ~~MacBook Air as Control Hub / HQ~~ — superseded. Mac Mini (Nami) is the operational hub.
> ~~Desktop as "Brain node (Planned)"~~ — superseded. Desktop is Franky, active.
> ~~GPU3060 as "Forge node (Planned)"~~ — superseded. GPU3060 is Chopper, active.
> ~~Pixel / Seeker as Edge execution~~ — deferred indefinitely; not relevant to current phase.

### Network (confirmed 2026-03-16)

- All three crew machines are on the home LAN at 10.0.0.x with DHCP reservations.
- Nami: 10.0.0.152 (Ethernet en0, reserved)
- Franky: 10.0.0.251 (reserved)
- Chopper: 10.0.0.16 (reserved)
- Nami ↔ Franky SSH: passwordless, keypair installed
- Nami ↔ Chopper SSH: passwordless, keypair installed
- Inter-crew OpenClaw gateway RPC: operational (ws:// over LAN, token-authenticated)

> ~~Basement switch isolated segment~~ — superseded. Switch is uplinked; all machines on shared LAN.
> ~~Desktop and GPU3060 not yet powered on~~ — superseded. Both active.

### Software

- OpenClaw 2026.3.13 running on Nami (Mac Mini), Franky (Desktop), Chopper (GPU3060)
- All gateways are launchd-managed, auto-start on login
- Nami primary model: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 (via setup-token auth)
- Franky primary model: claude-sonnet-4-6
- Chopper primary model: openai-codex/gpt-5.4
- Local inference: Ollama 0.17.7 on Nami — qwen2.5:3b (Local-Nano) + qwen2.5:7b (Local-Mid)
- Communication channels: Telegram (StrawHats HQ forum group, @CihanHawkBot), Discord (The Straw Hats)
- Mission Control dashboard: running on Nami at :18800

> ~~No AI model API key configured yet~~ — superseded. Anthropic active (SEC-001, SEC-003).
> ~~No agents defined yet beyond the default main~~ — superseded. Three-agent crew active.
> ~~No channels connected~~ — superseded. Telegram + Discord both operational.

### Legacy Assets to Carry Forward

Prior systems exist and contain real, recoverable value. These must be inventoried before any replacement work begins. Rebuilding from scratch without surveying what already works is waste.

Known asset categories:
- **n8n workflows** on Franky (Desktop) — automation pipelines built previously; status unknown, must be triaged
- **Discord / bot setup concepts** — prior bot configuration, channel structure, and webhook logic
- **Commerce / listing automation** — prior scripts or workflows for product listing, pricing, or fulfillment
- **SSH configs and runbooks** — prior remote access setup, known hosts, convenience scripts
- **Dashboard concepts** — prior UI sketches, data sources, or monitoring setups
- **Prompt packs** — prior agent prompts, system instructions, few-shot libraries

Each asset must be classified before Phase A closes:
- **Keep** — functional and compatible, migrate as-is
- **Adapt** — valuable intent, needs rewriting for new architecture
- **Retire** — superseded, redundant, or broken beyond use

This inventory lives in D5.3 LEGACY-ASSET-REGISTER.md.

### Mission Control MVP Scope

The first version of Mission Control does not need to be visually complete. It only needs to be legible and authoritative. It must serve as the source of truth for:

- **Task queue** — what work is active, queued, or blocked
- **Machine status** — which nodes are online and what they are running
- **Document set** — which D-series documents exist and their current state
- **Delegation status** — which task types have been delegated and at what maturity level
- **Proof links** — references to output artifacts and logs from completed delegations
- **Improvement intake** — references to incoming research, experiments, and signals
- **Experiment registry** — log of what has been tried, with outcomes

Beautiful UI is deferred. Legibility and accuracy come first. D1.2 ARCHITECTURE.md defines where Mission Control lives; this scope defines what it must contain before Phase A exits.

---

## 3. Planning Principles

1. **Document before building.** Every phase starts with a written spec, not code.
2. **One working thing beats three half-built things.** Complete each layer before adding the next.
3. **Real agents are few. Logical roles are many.** Three runtime agents carry all named roles internally. Do not create a new agent for every new capability.
4. **Earn autonomy.** New task types start supervised. Delegation expands only when track record justifies it.
5. **Local inference first.** Every worker machine runs local models. Cloud inference is a fallback, not a dependency.
6. **Avoid heartbeat noise.** Agents report on events and outcomes, not on existence. Silence is a valid state.
7. **The source of truth is Mission Control.** Telegram is the primary coordination channel. Logs are evidence. Code is implementation.
8. **Improvement is continuous but deliberate.** Articles, X posts, and experiments feed the system through defined intake lanes, not ad hoc sprints.
9. **Migrate before rebuilding.** Existing workflows, prompts, automations, docs, and infrastructure from prior systems must be inventoried before replacement work begins. Assume prior work has value until proven otherwise.
10. **Execution layers are subordinate.** Tools like n8n, scripts, browser automation, and external workflow systems are execution substrates — not the strategic brain and not the source of truth. They serve Mission Control; they do not replace it.
11. **Work moves through packets, not vibes.** Meaningful work must be described as a task packet with owner, scope, route, output, and handoff target. Informal instructions that skip this structure are not delegated work — they are chat.
12. **Every delegated task leaves proof.** Delegated execution must emit evidence — a log, a file, a structured result — not just a chat reply. Proof is what allows review, audit, and improvement.
13. **Agents may generate child prompts only within scope.** Prompt generation is allowed as part of delegated execution, but only inside an approved task class, with bounded depth and defined handoff rules. Unbounded prompt generation is not delegation — it is drift.
14. **Scale must respect reality.** The system is intended to overcome human execution bottlenecks, but must remain grounded in real hardware limits, model limits, and operational reliability. Ambition that ignores constraints produces technical debt, not capability.
15. **The swarm is self-managed within approved boundaries.** Once a goal, backlog, policy, workflow, or task class is approved, agents are expected to identify eligible work, pull tasks, sequence, execute, hand off, and close work on their own — without the human needing to push each step. Backlog hygiene, task pickup, estimation, sequencing, and workflow reuse are part of the swarm's operating responsibility. The human sets mission, priorities, constraints, approvals, and escalation thresholds. Agents manage execution. Self-managed, but not self-authorizing beyond policy.
16. **Tool lanes are self-managed within approved policy.** Three designated tool lanes serve specific task classes: OpenClaw (primary autonomous runtime — orchestration, scheduling, governance, and default lane for approved backlog work), Cursor (supervised/manual engineering lane — code, QA, document authoring; requires human-in-the-loop or explicit task assignment), Perplexity (live web research, current-information retrieval, browser/computer-use, and preferred UI-pilot lane for app/device/chatbox interaction when activated). OpenClaw is the primary autonomous executor — approved backlog work defaults to OpenClaw, not Cursor. Cursor is a supervised execution surface, not the swarm's autonomous runtime. Cursor may also be used indirectly through device-mediated control (Perplexity or OpenClaw operating the Cursor UI without direct API integration) — this is a legitimate documented access path, not a workaround. Once tool lane assignments are approved in policy, agents select the correct lane automatically for each task without per-task human instruction. Tool lane policy changes go through D1.15 change control. Within approved policy, lane selection is an execution decision, not an approval gate.
17. **Access minimization is an operating principle.** Agents prefer access paths that are stable, low-maintenance, and do not require ongoing human credential management or device interaction. API access is not always the preferred path — if a built-in OpenClaw capability or a stable local integration can fulfill the same need without an external dependency, that path is preferred. Device-mediated or computer-use fallback lanes are a last resort for access tasks that cannot be satisfied by stable paths, and are governed as a candidate capability class (not an ad-hoc improvisation). The goal is to minimize the number of access surfaces the organization must maintain, not to maximize what agents can reach.

---

## 4. Real Runtime Shape

### The Three Crew Agents

```
Nami     →  Mac Mini (M4, 10.0.0.152)    Chief of Staff, Navigator, Orchestrator
                                           Routing, scheduling, comms brokering, gateway management
Franky   →  Desktop (10.0.0.251)          Chief Engineer / Builder
                                           Code, builds, deployments, engineering execution
Chopper  →  GPU3060 (10.0.0.16)           QA / Health / Security Manager
                                           Verification, test results, security checks, watchdog
```

> ~~bridge → Mac Mini~~ — superseded by **Nami**
> ~~brain → Desktop~~ — superseded by **Franky**
> ~~forge → GPU3060~~ — superseded by **Chopper**

### Logical Roles (hosted inside agents, not separate processes)

| Role | Lives In | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| Conductor | Nami | Coordinates task flow across agents |
| Broker | Nami | Routes messages, manages channel I/O |
| Navigator | Nami | Strategic planning, sprint management |
| Builder | Franky | Code generation and execution |
| Engineer | Franky | Architecture, deployments, infrastructure builds |
| Critic | Chopper | QA, testing, evaluation |
| Watchdog | Chopper | Health monitoring, security checks, incident detection |
| Researcher | Franky / Chopper | Reads sources, extracts signal (as needed) |

These roles are logical labels, not separate OpenClaw agents. They describe what a given agent prompt context is doing at a given moment.

### Tool Lanes

Three designated tool lanes serve distinct task classes. Agents select the appropriate lane automatically within approved policy (D1.1 Principle 16):

| Tool Lane | Task Classes | Status |
|---|---|---|
| OpenClaw | Primary autonomous runtime. Orchestration, scheduling, cron, inter-agent dispatch, gateway management. Default lane for approved backlog work. | Active |
| Cursor | Supervised/manual engineering lane. Code generation, QA, document authoring, configuration. Requires human-in-the-loop or explicit task assignment. Not the autonomous runtime. | Active (supervised) |
| Perplexity | Live web research, current-information retrieval, browser tasks, computer-use. Preferred UI-pilot lane for app/device/chatbox interaction. | Candidate — see D5.7 CAP-006 |

**OpenClaw is the primary autonomous runtime.** Approved backlog work, Mission Control buildout, and self-managed swarm execution default to OpenClaw. Cursor is the supervised/manual engineering surface — it is used when a human is intentionally doing assisted work, when OpenClaw needs a manual engineering fallback, or when a specific engineering task is explicitly assigned to the Cursor lane. Cursor is not the autonomous executor.

**Visual/UI acceptance requires human visual approval.** No dashboard, Mission Control screen, visual surface, or user-facing interface is accepted just because an agent marks it done. Final acceptance for visual surfaces requires human visual approval.

### Control Flow

```
Cihan (Human — MacBook Air / Telegram)
    ↓ intent / directive
Mission Control (source of truth — Nami)
    ↓ task assignment via OpenClaw gateway RPC
Nami (Mac Mini) — dispatches, schedules, monitors
    ├── Franky (Desktop) — builds, engineers, deploys
    └── Chopper (GPU3060) — verifies, audits, monitors
    ↓ results
Telegram StrawHats HQ / Discord The Straw Hats → Cihan review
```

### Communication Channels

- **Telegram StrawHats HQ** — primary command bridge (7 forum topics: General, Planning, Briefing, Research, Finances, Life, Commerce)
- **Discord The Straw Hats** — crew workshop (7 channels: #ops, #dev, #qa, #tasks, #memory, #research-tech, #devops)

---

## 5. Phase Map

> **Current active phase: Phase A — Infrastructure (Sprint 0 — Foundation)**
> Bootstrap phases 0–3 from the prior model are superseded by the sprint-based crew model below.
> Phase 0 and Phase 1 are complete. Phase 2–3 are complete as of crew activation (2026-03-15).

---

### ✅ Phase 0 — Foundation _(complete)_
Mac Mini set up, always-on, remote-accessible, running OpenClaw gateway.

**All exit criteria met:**
- [x] Mac Mini never sleeps, restarts after power failure
- [x] SSH + Screen Sharing confirmed working remotely
- [x] OpenClaw gateway running under launchd
- [x] Model API key configured (SEC-001, SEC-003 active)
- [x] Basement switch uplinked to router — all machines on 10.0.0.x
- [x] Mac Mini on stable Ethernet IP with DHCP reservation (10.0.0.152)

---

### ✅ Phase 1 — Bridge Online _(complete)_
Mac Mini fully configured, model-connected OpenClaw node. First agent defined and running. Telegram connected.

**All exit criteria met:**
- [x] Model API key set (Anthropic claude-sonnet-4-6 via setup-token auth)
- [x] Local inference running on Mac Mini (Ollama qwen2.5:3b + 7b, CHG-007)
- [x] Local-vs-cloud routing policy defined (D1.5 MODEL_POLICY.md)
- [x] Nami agent defined in OpenClaw with correct workspace
- [x] Telegram channel connected (@CihanHawkBot, SEC-002 active)
- [x] Nami can receive a directive via Telegram and reply (confirmed CHG-006)
- [x] D1.2, D1.3, D1.4 exist and are accurate
- [x] Bridge Runbook written (D2.2-BRIDGE-RUNBOOK.md)

---

### ✅ Phase 2 — Cluster Connected _(complete, 2026-03-15)_
Desktop (Franky) and GPU3060 (Chopper) online, on LAN, OpenClaw running, paired to Nami's gateway.

**All exit criteria met:**
- [x] Switch uplinked; all three machines on 10.0.0.x with DHCP reservations
- [x] OpenClaw running on Franky (10.0.0.251) and Chopper (10.0.0.16)
- [x] Franky and Chopper agents defined and reachable via gateway RPC
- [x] Basic machine-to-machine task routing tested (Nami → Franky, Nami → Chopper — CREW-001 PASS)
- [x] Inter-crew communication verified end-to-end (Nami assigns, Franky builds, Chopper verifies)

---

### ✅ Phase 3 — First Delegation _(complete, 2026-03-15)_
CREW-001 (Crew Alive Check) confirmed end-to-end task routing, assignment, execution, and verification across all three agents.

**Exit criteria met:**
- [x] End-to-end crew task loop demonstrated (CREW-001)
- [x] Nami assigns tasks via gateway RPC
- [x] Franky executes and produces artifacts
- [x] Chopper verifies via SSH and independent gateway
- [x] Results logged in Mission Control

---

### 🟡 Phase A — Infrastructure _(active — Sprint 0: Foundation)_
Crew comms, channels, documentation, and governance are fully online. Sprint-based task execution model is live.

**Exit criteria:**
- [x] Telegram StrawHats HQ forum set up (7 topics, routing configured)
- [x] Discord The Straw Hats set up (7 channels live)
- [x] Daily digest cron configured and routing to Briefing topic
- [x] Heartbeat routing to group (not DM)
- [x] CREW-001 complete (first crew task loop)
- [x] CREW-002 complete (D1.1 ROADMAP updated to crew model) — completed 2026-03-16 by Franky
- [ ] D1.2 ARCHITECTURE.md updated to crew model
- [ ] D1.4 ORG_MAP.md updated to crew roles
- [ ] Legacy asset inventory started on Franky
- [ ] Sprint 0 retrospective and Sprint 1 planning complete

---

### Phase B — Continuous Operation _(next)_
The crew runs, improves itself, and produces value on a daily cadence without daily human intervention.

**Exit criteria:**
- [ ] Daily digest delivered to Briefing topic (Telegram) each morning
- [ ] At least one improvement per week sourced from intake lanes
- [ ] Failure recovery is automatic for all common failure modes
- [ ] Autonomy maturity level is at least 2 for core task types across all three agents
- [ ] D4.1 OPS-HANDBOOK.md written and current

---

### Phase C — Scale and Extend _(future)_
Add new capability streams, edge nodes, commerce automation, content engines.

_Intentionally underspecified. Design this phase when Phase B is stable._

---

## 6. Document Creation Sequence

Documents are created in dependency order. Do not write a document whose parent is incomplete.

```
D1.x — Organization Layer
  D1.1   ROADMAP.md                  ← this file
  D1.2   ARCHITECTURE.md             hardware topology, network, agent topology
  D1.3   REQUIREMENTS.md             functional requirements per agent and phase
  D1.4   ORG_MAP.md                  org structure, roles, ownership, decision rights
  D1.5   MODEL_POLICY.md             model selection, local-vs-cloud routing, fallback rules
  D1.6   LOCAL_INFERENCE_PLAN.md     per-machine model stack, hardware constraints, benchmarks
  D1.7   MEMORY_POLICY.md            what gets stored, how, retention, retrieval design
  D1.8   AUTOMATION_POLICY.md        what can be automated, approval gates, risk classification
  D1.9   BROKER_POLICY.md            message routing rules, channel priorities, escalation
  D1.10  X_RESEARCH_POLICY.md        how X/Grok content enters the system and gets processed
  D1.11  COMMUNICATION_MODES.md      Discord structure, digest format, alert levels
  D1.12  CAPABILITY_ACQUISITION_POLICY.md  how new skills, plugins, and tools are evaluated and adopted
  D1.13  ENV_CONTROL.md              environment configuration, secrets handling, machine state
  D1.14  SECRETS_POLICY.md           what secrets exist, where they live, rotation rules
  D1.15  CHANGE_CONTROL.md           how changes to agents, config, and infrastructure are made
  D1.16  MEMORY_RND.md               multimodal memory research agenda and experiment log

D2.x — Phase Runbooks
  D2.1   ACTIVATION-PACK.md          Phase 0/1 activation steps (complete)
  D2.2   BRIDGE-RUNBOOK.md           how to operate, recover, and update Nami (Mac Mini)
  D2.3   CLUSTER-RUNBOOK.md          how to bring up, connect, and manage Franky + Chopper

D3.x — Delegation Specs
  D3.1   DELEGATION-SPEC-RESEARCH.md
  D3.2   DELEGATION-SPEC-BUILD.md
  D3.3   DELEGATION-SPEC-MEMORY.md
  D3.4   DELEGATION-SPEC-CONTENT.md
  D3.5   DELEGATION-SPEC-COMMERCE.md

D4.x — Operations
  D4.1   OPS-HANDBOOK.md             daily ops, maintenance, upgrade process
  D4.2   RECOVERY-PLAYBOOK.md        failure modes and recovery steps

D5.x — Improvement System
  D5.1   INTAKE-LANES.md             how research, experiments, and X posts enter the system
  D5.2   SKILL-REGISTRY.md           known skills, plugins, acquisition plan
  D5.3   LEGACY-ASSET-REGISTER.md    inventory of prior workflows, prompts, and infrastructure
  D5.4   EXPERIMENT-REGISTRY.md      log of all experiments, outcomes, and adoption decisions
  D5.5   WORKFLOW-MIGRATION-PLAN.md  migration path from prior n8n / automation stack to new architecture
  D5.6   WORKFLOW_LIBRARY.md         approved reusable workflow definitions; lifecycle and promotion rules
  D5.7   CAPABILITY_REGISTER.md      master register of all approved capabilities; versioning and compliance
```

---

## 7. Autonomy Maturity Ladder

Each task type is rated independently on this scale. The overall system maturity is determined by the lowest-rated critical task type.

| Level | Name | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Manual | Human does the work directly |
| 1 | Assisted | Agent drafts, human executes |
| 2 | Supervised | Agent executes, human reviews every result |
| 3 | Delegated | Agent executes, human spot-checks on schedule |
| 4 | Trusted | Agent executes, human reviews only flagged items |
| 5 | Autonomous | Agent executes and self-corrects, human reviews digests |

**Current system maturity: Level 1–2** (crew comms + simple task execution demonstrated).
Target end of Phase A: Level 2 for core task types.
Target end of Phase B: Level 3 for core task types, Level 2 for new task types.

---

## 8. First Delegation Targets

These are the first real-world task types to hand off to agents, chosen because they are low-risk, reversible, and produce clear outputs.

| # | Task Type | Agent | Risk | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Research summary | Franky (Engineer) | Low | Written brief delivered to Telegram / Discord |
| 2 | Code scaffold | Franky (Builder) | Low | File output in workspace |
| 3 | Knowledge archiving | Nami (Navigator) | Low | Indexed entry in memory store |
| 4 | Task scheduling | Nami (Conductor) | Medium | Scheduled job in OpenClaw cron |
| 5 | Build + QA | Franky (Builder) + Chopper (Critic) | Medium | Pass/fail result with log |
| 6 | Legacy asset migration brief | Franky + Nami | Low | Keep/adapt/retire recommendation doc per asset category |
| 7 | Security audit | Chopper (Watchdog) | Low | Health report delivered to #qa |

Each target gets a Delegation Spec (D3.x) before being activated.

---

## 9. Improvement Lanes

These are the defined channels through which new capabilities, knowledge, and methods enter the system. Intake happens deliberately, not reactively.

### Research Lane
- Source: X/Grok posts, articles, YouTube, arXiv, blogs
- Owner: Franky (Engineer/Researcher)
- Output: brief filed to Nami (memory), summary posted to Telegram Briefing / Discord #research-tech
- Cadence: on trigger or scheduled sweep

### Experiment Lane
- Source: human-initiated experiments with new tools, models, or methods
- Owner: human + Franky
- Output: result doc in D5.x or docs, pass/fail verdict, adoption decision

### Skill/Plugin Lane
- Source: OpenClaw skills marketplace, community, custom builds
- Owner: human approves, Nami installs
- Output: new capability registered in D5.2 SKILL-REGISTRY.md

### Model Lane
- Source: new local or API models
- Owner: human evaluates, Franky / Chopper benchmarks
- Output: model added to inference roster or rejected with reason

### Environment/Change Lane
- Source: macOS updates, OpenClaw updates, hardware changes
- Owner: Nami monitors, human approves changes
- Output: updated runbooks, config committed to docs

### Cost / Usage Lane
- Source: model usage logs, token burn rates, provider billing, recurring automation load
- Owner: Nami (finance-facing routing) with Telegram Finances topic as output channel
- Output: visibility into what the system costs to run, where usage is concentrated, and where optimization is warranted
- Cadence: reviewed per sprint and when new models or automations are introduced

### Security / Health Lane
- Source: Chopper watchdog, SSH audits, gateway health checks
- Owner: Chopper (Watchdog)
- Output: health reports to Discord #qa, alerts to Telegram #ops

---

## 9.5 Strategic Outcome Tracks

These are the five long-term value streams the organization is building toward. Each track is a distinct domain of work that agents will eventually own and operate. They are not phases — they run in parallel once the foundation is stable.

### Track 1 — Personal Operating System / Jarvis Track
The system manages the human's schedule, communications, research intake, task queue, and ambient awareness. The human receives digests, makes decisions, and delegates execution. The goal is to eliminate low-value cognitive overhead from the human's day entirely.

### Track 2 — Content and Traffic Engine Track
The system produces written, visual, or structured content from research and directives. It publishes, distributes, and monitors performance. Managed by Franky (Builder, Researcher) with Nami (Navigator) providing orchestration.

### Track 3 — Commerce and Sales Engine Track
The system manages product listings, pricing logic, fulfillment triggers, and sales channel activity. Prior commerce automation is the migration baseline. Nami routes; Franky builds; Chopper verifies.

### Track 4 — Financial Operating System Track
The system tracks revenue, costs, and margins across all active operations. It produces financial digests, flags threshold breaches, and maintains a live picture of the organization's economic state. Outputs to Telegram Finances topic.

### Track 5 — Experimentation and Intelligence Engine Track
The system continuously runs small experiments — new models, new tools, new methods, new prompts — and files results into D5.4 EXPERIMENT-REGISTRY.md. Franky owns execution; Chopper owns verification; human approves adoption decisions.

---

## 10. Completion Standard Per Phase

A phase is complete when:
1. All exit criteria are checked off
2. All phase documents are written and accurate
3. At least one human review session has verified the system behaves as documented
4. No open critical failures exist
5. The next phase's first document has been drafted

A phase is **not** complete because the code works. It is complete when the system is documented, verified, and stable.

---

## 11. Immediate Next Steps (Phase A — Sprint 0)

In order. Do not skip ahead.

1. ~~**Complete CREW-002**~~ — Done. D1.1 updated to crew model (Nami/Franky/Chopper), phase map current, superseded assumptions marked. Completed 2026-03-16 by Franky.

2. **Update D1.2 — ARCHITECTURE.md**
   Capture confirmed crew machine specs, LAN topology (10.0.0.x), agent-to-machine bindings, OpenClaw config paths per machine, and gateway RPC topology

3. **Update D1.4 — ORG_MAP.md**
   Replace bridge/brain/forge role assignments with Nami/Franky/Chopper. Map Telegram topics and Discord channels to crew responsibilities.

4. **Start legacy asset inventory on Franky**
   Survey Desktop for n8n workflows, prior commerce automation, bot configs, dashboard concepts, prompt packs, and SSH runbooks. File results in D5.3 LEGACY-ASSET-REGISTER.md.

5. **Sprint 0 retrospective + Sprint 1 planning**
   Review what worked in crew activation. Define Sprint 1 task list. Assign first real-work tasks to Franky and Chopper.

6. **Activate daily digest routing**
   Confirm morning digest is posting to Telegram Briefing topic (thread 8). Verify format is useful.

7. **Define Chopper's first security audit scope**
   Assign Chopper a health check task covering all three machines. Output to Discord #qa.

---

_This document is the master spine. All other documents reference it. Update the exit criteria checkboxes as milestones are completed. The crew model (Nami/Franky/Chopper) is now canonical — do not revert to bridge/brain/forge terminology._

docs.title: D1.10 — X_RESEARCH_POLICY
docs.content:
# D1.10 — X_RESEARCH_POLICY
**Autonomous AI Organization — How X/Twitter Research Enters the System, Is Filtered, Validated, and Routed**
_Last updated: 2026-03-11 | Status: Active | Parent: D1.1-ROADMAP.md_

---

## 1. Purpose

This document defines how X/Twitter-derived research enters the organization, how it is filtered and validated, and under what conditions the Grok/xAI API may be activated as a research processing layer.

X is an important improvement and opportunity-discovery lane for this organization. It surfaces model and tool developments, architecture patterns, content opportunities, commerce signals, and early intelligence on changes that affect the system's strategic tracks. Used well, it accelerates the Experimentation and Intelligence Engine (Track 5). Used carelessly, it is noise that produces false urgency, premature tool changes, and drift.

**This document governs:**
- How X content is submitted to the organization (intake modes)
- Which sources are permitted and at what trust tier
- What a research packet is and what it must contain
- How signals are classified and validated
- What the Grok/xAI activation gate requires
- What valid outputs of X research look like
- How human-facing research summaries are formatted
- Automation boundaries for each intake mode

**What this document does not cover:**
- General content strategy or social media management — that is Track 2 (D1.8 §16)
- The full intake lane system — D5.1-INTAKE-LANES.md
- Experiment filing and outcome tracking — D5.4-EXPERIMENT-REGISTRY.md
- Grok API credentials — D1.14-SECRETS_POLICY.md
- Research delegation spec — D3.1-DELEGATION-SPEC-RESEARCH.md

---

## 2. Scope

This policy covers X content that enters the organization as research. X content used for other purposes (e.g. publishing to X as part of Track 2) is governed by D1.8 §16 Track 2, not by this document.

**In scope:**
- X posts, threads, and profile content submitted by the human
- X content processed by forge (Researcher) as part of a research task
- Grok/xAI API outputs used to analyze or extract signal from X content (when activated)

**Out of scope:**
- Posting to X or managing a presence on X — that is a Track 2 output operation
- LinkedIn, Reddit, newsletters, arXiv, or other research sources — those are part of the broader intake lane system in D5.1, not this document
- General web search or news research — separate from X research policy

---

## 3. Policy Principles

1. **X posts are not authoritative by default.** A post — regardless of the author's reputation — is an unvalidated claim until it is cross-referenced, tested, or confirmed by another source. No X post alone constitutes sufficient evidence to change architecture, policy, or production behavior.

2. **Signal extraction is the goal, not post collection.** The organization is not building a database of interesting tweets. It is extracting specific, actionable signals that can be routed to an appropriate destination: an experiment, a content idea, a capability evaluation, a market observation. Posts without extractable signal are noise.

3. **Noise is the default; signal is the exception.** The X environment is high-noise. When the system processes X content, it should assume most of it is not actionable. Aggressive filtering is a feature, not a shortcoming.

4. **Every finding passes through a research packet before it becomes anything else.** Raw post content never directly reaches the canonical memory store, the experiment registry, the content pipeline, or architecture documents. It goes through a research packet first. The packet is the filter.

5. **Raw posts never directly change production behavior.** No X post — not even one about a critical OpenClaw bug or a new model release — directly updates a policy document, modifies an agent configuration, or changes a running automation. It opens a research task, which may eventually result in a controlled change through the appropriate change control process (D1.15-CHANGE_CONTROL.md).

6. **Quality over volume.** A weekly digest of five validated, high-signal findings is more valuable than a daily flood of loosely filtered posts. The system must err on the side of extracting less and validating more.

7. **Grok is not active and is not assumed.** Phase 1 and Phase 2 X research operates without Grok. All processing uses local models or the human's own judgment. The Grok activation gate is defined in §9 and requires a specific set of conditions before it may be activated.

---

## 4. Intake Modes

X research enters the organization through three modes, each active at a different phase and each with a different automation level.

### Mode 1 — Manual Submission (Phase 1)
**Description:** The human pastes an X link, copies post text, attaches a screenshot, or types a summary of something they saw. This is submitted via Telegram to bridge (the primary coordination channel), which creates a research packet and routes it for processing.

**Who acts:** Human (source identification); bridge (packet creation); forge (Researcher) if processing is delegated, or human processes directly.

**Automation level:** None. The human initiates every intake event. Bridge assists with packet formatting.

**Phase:** Active from Phase 1.

### Mode 2 — Structured Intake (Phase 2)
**Description:** The human uses a defined intake template when submitting X content. The template includes fields for source URL or text, source account, perceived signal type, and priority. Bridge processes the template automatically: creates the research packet, routes to forge (Researcher) for extraction, and files the output.

**Who acts:** Human (source + template); bridge and forge (automated packet processing).

**Automation level:** Level 2 (supervised). Human submits; system processes and returns a summary for review. Requires a written delegation spec (D3.1) before activation.

**Phase:** Phase 2 target.

### Mode 3 — Automated Research Sweep (Phase 3)
**Description:** A defined list of high-signal X accounts or topics is monitored on a schedule. Forge (Researcher) processes new posts, extracts signal, and files findings without human initiation per item. The human receives a digest of validated findings.

**Who acts:** Forge (Researcher) — automated; bridge (Broker) — delivers digest; human — reviews digest and makes decisions.

**Automation level:** Level 2 required for sweep; individual validated findings that would trigger experiments require Level 3 approval for the resulting experiment, not for the sweep itself.

**Activation gate:** Mode 3 may not be activated until:
1. A curated account/topic list has been reviewed and approved by the human
2. Noise filtering thresholds have been confirmed through Mode 1 and Mode 2 experience
3. D5.1-INTAKE-LANES.md formalizes the research lane
4. A written delegation spec for automated research sweeps exists in D3.1
5. The circuit breaker in §13 is configured

**Phase:** Phase 3 target.

---

## 5. Allowed Sources

### 5.1 Source Types Permitted

| Source Type | Permitted | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Public X posts (any account) | Yes — with trust tier applied | Trust tier affects validation weight, not whether it can be submitted |
| X threads | Yes | Treated as a single research unit |
| X profile context (bio, pinned post) | Yes — for account credibility assessment only | Not a research source in itself |
| Grok-generated summaries of X content | Conditional | Only when Grok is activated per §9 |
| Screenshots of X content | Yes — with caveats | Must include account handle and post date; screenshots cannot be used alone as high-confidence evidence |

### 5.2 Source Types Prohibited

| Source Type | Prohibited | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Private/protected X accounts | Yes | Accessing protected content without consent is outside policy |
| DMs or private communications | Yes | Private communications are not research sources |
| Content requiring paid X subscription features to access | Yes | Credential exposure risk; declared in D1.14 if relevant |
| Synthetic or AI-generated posts presented as organic | Yes | Validation cannot be applied reliably; flag and discard |

### 5.3 Source Trust Tiers

Trust tier affects how much validation is required before a finding is treated as confirmed. It does not prevent submission.

| Tier | Description | Required Validation Before Acting |
|---|---|---|
| High | Account known to the human; track record of accurate, domain-relevant posts | One corroborating source or human judgment sufficient |
| Medium | Account unfamiliar but in a relevant domain; reasonable follower base; posts internally consistent | Two corroborating sources or forge cross-reference |
| Low | Unknown account, anonymous, or account with history of inaccurate posts | Strong corroboration required; cannot be sole basis for any action |
| Unverifiable | Screenshot with no visible account handle, or account since deleted | May not be used as evidence for any action; filed as `observation` only |

---

## 6. Research Packet Policy

Every piece of X content that enters the organization must be converted into a research packet before it is processed or acted on. A research packet is the structured form of a raw research input.

### 6.1 Required Fields

```
---
id: <unique identifier>
date_submitted: YYYY-MM-DD
submitted_by: human | forge
source_url: <X post URL or null>
source_account: <@handle or "unknown">
source_tier: high | medium | low | unverifiable
intake_mode: manual | structured | automated-sweep
signal_type: <from §7.1>
raw_content: <post text, screenshot reference, or summary>
signal_summary: <1–3 sentence extraction of the actionable finding>
validation_status: pending | confirmed | unconfirmed | contradicted | disputed
output_destination: <where this goes if validated — see §10>
---
```

### 6.2 Who Creates the Packet

| Intake Mode | Packet Creator |
|---|---|
| Mode 1 (manual) | Bridge creates the packet from the human's submission. Human reviews and corrects the `signal_summary` field before processing continues. |
| Mode 2 (structured) | Bridge creates the packet automatically from the intake template. No human review before processing, but human reviews the output. |
| Mode 3 (automated) | Forge (Researcher) creates the packet. Bridge logs it. Validated findings are included in the next digest for human review. |

### 6.3 Research Packets Are Not Memory Entries

Research packets are working documents, not canonical memory entries. A validated research packet that produces a finding is the input to a `knowledge` entry in canonical memory (via Archivist). The packet itself lives in working memory (forge) or operational memory (bridge) and is subject to those tiers' retention windows (D1.7 §10).

---

## 7. Signal Classification Policy

### 7.1 Signal Types

Every research packet is classified as one of the following signal types:

| Signal Type | Description | Potential Destination |
|---|---|---|
| `capability` | New model, tool, plugin, or capability relevant to the org's tech stack | Experiment proposal → D5.4; Capability evaluation → D1.12 |
| `architecture` | Pattern or approach used by others that may inform how the org builds | Architecture suggestion → D1.15 review queue |
| `content-opportunity` | Topic, format, or trend with high engagement relevant to Track 2 | Content track queue |
| `commerce-signal` | Product demand, pricing pattern, competitor activity relevant to Track 3 | brain (Merchant) queue |
| `risk` | Security vulnerability, legal development, market threat | Immediate escalation if critical; else queued for review |
| `openClaw` | OpenClaw-specific development, update, community discussion, bug report | Bridge update queue; architecture review if significant |
| `research-reference` | Academic paper, technical post, or deep-dive that expands a topic already in canonical memory | Canonical memory `knowledge` entry (via Archivist) |
| `noise` | Post with no extractable actionable signal after review | Discarded with reason logged |

### 7.2 Signal Strength

After classification, signal strength is assessed:

| Strength | Description | What Happens |
|---|---|---|
| High | Specific, falsifiable, directly relevant, from a credible source | Processed immediately; included in next digest |
| Moderate | Relevant but requires more evidence or context to act on | Filed for later; included in weekly digest |
| Low | Weakly relevant or speculative | Discarded unless part of an emerging pattern |
| Noise | Not actionable under any reasonable interpretation | Discarded; reason logged |

**Pattern escalation:** If 3 or more Low-signal posts from different sources converge on the same topic within a 7-day window, the cluster is re-evaluated as potential Moderate signal. The system does not discard clusters silently.

---

## 8. Validation Policy

### 8.1 Validation Requirement

No validated finding that would result in a system change (experiment, architecture suggestion, capability evaluation) may be based on a single unvalidated X post, regardless of the source's reputation. Validation is required before any finding moves from `pending` to `confirmed`.

### 8.2 Validation Criteria

A finding is considered validated when at least one of the following is true:
1. It can be cross-referenced against a second independent source (documentation, another post, a direct test)
2. It describes a testable claim that forge (Researcher) or the human can verify directly
3. The human explicitly marks it as confirmed based on their own knowledge

A finding is **not** validated solely because:
- It was posted by a high-follower account
- It was widely shared
- It aligns with what the organization hoped to find
- It cannot be immediately disproven

### 8.3 Validation Outputs

| Status | Meaning | What Happens |
|---|---|---|
| `confirmed` | Evidence supports the finding | Route to declared output destination (§10) |
| `unconfirmed` | Insufficient evidence; not disproven | Hold; add to research backlog for further investigation |
| `contradicted` | Evidence contradicts the finding | Discard; log the contradiction in case the topic recurs |
| `disputed` | Mixed evidence; competing claims | File as `observation` in canonical memory noting the dispute; do not act on it |

### 8.4 Who Validates

| Finding Type | Who Validates |
|---|---|
| High Signal findings that would affect architecture or policy | Human validates before output is routed |
| High Signal findings that feed experiments | Forge (Researcher) validates via cross-reference; human reviews the experiment proposal before it is filed to D5.4 |
| Moderate Signal findings | Forge (Researcher) validates; result included in digest for human awareness |
| Low Signal / Noise | Forge (Researcher) classifies and discards; no human review required |

The human is never the sole validator for Low/Noise signals — that would recreate the bottleneck this system is designed to remove.

---

## 9. Research Processing Capability Gates

### 9.0 Perplexity — Alternative Research Processing Capability

Perplexity (D5.7 CAP-006) is a candidate external callable capability that may serve as a research processing layer within this pipeline, complementary to Grok with different strengths: live web retrieval, current news, and structured search.

**Current status:** Not activated. Candidate only. See D5.7 CAP-006 and TASK-013, TASK-014.

**If activated, Perplexity's role in this pipeline would be:**
- Live web retrieval for research packet validation (cross-referencing claims against current sources)
- Mode 2 and Mode 3 research processing where live retrieval adds signal that local models cannot provide
- Augmenting research briefs with current information not in static model weights

**Constraints that apply regardless of research processing tool:**
- The §8 validation policy applies to all external research sources and all processing tools — Perplexity outputs are not authoritative by default
- Findings processed by Perplexity follow the same packet format (§6), signal classification (§7), and validation criteria (§8) as any other finding
- Perplexity does not replace forge (Researcher) — it is a callable helper invoked by forge or bridge within research tasks, not an independent agent

**Activation gate:** Perplexity activation follows D1.12 capability acquisition independently of the Grok gate in §9.3.

---

### 9.1 Current Status

**Grok is not activated.** The Research-External model class (D1.5 §4) is defined but inactive. No xAI API calls are made at Phase 1 or Phase 2.

**Note on Perplexity:** Perplexity (D5.7 CAP-006) is a separate candidate capability for live web research. If activated, it may serve as a research processing layer within this pipeline (cross-referencing claims, live source retrieval, structured web search). It is governed independently of the Grok activation gate — see §9.0 above. All §8 validation rules apply to Perplexity outputs as they do to any research source.

### 9.2 What Grok Would Do If Activated

If activated, Grok/xAI API is used exclusively as a research processing layer within this pipeline:
- Structured extraction of signal from raw X post content
- Topic classification and trend identification across a set of posts
- Cross-referencing claims against xAI's training data (with the same validation requirements as any other source — Grok outputs are not authoritative by default)

Grok is not used for: policy decisions, agent configuration, financial analysis, production task execution, or any purpose outside the X research intake pipeline.

### 9.3 Activation Requirements

All of the following must be true before Grok may be activated:

| # | Requirement | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | D1.10-X_RESEARCH_POLICY.md is written and approved | Satisfied by this document |
| 2 | D1.14-SECRETS_POLICY.md is written and the xAI credential class is governed by it — the actual xAI key value is obtained, registered, and stored only after all other gate conditions are satisfied and human activation approval (condition 3) is given | Not yet satisfied — D1.14 is written; key not yet obtained |
| 3 | Human explicitly approves Grok activation in writing (Telegram, Discord, or Mission Control record) | Not yet satisfied |
| 4 | A research experiment spec for Grok's use is filed in D5.4-EXPERIMENT-REGISTRY.md | Not yet satisfied |
| 5 | Grok tasks are declared as Research-External class in a task packet; routing follows D1.5 §5 | Not yet satisfied |

If any requirement is unmet, Grok is not activated. There is no partial activation.

**Sequencing note on condition 2:** The xAI key value must not be obtained or stored before conditions 3–5 are also met and the human has given activation approval. The governance framework (D1.14 written, xAI class defined) satisfies condition 2 as a policy prerequisite. The actual credential lifecycle — obtain → register metadata → store in approved location → set active — is executed immediately after approval, not in advance of it. This prevents a circular dependency where the key must exist before the gate approves its existence. (Source: D1.14 §14, §10.1)

### 9.4 Grok Cost and Usage

If activated, Grok API calls are governed by D1.5 §6 (cost policy) and are counted against the Research-External model class budget. Token usage is logged per call. If costs exceed a declared threshold, Grok calls are suspended and the human is notified.

---

## 10. Output Policy

A validated research finding from this pipeline produces one or more of the following outputs. Each output type maps to a defined destination in the organization.

| Output Type | Description | Destination | Who Files |
|---|---|---|---|
| Research brief | A structured summary of a validated finding or a cluster of related findings | Canonical memory `knowledge` entry via Archivist (D1.7 §5) | Forge (Researcher) → Bridge → Archivist |
| Experiment proposal | A proposal to test a capability, architecture idea, or tool discovered via X research | D5.4-EXPERIMENT-REGISTRY.md (pending human approval before filing) | Forge (Researcher) drafts; human approves before filing |
| Content opportunity note | A validated topic, format, or trend that Track 2 should evaluate | Content track queue in Mission Control | Forge → Bridge → content queue |
| Architecture suggestion | A pattern or approach that may inform how the org builds | D1.15-CHANGE_CONTROL.md review queue | Forge drafts; human reviews before it enters the queue |
| Commerce/market note | A market signal relevant to Track 3 | brain (Merchant) queue | Forge → Bridge → brain task queue |
| Discarded | No actionable signal | Logged with reason; not filed anywhere else | Forge discards; reason retained in working memory until task closes |

**One finding, one destination.** A single research packet produces the highest-priority applicable output type. It does not flood multiple queues simultaneously. If a finding is relevant to multiple tracks, the research brief is the destination — track-specific teams read the brief.

---

## 11. Human-Facing UX Policy

### 11.1 What the Human Receives

The human should receive exactly enough information to make a decision or take note of a finding. They should not receive a wall of raw post content, a list of everything that was processed, or a detailed summary of everything that was discarded.

### 11.2 Per-Finding Message Format

For High Signal findings, bridge posts a summary to Telegram (primary coordination channel) in this format:

```
Research signal: [signal type]
Source: [account handle or "anonymous"] | Trust: [tier]
Finding: [1–2 sentences — the actionable insight only]
Validation: [confirmed / unconfirmed / disputed]
Filed to: [destination]
Action needed: [yes — describe / no]
```

Nothing else. No post text. No screenshot. No model output. The human reads the finding. If they want the source, they ask.

### 11.3 Digest Format for Batched Findings

Moderate and Low Signal findings are not sent individually. They are batched into a weekly research digest. The digest format:

```
Research digest — week of YYYY-MM-DD
X research lane: [N] packets processed | [N] confirmed | [N] discarded

High signal (included above as individual notices): [N]

Moderate signal:
- [Finding 1: 1 sentence] — filed to [destination]
- [Finding 2: 1 sentence] — filed to [destination]

Noise this week: [N] posts discarded — [dominant noise category if notable]
```

### 11.4 What Bridge Never Sends to the Human from This Pipeline

- Raw X post text or quote-tweets
- Lists of posts that were checked and discarded
- Model reasoning or classification outputs
- Research packets in their raw form
- Repeated notices for the same finding in different digest cycles

---

## 12. Automation Boundaries

Automation boundaries for X research are governed by D1.8 and are summarized here for this specific pipeline.

| Mode | Automation Class | Risk Level | Approved | Gate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mode 1 (manual submission) | Class 2 (triggered) | Level 1–2 | Level 0 — human triggers per submission; system assists | No delegation spec required; bridge processes by default |
| Mode 2 (structured intake) | Class 3 (delegated single-step) | Level 2 | Level 2 (supervised) — requires delegation spec | D3.1 must exist; human reviews each output before next run |
| Mode 3 (automated sweep) | Class 1 (scheduled) + Class 4 (multi-step) | Level 2 for sweep; Level 3 for any resulting external action | Level 2 minimum; Level 3 for content or capability actions | Curated source list approved; D3.1 exists; D5.1 formalized; circuit breaker configured |
| Grok-assisted processing | Class 3 (delegated) | Level 2 (analysis only) | Conditional — activation gate §9 not satisfied | All §9 requirements must be met |

**What is never automated at any maturity level:**
- Validation of High Signal findings that would affect architecture or policy — human reviews these
- Filing an architecture suggestion to the D1.15 review queue without human sign-off
- Activating a new capability or tool based solely on an X research finding

---

## 13. Risk / Noise Controls

### 13.1 Noise Threshold

If any automated sweep (Mode 3) produces a noise rate greater than 70% in a single run, the sweep is paused and bridge posts an alert: "Research sweep pause: noise threshold exceeded ([X]% noise in last run). Review source list before resuming." The sweep does not restart automatically.

### 13.2 Source List Integrity

The curated source list for Mode 3 is owned by the human. Forge does not add accounts to the monitored list. Bridge does not add accounts to the monitored list. The list is a human-maintained document. If a new account is discovered through research and appears valuable, it is flagged as a recommendation in a digest — not added automatically.

### 13.3 Maximum Findings Per Run

Every research task packet (Modes 2 and 3) must declare a maximum number of findings it will surface. Default maximum: 10 findings per run. If more than the declared maximum would qualify, the highest-signal items are selected and the rest are discarded with a count noted in the digest. This prevents a single sweep from overwhelming the human's attention.

### 13.4 Recency Check

Before processing any post, the research packet must record the post's publication date. Posts older than 90 days are tagged `aged` and require explicit justification to be treated as current signal. Rediscovered old posts are common in algorithmic feeds and should not be treated as new developments.

### 13.5 Claim Specificity Requirement

A finding must be specific before it is treated as Moderate or High Signal. Vague directional claims ("AI is changing everything," "this model is amazing") are classified as noise regardless of source. A claim that names a specific model, tool, behavior, or capability with testable implications is eligible for classification as signal.

### 13.6 No Bandwagon Validation

A finding does not gain validity from the number of people sharing it. Viral posts are subject to the same validation requirements as obscure posts. Popularity on X is not evidence.

---

## 14. Blocked / TBD Decisions

| Item | Blocked On | Expected Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| Grok/xAI activation | D1.14 not written; human approval not given | D1.14 authored; explicit human opt-in; §9 gate fully satisfied |
| Mode 3 curated source list | Human has not yet created it | Human creates during Phase 2 using Mode 1 experience as input |
| Mode 2 and Mode 3 delegation spec | D3.1-DELEGATION-SPEC-RESEARCH.md not yet written | Phase 2 start |
| Research digest format calibration | No research packets have been processed yet | Calibrated after first 2–4 weeks of Mode 1 operation |
| Noise threshold for Mode 3 circuit breaker | Requires Mode 1 and Mode 2 experience to calibrate | Set before Mode 3 activation |
| Content opportunity routing to Track 2 pipeline | Track 2 content pipeline not yet defined | Phase 2–3 as Track 2 implementation matures |
| Architecture suggestion routing | D1.15-CHANGE_CONTROL.md not yet written | D1.15 authored; routing confirmed then |
| OpenClaw-specific signal handling | OpenClaw community channels not yet monitored | Phase 2+; defined in Mode 3 source list |
| Research-External model class costs (Grok) | Grok not activated | Defined when Grok activation is evaluated |

---

## 15. Immediate Priorities

### Phase 1 — before any research automation

1. **Practice Mode 1 manual intake for 2–4 weeks** — The human submits X findings manually via Telegram. Bridge creates research packets. Forge (or the human directly) extracts signal. No automation. No Grok. The goal is to learn what the noise-to-signal ratio actually looks like and what the human finds valuable. This is the foundation for calibrating everything that comes later. (Source: §4 Mode 1)

2. **Define and test the research packet template** — Create the intake template the human will use in Mode 1. Test it with 3–5 real submissions. Refine the `signal_summary` field until it produces consistently useful 1–3 sentence extractions. (Source: §6)

3. **Identify 5–10 high-signal X accounts manually** — Based on Mode 1 experience, the human compiles a first draft of the curated source list. This is not automation — it is the human knowing which accounts are consistently useful. This list becomes the seed for Mode 3 when the time comes. (Source: §13.2)

4. **Write D3.1-DELEGATION-SPEC-RESEARCH.md** — The delegation spec for research tasks is the prerequisite for Mode 2 and Mode 3. It cannot be written correctly without Mode 1 experience informing it. Write it after 2–4 weeks of manual intake. (Source: §4 Mode 2 gate)

5. **Do not activate Grok** — The §9 activation gate is not satisfied. Record this as a known future option, not a current priority. (Source: §9.1)

### Phase 2 — after Mode 1 experience is established

6. **Write D5.1-INTAKE-LANES.md** — Formalize the X research lane alongside other intake lanes (YouTube, arXiv, newsletters). X research does not stand alone — it is one lane in a broader intake system. (Source: §4 Mode 3 gate)

7. **Activate Mode 2 (structured intake)** — Once D3.1 exists and Mode 1 experience has informed the packet template, activate Mode 2. First runs are supervised (Level 2). (Source: §4 Mode 2, §12)

8. **Calibrate noise and signal thresholds** — Set the Mode 3 circuit breaker threshold, the maximum findings per run, and the digest cadence based on Mode 2 experience. These are not guesswork — they require real data. (Source: §13.1, §13.3)

---

_This document is the authoritative policy for X/Twitter research intake. Content strategy, social media presence, and publishing to X are not governed here — see D1.8 §16 Track 2. When Grok is evaluated for activation, revisit §9 and ensure D1.14 and D5.4 are updated at the same time._

docs.title: D1.11 — COMMUNICATION_MODES
docs.content:
# D1.11 — COMMUNICATION_MODES
**Autonomous AI Organization — Channel Hierarchy, Message Types, Operating Rhythms, and Noise Control**
_Last updated: 2026-03-11 | Status: Active | Parent: D1.1-ROADMAP.md_

---

## 1. Purpose

This document defines how communication works across the organization. It governs which channels exist, what each is for, what message types the human receives, how the swarm communicates internally, and how operating rhythms produce useful signal without creating noise.

The goal is a communication layer that feels like a well-run company: agents self-manage approved work and surface what matters, the human sees meaningful updates without having to dig, and the system stays quiet when there is nothing worth saying.

**This document governs:**
- Channel hierarchy and the role of each channel
- How Mission Control, Telegram, and Discord relate to each other
- What message types the human receives and their format constraints
- How agent-to-agent communication works internally
- Operating rhythms for daily, weekly, and sprint cadences
- How self-managed work is communicated (not just directed work)
- Escalation and alert communication standards
- Digest and summary design
- Rules for preventing clutter and noise

**What this document does not cover:**
- Channel setup steps — D2.1-BRIDGE-RUNBOOK.md
- Secrets and tokens for Telegram or Discord bots — D1.14-SECRETS_POLICY.md
- Broker routing mechanics — D1.9-BROKER_POLICY.md
- Research-specific digest format — D1.10-X_RESEARCH_POLICY.md §11
- Memory recording of events — D1.7-MEMORY_POLICY.md

---

## 2. Scope

This policy covers all channels through which the organization communicates:

| Channel | Phase | Role | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mission Control | Phase 0+ | Authoritative record — not a communication channel | Active |
| Telegram | Phase 1 | Primary coordination layer — day-to-day operating room | Required |
| Discord | Phase 1 optional | Secondary / technical layer | Optional at Phase 1 |
| LAN (inter-agent) | Phase 2 | Internal agent-to-agent message routing | Planned |
| Edge channels (Pixel/Seeker) | Phase 5 | Edge receipt and alert forwarding | Far future |

---

## 3. Communication Principles

1. **Telegram is the operating room.** Day-to-day coordination, directives, completions, alerts, and digests happen in Telegram. This is where the human manages the swarm and where the swarm surfaces what matters.

2. **Mission Control is the record.** Task state, policies, decisions, workflows, memory, and org structure live in Mission Control. Mission Control is read and written by agents and the human — it is not a chat. Nothing important lives only in a message; it is committed to Mission Control.

3. **Discord is technical and optional.** Discord may carry technical output, build logs, system alerts for developer-level visibility, or integration-specific notifications. It is not where the human manages the swarm day-to-day. At Phase 1, Discord may not be used at all.

4. **The swarm communicates completion, not activity.** Agents do not report that they are working. They report that they are done. In-progress messages exist only for declared long-running tasks at defined intervals. Silence means the swarm is working as expected.

5. **Human-facing messages are calm and company-like.** No jargon, no stack traces, no model names, no internal technical state. Messages read like briefings from a capable team: clear, relevant, action-oriented.

6. **Self-managed work surfaces the same as directed work.** When the swarm picks up and completes a backlog task without a human directive, the human receives the same completion notice format. The distinction — agent-initiated vs human-directed — is visible but not intrusive.

7. **Technical detail is always available, never pushed.** Every completion notice and alert references the Mission Control record or proof artifact. The human reads the summary in Telegram and can drill into the detail if they want it.

8. **One channel per message class.** Each message type has one default destination. Messages do not simultaneously go to Telegram and Discord unless there is an explicit reason. Duplication across channels creates noise.

9. **No channel is the source of truth.** Telegram messages, Discord posts, and email notifications are all transient. The source of truth for every decision, task, and event is Mission Control. Channels deliver information; they do not store it.

10. **Human-facing output is tool-agnostic.** Whether a task result was produced by an agent running in OpenClaw (the primary autonomous runtime), generated via Cursor (the supervised/manual engineering lane), or retrieved through Perplexity (the preferred UI-pilot and live research lane, when activated), the human-facing message format is identical: clean, company-like, no tool names, no internal details. The tool that produced the output is not surfaced in messages unless the human explicitly queries for it. Tool lanes are an execution concern, not a communication concern.

---

## 4. Channel Hierarchy

The channel hierarchy determines which channel is used by default for each communication purpose.

```
Tier 1 — Mission Control (authoritative record)
    ↓ agents write; humans read; no chat

Tier 2 — Telegram (primary coordination)
    ↓ directives, completions, alerts, escalations, digests
    ↓ human ↔ bridge, daily operating rhythm

Tier 3 — Discord (secondary / technical)
    ↓ technical output, optional developer-facing notifications
    ↓ not the operating room; not required at Phase 1

Tier 4 — LAN (internal, Phase 2+)
    ↓ agent-to-agent task packets, not human-facing

Tier 5 — Edge receipts (Phase 5)
    ↓ Pixel/Seeker → bridge → operational memory
```

When there is doubt about which channel a message belongs in, the answer is Telegram for human-facing messages and Mission Control for record-keeping. Discord is used when there is a specific reason, not by default.

---

## 5. Mission Control Communication Role

Mission Control is not a communication channel. It is the authoritative organizational record. Understanding the distinction matters:

**What Mission Control holds:**
- Task state (backlog, in-progress, blocked, completed)
- Policies (D1.x series)
- Workflows (D5.6)
- Memory entries (via Archivist, as defined in D1.7)
- Org structure, decision rights, autonomy register (D1.4)
- Experiment registry, legacy asset register, skill registry (D5.x)

**How Mission Control is accessed:**
- Agents write task state changes and proof artifact references to it
- Agents read policies, workflows, and canonical memory from it
- The human reads it directly via Mission Control interface (browsing, search)
- The human does not receive Mission Control content pushed to them as messages — they pull it when they need it

**What Mission Control is not:**
- Not a chat interface
- Not the place where the human gets briefed
- Not a log of agent conversations
- Not a notification system

Every Telegram message that summarizes something should reference the Mission Control record or proof artifact where the full detail lives.

---

## 6. Telegram Communication Role

Telegram is the primary coordination layer. It serves the same function that Slack or a company messaging tool serves: day-to-day operating visibility, fast directives, and timely results.

### 6.1 What Telegram Carries

| Message Type | Direction | Default |
|---|---|---|
| Human directives | Human → Bridge | Primary channel for instructions |
| Directive acknowledgements | Bridge → Human | Immediate, in Telegram |
| Task completion notices | Bridge → Human | After every completed task |
| Self-managed completion notices | Bridge → Human | Same format as directed; labelled as agent-initiated |
| Alerts (non-critical) | Bridge → Human | In Telegram; does not require immediate response |
| Escalations | Bridge → Human | In Telegram; requires response before automation continues |
| Daily digest | Bridge → Human | Scheduled; one message per day |
| Weekly summary | Bridge → Human | Scheduled; one message per week |
| Research signal notices (High Signal) | Bridge → Human | Per D1.10 §11.2 |
| Workflow candidate notices | Bridge → Human | Batched into weekly digest unless high-priority |
| Backlog hygiene flags | Bridge → Human | Batched into daily digest |

### 6.2 What Telegram Does Not Carry

- Internal agent-to-agent task packets
- Raw log files or stack traces
- Heartbeat confirmations
- Intermediate model reasoning
- Every task state change (only completions and escalations generate messages)
- Duplicate notices for the same event

### 6.3 Telegram Channel Structure

At Phase 1, a single Telegram chat between the human and the bridge bot is sufficient. One channel, all operating communication.

As volume grows, a second channel for escalations-only may be added. This is a Phase 2 decision, made when Phase 1 volume data justifies it. Not before.

Channel names, bot token, and chat IDs are registered in D1.14-SECRETS_POLICY.md.

---

## 7. Discord Communication Role

Discord is secondary and optional at Phase 1. It is not the primary operating room.

### 7.1 When Discord Is Used

Discord may be used for:
- Technical output that a developer-audience wants (build logs, system diagnostics, detailed error reports)
- Integration-specific notifications where Telegram is not the natural target
- Community-facing content if the organization ever develops a public presence

At Phase 1, the default is to not configure Discord at all unless there is a specific justified need. Setting up Discord for its own sake creates maintenance overhead without benefit.

### 7.2 If Discord Is Configured

If Discord is configured, the same message class rules from D1.9-BROKER_POLICY.md apply. Discord is not a second operating room — it carries a subset of message types defined for that channel. The human does not manage the swarm from Discord.

### 7.3 Discord Is Not a Fallback

If Telegram is unavailable, Discord is not automatically promoted to primary and is not a fallback. The degraded mode policy in D1.9 §15 governs what happens when the primary channel is unreachable.

Discord may serve as an explicitly approved emergency secondary path only if all of the following are true:
- It is configured for that purpose
- That configuration is documented in D1.9 and D1.14
- The human has explicitly authorized its use in that degraded role

Absent explicit authorization and documentation in both D1.9 and D1.14, Discord is not used when Telegram is unavailable. "Discord is configured" does not imply it is authorized as an emergency path.

---

## 8. Human-Facing Message Types

These are the message types the human receives through Telegram. Each has a defined format and defined constraints.

### 8.1 Completion Notice
Sent when a task closes — whether human-directed or agent-initiated.

```
✓ [Task name]
Result: [1–2 sentences — what was produced]
Proof: [MC path or artifact reference]
Initiated by: [human / agent]
```

No elaboration. No technical detail. If the human wants the detail, they follow the proof reference.

### 8.2 Alert (Non-Critical)
Sent when a system condition warrants human awareness but does not halt automation.

```
⚠ [Condition name]
What: [1 sentence description]
Impact: [none / minor / moderate — specify]
Action needed: no / [specify if yes]
```

Alerts that do not require human action are batched into the next daily digest if possible. Only alerts that warrant immediate awareness are sent in real time.

### 8.3 Escalation (Requires Human Response)
Sent when automation is halted and waiting for a human decision. Never batched. Always sent immediately.

```
🔴 Decision needed: [Trigger type]
What stopped: [Task name and ID]
Why: [1 sentence — the blocking condition]
Options: [A] [action] / [B] [action] / [C] close task
Respond to continue.
```

The escalation remains visible and is re-sent at defined intervals (D1.9 §10.3) until acknowledged.

### 8.4 Daily Digest
One message per day summarizing the operating period. Sent on a declared schedule. Suppressed if there is nothing meaningful to report.

```
Daily summary — [date]
Completed: [N tasks] — [names or "see MC for details"]
In progress: [N tasks]
Blocked: [N tasks] — [brief reason each]
Alerts: [N] — [1-line each if any]
Backlog hygiene: [N flags] — [1-line summary or "none"]
Workflow candidates: [N] — [1-line each or "none"]
```

### 8.5 Weekly Summary
One message per week with broader operating context. Sent at end of week.

```
Weekly summary — week of [date]
Completed: [N tasks] total
Sprint progress: [N] of [N] planned tasks completed
Self-managed pickups started: [N]
Self-managed completions: [N]
Escalations: [N resolved] / [N outstanding]
Experiments proposed: [N]
Memory archived: [N entries]
Next week's backlog: [N tasks eligible]
```

### 8.6 Research Signal Notice (High Signal)
Per D1.10 §11.2. Sent per-finding for High Signal items only. Others batched into the daily or weekly digest.

### 8.7 Workflow Candidate Notice
Sent when the swarm identifies a recurring pattern and proposes formalizing it into D5.6.

```
Workflow candidate: [pattern name]
Pattern: [N] similar executions in [N] days
Consistency: [high / moderate / low]
Proposed workflow ID: WF-[draft ID in D5.6]
Action: Approve / Modify / Decline
```

---

## 9. Agent-to-Agent Communication Modes

Agent-to-agent communication is internal. It does not appear in Telegram or Discord. The human sees only the outcome (a completion notice), not the internal exchange.

### 9.1 Task Packets (Bridge ↔ Agent)

The primary internal communication mode. Bridge (Conductor) issues task packets to brain or forge. Agents return result packets to bridge. The packet format is defined in D1.8 §2 and D1.9 §8.

Task packets travel through bridge — brain and forge do not issue packets to each other directly.

### 9.2 Memory Promotion Packets (Agent → Archivist)

When bridge or forge has a completed task record to commit to canonical memory, it submits a memory promotion packet to brain (Archivist). The format is defined in D1.7 §9. This is the only cross-agent communication that does not require a human directive — it is part of the self-managed operating loop.

### 9.3 LAN Routing (Phase 2+)

When Desktop and GPU3060 are online, task packets travel over LAN. The message format and authentication are defined in D1.9 §13 and D1.14. Until Phase 2, there is no LAN inter-agent communication.

### 9.4 No Side-Channel Communication

Agents do not communicate outside of defined task packets and memory promotion packets. No shared files used as message queues, no direct sockets between brain and forge, no agent reads another agent's state without a declared access path. (Source: D1.9 §8.3, D1.4 guardrail 6)

---

## 10. Operating Rhythms

Operating rhythms define when things happen without the human having to ask. These are the swarm's heartbeat at the human-visible layer. Internal execution cadence is a bridge scheduling concern; this section defines the communication cadences.

### 10.1 Daily Rhythm

| Time | Event | Channel |
|---|---|---|
| Declared digest time (TBD) | Daily digest assembled and posted | Telegram |
| Throughout day | Completion notices as tasks close | Telegram |
| Immediately when triggered | Escalations | Telegram |
| Immediately when triggered | Critical alerts | Telegram |

The digest time is set by the human during Phase 1 setup. Default suggestion: end of operating day. Exact time is registered as a cron definition in Mission Control's automation register (D1.8 §9.1).

### 10.2 Weekly Rhythm

| Day | Event | Channel |
|---|---|---|
| Declared weekly review day (TBD) | Weekly summary posted | Telegram |
| Same day | Workflow candidates surfaced (if any) | Telegram |
| Same day | Research digest posted (if X research is active) | Telegram |

### 10.3 Sprint / Planning Rhythm

Sprint cadence is TBD (see §16). When defined, it adds:
- Sprint start: bridge posts next sprint's planned tasks to Telegram
- Sprint close: weekly summary serves as sprint retrospective summary
- Mid-sprint backlog review: if bridge identifies capacity, it self-selects next eligible backlog items without waiting for a human sprint planning session

The human sets sprint goals and priorities. Bridge manages execution within them.

### 10.4 Event-Driven (No Cadence)

| Event | Channel | Timing |
|---|---|---|
| Task completes (any) | Telegram | Immediately after close |
| Escalation fires | Telegram | Immediately |
| System enters DEGRADED state | Telegram | Immediately |
| Backlog exceeds high-water mark | Telegram digest | Next digest cycle |
| New workflow candidate (high-priority) | Telegram | Next digest cycle (immediate only if human-requested urgency) |

---

## 11. Sprint / Backlog Communication Rules

The swarm is self-managed within approved boundaries. This creates specific communication needs that do not exist in purely human-directed systems.

### 11.1 Self-Managed Pickup Visibility

When bridge picks up a backlog task without a human directive, the human learns about it through:
- The task's completion notice (labelled `Initiated by: agent`)
- The daily digest (count of self-managed completions)
- Mission Control (task record shows `initiated_by: conductor`, not a human)

The human does not receive a notification at the moment of pickup — only at completion. This prevents a flood of "I just started working on X" messages.

### 11.2 Backlog Hygiene Communication

Backlog hygiene issues (stale tasks, missing fields, blocked without a recorded reason) are surfaced in the daily digest, not as real-time alerts. The exception is a task that has been blocked for more than a declared threshold (TBD) — that escalates.

Format in the daily digest:
```
Backlog hygiene: [N] flags
- [Task name]: stale / missing field / blocked [N] days — [brief]
```

### 11.3 Blocked Task Communication

When a task becomes blocked:
1. Bridge marks it `blocked` in Mission Control with a reason
2. If the block requires human input: escalation is sent immediately to Telegram
3. If the block is self-resolvable (waiting on another task): bridge holds it and the next digest includes the blocked count

Blocked tasks are not nagged — the human sees them in the digest unless they require a decision, in which case they escalate.

### 11.4 Estimation and Sequencing

When bridge sequences backlog tasks (deciding what to pick up in what order), it does not request human approval per sequence decision. It follows the priority order in Mission Control (set by the human) and the automation rules in D1.8. If there is ambiguity about priority, bridge picks the highest-priority unambiguous task and surfaces the ambiguous ones in the next digest.

---

## 12. Escalation and Alert Communication

### 12.1 Alert Levels

| Level | Name | Description | Channel | Timing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Info | Routine completion or status. No action needed. | Telegram (digest) | Batched |
| 1 | Notice | Condition worth knowing; no immediate action required | Telegram (digest or real-time if time-sensitive) | Digest or real-time |
| 2 | Warning | System condition that may require attention soon | Telegram (real-time) | Immediate |
| 3 | Escalation | Automation halted; human decision required | Telegram (real-time) | Immediate |
| 4 | Critical | System integrity or irreversibility risk | Telegram (real-time) | Immediate |

### 12.2 Alert Batching Policy

Level 0 and 1 alerts are batched into the daily digest unless:
- The human has declared specific conditions that should always be real-time
- The alert is time-sensitive (e.g. an opportunity that expires)

Level 2 and above are always real-time. They are never held for a digest.

### 12.3 Escalation Acknowledgement

Escalations require explicit human acknowledgement in Telegram before halted automation may resume. The acknowledgement must address the decision requested — not just confirm receipt. Bridge re-sends at defined intervals (D1.9 §10.3) until acknowledged.

### 12.4 Cascading Escalations

If multiple escalations fire within a short window (e.g. machine goes DEGRADED and several tasks halt simultaneously), bridge sends one combined escalation notice listing all affected tasks, not one notice per task. The human acknowledges once; bridge resumes all affected automations.

---

## 13. Digest and Summary Communication

### 13.1 Design Principles for Digests

- A digest that has nothing to report is suppressed. An empty digest is noise.
- A digest covers the period since the last digest of the same type. It does not overlap with or duplicate prior digests.
- Digests do not contain raw data — they contain summaries. Detail is available in Mission Control.
- The human can act on the digest or ignore it. A digest never requires a response unless it contains an escalation item that was missed.

### 13.2 Daily Digest Structure

See §8.4 for the format. The daily digest is the primary vehicle for batched info: completions that are routine, non-critical alerts, backlog hygiene flags, and minor workflow signals. Its purpose is to give the human a fast read on what happened without requiring them to pull up Mission Control.

### 13.3 Weekly Summary Structure

See §8.5 for the format. The weekly summary is the sprint-level view: how much got done, how much was self-managed vs directed, what's coming, and whether anything needs strategic attention.
