| BRG-STO-03 | P2 | SHOULD | OpenClaw state SHOULD be backed up to a second location (external drive or remote) on a scheduled basis. |

### 5.5 Local Inference

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| BRG-INF-01 | P1 | MUST | The Mac Mini MUST run at least one local model suitable for bridge-level reasoning before Phase 1 is **complete** (Phase 1 exit). This is a Phase 1 exit gate — not a bridge activation gate. During Phase 1 bootstrap, cloud inference (Anthropic) may serve as bridge's primary inference lane while the local model is being selected, surveyed, and installed. The bootstrap window closes when the local model passes the D1.6 stability check and bridge routing is switched to local-primary. Phase 1 cannot be declared complete while this requirement is unsatisfied. (CHG-002) |
| BRG-INF-02 | P1 | MUST | Bridge routing policy MUST define when local inference is used vs. when cloud inference is permitted. Satisfied by D1.5 §5 routing decision tree and §9 bootstrap exception. |
| BRG-INF-03 | P1 | TBD | TBD: Exact local model and serving stack for Mac Mini. Deferred to D1.5-MODEL_POLICY.md and D1.6-LOCAL_INFERENCE_PLAN.md. Unblocked after hardware survey (TASK-002). |

### 5.6 Self-Managed Execution

The swarm is self-managed within approved boundaries. Bridge (Conductor) is responsible for identifying eligible work, pulling tasks, and maintaining queue health without human prompting per task. Agents do not self-expand authority.

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| BRG-SWM-01 | P1 | MUST | Bridge (Conductor) MUST be capable of identifying and pulling eligible tasks from the Mission Control backlog without a human prompt per task pickup. |
| BRG-SWM-02 | P1 | MUST | Bridge MUST perform backlog hygiene: tasks that are stale, missing required fields, or blocked without a recorded reason MUST be flagged — not silently left. |
| BRG-SWM-03 | P2 | MUST | When a task class has an approved workflow in the Workflow Library (D5.6), bridge MUST execute using the approved workflow rather than improvising a new approach for a known pattern. |
| BRG-SWM-04 | P2 | SHOULD | Bridge SHOULD identify recurring ad-hoc task patterns and surface them as workflow candidates for human approval and promotion to D5.6. |
| SWM-AUTH-01 | P1 | MUST | Self-managed execution MUST NOT expand agent authority beyond the approved automation class, autonomy level, or escalation policy defined in D1.8. Agents are self-managed, but not self-authorizing beyond policy. |

### 5.7 Tool Lane Self-Management

Agents select the correct tool lane for each task class automatically within approved policy. Lane assignments are defined in D1.1 Principle 16 and the D1.2 §12 tool lane architecture.

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| BRG-TRL-01 | P1 | MUST | Engineering tasks (code generation, QA, build, document authoring) MUST route to Cursor as the designated supervised/manual engineering lane. Cursor requires human-in-the-loop or explicit task assignment — it is not the autonomous runtime. Bridge does not improvise ad-hoc engineering execution outside this lane. Cursor may also be operated indirectly through device-mediated control (Perplexity or OpenClaw navigating the Cursor UI) without requiring direct API integration. |
| BRG-TRL-02 | P1 | MUST | Orchestration, scheduling, inter-agent dispatch, gateway management, and approved backlog execution MUST execute within OpenClaw as the primary autonomous runtime. OpenClaw is the default lane for self-managed swarm work and Mission Control buildout. These functions are not delegated to Cursor or any external tool lane. |
| BRG-TRL-03 | P1 | SHOULD | When Perplexity (CAP-006) is activated, tasks requiring live web retrieval, current information, or browser/computer-use SHOULD route to Perplexity rather than falling back to local inference for real-time information. Perplexity is also the preferred UI-pilot lane for app, device, and chatbox interaction — including indirect operation of Cursor Claude chat through the UI. Perplexity is not activated at Phase 1 — this requirement becomes binding upon CAP-006 adoption. (Source: D1.3 INF-CON-01: local inference must not be used for tasks requiring real-time information without retrieval augmentation) |
| BRG-TRL-04 | P2 | SHOULD | Device-mediated access (computer-use, device-piloting, GUI automation as a fallback) SHOULD only be used when no stable API-based or built-in-capability path exists for the required task. Device-mediated fallback is a governed candidate capability class (CAP-007 candidate), not an ad-hoc improvisation. Before any device-mediated action is executed, it MUST have an approved task packet declaring the target surface, scope, expected output, and why stable paths are unavailable. Indirect Cursor usage through device-mediated control is a specific, documented use case of this lane. (Source: D1.1 Principle 17) |
| BRG-TRL-05 | P1 | MUST | Visual surfaces (dashboards, Mission Control screens, user-facing interfaces) MUST NOT be accepted solely because an agent marks them complete. Agent completion status for visual outputs may be "drafted," "built," or "ready for visual review." Final acceptance requires human visual approval. (Source: D1.1 §4 Tool Lanes) |

### 5.8 Access Minimization

The organization minimizes the number of external access surfaces agents must maintain. Stable, built-in access paths are preferred over device-mediated fallbacks, which are preferred over new external API dependencies.

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| BRG-AMP-01 | P1 | SHOULD | Before establishing a new external API dependency or device-mediated access path for a recurring task, bridge (Conductor) SHOULD verify that an existing approved capability (OpenClaw built-in, active CAP-SVC, or Cursor lane) cannot fulfill the same need. New access surfaces are a last resort, not a first option. (Source: D1.1 Principle 17) |

---

## 6. Brain Requirements (Desktop)

The Desktop is the memory and planning node. It is not yet powered on or integrated.

### 6.1 Availability

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| BRN-AVL-01 | P1 | MUST | The Desktop MUST be network-reachable from the Mac Mini before any Brain agent work begins. |
| BRN-AVL-02 | P1 | BLOCKED | BLOCKED: Desktop connectivity requires basement switch uplink to router. |
| BRN-AVL-03 | P2 | MUST | The Desktop MUST have Wake-on-LAN configured so the Bridge can wake it on demand. |
| BRN-AVL-04 | P2 | TBD | TBD: Sleep/power schedule for Desktop. Options: always-on, WOL-on-demand, scheduled windows. Decision deferred to D1.5 or dedicated runbook. |

### 6.2 Software Stack

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| BRN-SW-01 | P1 | TBD | TBD: OS for Desktop not confirmed. Requirements will be finalized once machine is powered on and surveyed. |
| BRN-SW-02 | P1 | MUST | OpenClaw CLI MUST be installed on Desktop before any `brain` runtime agent is defined. |
| BRN-SW-03 | P2 | MUST | The `brain` runtime agent MUST be defined and registered in OpenClaw on Desktop before any Archivist or Planner logical roles are assigned. |

### 6.3 Memory and Storage

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| BRN-MEM-01 | P2 | MUST | The Desktop MUST be the canonical owner of long-term organizational memory. No other machine may hold the authoritative copy of the memory store. |
| BRN-MEM-02 | P2 | MUST | Memory retrieval from Brain MUST be available to Bridge agents without requiring human action. |
| BRN-MEM-03 | P2 | TBD | TBD: Memory technology (vector DB, structured files, hybrid). Decision deferred to D1.7-MEMORY_POLICY.md. |

### 6.4 Legacy Assets

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| BRN-LEG-01 | P1 | MUST | Before any legacy system on Desktop is modified or replaced, a complete inventory MUST exist in D5.3-LEGACY-ASSET-REGISTER.md. |
| BRN-LEG-02 | P1 | MUST | Existing working systems on Desktop (n8n workflows, commerce automation, bots) MUST NOT be disrupted until they are either migrated or explicitly decommissioned with human approval. |

---

## 7. Forge Requirements (GPU3060 Laptop)

The Forge is the build, QA, research, and local inference node.

### 7.1 Availability

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| FRG-AVL-01 | P2 | MUST | The GPU3060 Laptop MUST be network-reachable from the Mac Mini before any Forge agent work begins. |
| FRG-AVL-02 | P2 | BLOCKED | BLOCKED: Forge connectivity requires basement switch uplink to router. |
| FRG-AVL-03 | P2 | TBD | TBD: Sleep/power schedule for Forge. GPU inference workloads have significant power draw. Decision deferred. |

### 7.2 Software Stack

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| FRG-SW-01 | P2 | TBD | TBD: OS for GPU3060 Laptop not confirmed. Requirements finalized once machine is powered on and surveyed. |
| FRG-SW-02 | P2 | MUST | OpenClaw CLI MUST be installed on Forge before any `forge` runtime agent is defined. |
| FRG-SW-03 | P2 | MUST | The `forge` runtime agent MUST be defined and registered in OpenClaw before any Builder, Critic, or Researcher logical roles are assigned. |

### 7.3 Local Inference

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| FRG-INF-01 | P2 | MUST | At least one local model MUST be runnable on the GPU3060 before any offline inference task is assigned to Forge. |
| FRG-INF-02 | P2 | TBD | TBD: Local inference stack (Ollama, llama.cpp, LM Studio, other). Decision deferred to D1.5-MODEL_POLICY.md. |
| FRG-INF-03 | P3 | SHOULD | Forge SHOULD serve inference requests to other agents over LAN so that Bridge and Brain can offload inference without cloud API calls. |
| FRG-INF-04 | P3 | SHOULD | Local inference SHOULD be available as a fallback when cloud API keys are rate-limited or unavailable. |

### 7.4 Build and QA

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| FRG-BLD-01 | P3 | MUST | Forge MUST have a working build environment (language runtime, package manager) appropriate to the first software task assigned before that task begins. |
| FRG-BLD-02 | P3 | TBD | TBD: CI/CD integration scope. Depends on what is being built. Decision deferred. |

---

## 8. Edge Requirements (Pixel / Seeker)

Edge devices are execution surfaces, not compute nodes. They are planned but not yet integrated.

### 8.1 General

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| EDG-GEN-01 | P3 | MUST | No edge device MUST be used for autonomous task execution until it has a defined role, a communication path to Bridge, and human approval of that path. |
| EDG-GEN-02 | P3 | TBD | TBD: Communication path between Pixel/Seeker and Bridge (ADB, HTTP, Termux, other). Decision deferred. |
| EDG-GEN-03 | P3 | SHOULD | Edge devices SHOULD be able to receive task results and surface them to the human without requiring Bridge to be the relay. |
| EDG-GEN-04 | P4 | TBD | TBD: Whether Seeker runs a full agent or only acts as a thin execution relay. Decision deferred. |

---

## 9. Mission Control Requirements

Mission Control is the source of truth. These requirements are in force starting Phase 0.

### 9.1 Structure

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| MC-STR-01 | P0 | MUST | Mission Control MUST exist as a structured markdown directory on the Mac Mini at the path documented in D1.2. |
| MC-STR-02 | P0 | MUST | D1.1 (ROADMAP) MUST be present, complete, and current before any Phase 0 exit criteria are checked. |
| MC-STR-03 | P0 | MUST | D1.2 (ARCHITECTURE) MUST be present, complete, and current before any Phase 0 exit criteria are checked. |
| MC-STR-04 | P0 | MUST | D1.3 (REQUIREMENTS — this document) MUST be present before Phase 0 exit. |
| MC-STR-05 | P1 | MUST | Mission Control MUST be version-controlled (git) with at least one commit representing a stable baseline before Phase 1 begins. |
| MC-STR-06 | P1 | MUST | The git repository MUST be initialized at the root of the Mission Control directory, not a subdirectory. |

### 9.2 Maintenance

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| MC-MNT-01 | P0 | MUST | No structural decision that affects the architecture (machine added/removed, network path changed, agent moved) MUST be made without updating D1.2 first. |
| MC-MNT-02 | P1 | MUST | When D1.2 changes, all downstream documents that reference the changed structure MUST be flagged for review within the same commit or pull request. |
| MC-MNT-03 | P1 | SHOULD | Mission Control SHOULD be readable by agents (plain text, no binary formats, no proprietary editors required). |
| MC-MNT-04 | P2 | SHOULD | Changes to Mission Control made by agents SHOULD be submitted as proposed edits subject to human review, not direct writes, until Autonomy Level 3 is granted for document editing. |

### 9.3 Accessibility

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| MC-ACC-01 | P0 | MUST | The MacBook Air MUST have read/write access to Mission Control on the Mac Mini via a remote workspace (Cursor remote workspace, SSH-mounted workspace, or equivalent remote editing path). |
| MC-ACC-02 | P1 | SHOULD | Mission Control SHOULD be mirrored to a remote git host (GitHub, Gitea, or equivalent) for offsite backup and collaboration. |
| MC-ACC-03 | P2 | TBD | TBD: Whether agents get direct filesystem access to Mission Control or only access via a structured API. Decision deferred. |

### 9.4 Core Screens

Mission Control is not only a file system — it provides functional surfaces for humans and agents. The following screen areas are declared product requirements. Each must exist in a usable form before the organization can operate legibly at scale.

#### A. Tasks / Kanban

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| MC-TSK-01 | P1 | MUST | Mission Control MUST provide a Kanban-style task board with at minimum the columns: recurring, backlog, in progress, blocked, completed. |
| MC-TSK-02 | P1 | MUST | Agents and approved subagents MUST be able to pick up, move, and complete tasks through this system. |
| MC-TSK-03 | P1 | MUST | Task state changes MUST be recorded in Mission Control with a timestamp and the actor responsible. |
| MC-TSK-04 | P2 | SHOULD | Task scheduling and assignment to available agent capacity SHOULD be supported as the agent roster matures. |
| MC-TSK-05 | P1 | MUST | The task board MUST distinguish between human-assigned tasks and agent-initiated task pickups so the human can audit what the swarm started vs what was directed. (Source: BRG-SWM-01, SWM-AUTH-01) |
| MC-TSK-06 | P2 | MUST | Tasks with an approved workflow in D5.6 MUST be linked to their workflow definition. Agents executing without an applicable workflow MUST note "no workflow" in the task record. (Source: BRG-SWM-03) |

#### B. Calendar

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| MC-CAL-01 | P1 | MUST | Mission Control MUST provide a calendar layout usable before heavy population. |
| MC-CAL-02 | P1 | MUST | Visual simplicity is required. A sparse calendar that is easy to read is preferred over a dense one that requires filtering. |
| MC-CAL-03 | P2 | SHOULD | The calendar SHOULD support project-linked scheduling so that entries are anchored to projects, not free-floating. |

#### C. Projects

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| MC-PRJ-01 | P1 | MUST | Mission Control MUST provide a projects view. |
| MC-PRJ-02 | P1 | MUST | The projects view MUST surface major active projects — including at minimum: Mission Control itself, Telegram integration, content automation, and commerce projects — as containers of work, not only as tags or labels. |
| MC-PRJ-03 | P2 | MUST | New projects MUST be registerable in the projects view without requiring document-level restructuring. |

#### D. Memory

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| MC-MEM-01 | P1 | MUST | Mission Control MUST provide a human-readable memory view. |
| MC-MEM-02 | P1 | MUST | The memory view MUST support browsing entries organized by day. |
| MC-MEM-03 | P2 | MUST | Long-term memory MUST be visually distinguishable from daily operational memory. |
| MC-MEM-04 | P2 | SHOULD | The memory view SHOULD support both human browsing and agent read access via the same underlying structure. |
| MC-MEM-05 | P2 | SHOULD | The memory architecture SHOULD support larger persistent storage expansion (e.g. external drive, NAS, or dedicated node volume) where available hardware permits. |

#### E. Docs

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| MC-DOC-01 | P0 | MUST | Mission Control MUST provide a docs hub for reusable operational reference documents. |
| MC-DOC-02 | P0 | MUST | The docs hub MUST include common operational reference material — including SSH notes, rules, routing configuration, and system-generated reference docs from the D-series. |
| MC-DOC-03 | P1 | MUST | The docs hub MUST be treated as the central location for all documents created by or for the system. Documents MUST NOT be scattered across arbitrary file paths outside Mission Control. |

#### F. Office

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| MC-OFF-01 | P3 | SHOULD | Mission Control SHOULD provide a visual office view showing agent and subagent activity state. |
| MC-OFF-02 | P3 | SHOULD | The intended form is a 2D pixel-art office layout. Agent presence states should include at minimum: working, idle, cooling down, and offline. |
| MC-OFF-03 | P3 | TBD | TBD: Implementation approach (web UI, static render, terminal canvas). Deferred to Phase 3. This is a declared product requirement but not a blocking requirement before P3. |

#### G. Team

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| MC-TM-01 | P1 | MUST | Mission Control MUST provide a team / org responsibility view. |
| MC-TM-02 | P1 | MUST | The team view MUST connect agents to their assigned responsibilities and business functions so the human operator can determine at a glance who owns what. |
| MC-TM-03 | P2 | MUST | The team view MUST be updated when agents are added, removed, or reassigned. It MUST NOT be allowed to become stale relative to the active agent roster. |

### 9.5 User Experience Constraints

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| MC-UX-01 | P0 | MUST | Mission Control MUST remain user-friendly. No screen surface MUST become so dense or cluttered that the human operator cannot orient within 30 seconds. |
| MC-UX-02 | P0 | MUST | Default user-facing views MUST prioritize clarity over technical density. The top layer of any view must be readable and calm. |
| MC-UX-03 | P1 | SHOULD NOT | Internal identifiers, special-character-heavy strings, and low-level implementation details SHOULD NOT dominate user-facing screens. |
| MC-UX-04 | P1 | MAY | Technical detail MAY exist in drill-down views, docs, or agent-facing surfaces — but must not surface by default in human-facing screens. |

---

## 10. Communications Requirements

Telegram is the primary human-facing communication layer. These requirements are binding from Phase 1. Discord is an optional secondary channel requiring an explicit activation decision (TASK-010, D1.14 §16 Priority 5).

### 10.1 Telegram Integration (Primary — Phase 1 Required)

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| COM-TLG-01 | P0 | MUST | The Telegram bot token (SEC-002) MUST be registered in D1.14-SECRETS_POLICY.md and stored in the Mac Mini keychain before Phase 1 is operational. |
| COM-TLG-02 | P1 | MUST | Bridge MUST be able to receive a directive from the human via Telegram and acknowledge receipt before Phase 1 communication is considered operational. |
| COM-TLG-03 | P1 | MUST | The first end-to-end communication cycle (human → Telegram → Bridge → response) MUST succeed once before Phase 1 is declared complete. |

### 10.2 Discord Integration (Optional — Activation Decision Required)

Discord activation requires an explicit human decision (TASK-010). Before any Discord credentials are obtained, the human must decide: activate Discord in Phase 1, or defer. Obtaining a Discord bot token before this decision is a policy violation (D1.14 §16 Priority 5).

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| COM-DSC-01 | P1 | MAY | If Discord is activated (TASK-010 outcome: activate), at least one Discord channel MAY be connected to the Bridge agent as a secondary supplement to Telegram. |
| COM-DSC-02 | P1 | MUST | If a Discord bot token is obtained, it MUST NOT be stored in plain text in Mission Control. It must be stored in an environment variable or secrets file excluded from version control. |
| COM-DSC-03 | P1 | MAY | If Discord is activated, Bridge MAY receive directives from Discord as a secondary channel. Telegram acknowledgement and routing remain primary. |
| COM-DSC-04 | P2 | SHOULD | If Discord is activated, agent status updates SHOULD be surfaced to a designated Discord channel without requiring the human to poll. |
| COM-DSC-05 | P2 | SHOULD | If Discord is activated, distinct agent roles SHOULD post to distinct Discord channels or threads so human can track activity by role without filtering. |

### 10.3 Inter-Agent Communication

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| COM-IAC-01 | P2 | MUST | Bridge MUST be able to dispatch a task to Brain and receive a result before any multi-agent workflow is declared operational. |
| COM-IAC-02 | P2 | MUST | Inter-agent messages MUST be logged with enough detail to reconstruct the exchange without agent cooperation. |
| COM-IAC-03 | P2 | TBD | TBD: Protocol for inter-agent communication (OpenClaw native routing, direct HTTP, message queue). Decision deferred to architecture review at P1→P2 boundary. |
| COM-IAC-04 | P3 | SHOULD | Agents SHOULD degrade gracefully when a peer agent is unreachable — queuing the request rather than failing the task. |

---

## 11. Memory Requirements

Memory is the persistence layer for all organizational state beyond the current conversation.

### 11.1 Operational Memory (Mac Mini)

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| MEM-OPS-01 | P1 | MUST | OpenClaw state (`~/.openclaw/`) on Mac Mini MUST persist across reboots. It MUST NOT be on a tmpfs or volatile path. |
| MEM-OPS-02 | P1 | MUST | Active task state MUST be recoverable after an unplanned Mac Mini reboot without loss of task identity or prior steps. |
| MEM-OPS-03 | P2 | SHOULD | Operational memory SHOULD be structured so Bridge can hand off context to Brain without reformatting or manual copy. |

### 11.2 Canonical Memory (Desktop)

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| MEM-CAN-01 | P2 | MUST | Long-term organizational memory MUST live on Desktop and MUST NOT be duplicated as the authoritative copy on any other machine. |
| MEM-CAN-02 | P2 | MUST | Canonical memory MUST be accessible to Bridge without requiring the human to manually transfer data. |
| MEM-CAN-03 | P3 | MUST | When a task completes, a summary of inputs, decisions, and outputs MUST be committed to canonical memory. |
| MEM-CAN-04 | P3 | TBD | TBD: Retention policy. How long do task records live before archival or deletion? Decision deferred. |

### 11.3 Human-Readable Memory Surface

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| MEM-HUM-01 | P1 | MUST | Human-readable memory browsing by day MUST be a required surface of Mission Control — not only an internal storage concern. The human operator MUST be able to read what was remembered without querying a database or reading raw files. |
| MEM-HUM-02 | P2 | MUST | Long-term memory entries MUST be accessible in a distinct view from daily operational memory so the human can distinguish persistent organizational knowledge from transient task context. |

### 11.4 Working Memory (GPU3060)

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| MEM-WRK-01 | P2 | MUST | Working memory on Forge (in-progress build artifacts, QA states, research scratch) MUST be cleaned up after task completion to prevent unbounded growth. |
| MEM-WRK-02 | P3 | SHOULD | Working memory SHOULD be readable by Brain so that research findings on Forge can be archived to canonical memory without going through Bridge. |

---

## 12. Local Inference Requirements

### 12.1 Capability

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| INF-CAP-01 | P2 | MUST | At least one local model MUST be runnable on Forge (GPU3060) before any offline inference task is assigned. |
| INF-CAP-02 | P2 | TBD | TBD: Minimum model size / capability threshold for local inference. Depends on use cases defined at P1→P2. |
| INF-CAP-03 | P3 | SHOULD | Local inference SHOULD be available as fallback when cloud API is unavailable or rate-limited. |
| INF-CAP-04 | P3 | SHOULD | Bridge SHOULD be able to route an inference request to either cloud or local based on a policy, without the requesting agent needing to know which backend was used. |

### 12.2 Constraints

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| INF-CON-01 | P2 | MUST NOT | Local inference MUST NOT be used for tasks that require real-time information (web search, current market data) without an explicit retrieval augmentation step. |
| INF-CON-02 | P3 | SHOULD NOT | High-power GPU inference tasks SHOULD NOT run simultaneously with other GPU-intensive workloads on the same machine without explicit scheduling. |

---

## 13. Legacy Asset Requirements

Legacy systems may contain real, recoverable value. These requirements protect against accidental destruction.

### 13.1 Inventory

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| LEG-INV-01 | P1 | MUST | A complete inventory of legacy assets on Desktop MUST exist in D5.3-LEGACY-ASSET-REGISTER.md before any existing system is modified, migrated, or decommissioned. |
| LEG-INV-02 | P1 | MUST | The inventory MUST include: system name, current status (running/stopped/unknown), estimated value/risk rating, and migration disposition (keep/migrate/decommission/evaluate). |
| LEG-INV-03 | P1 | MUST NOT | Legacy systems MUST NOT be powered off, wiped, or reconfigured until they appear in the inventory with a disposition decision. |

### 13.2 Migration

| ID | Phase | Level | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| LEG-MIG-01 | P2 | MUST | Each legacy asset marked "migrate" MUST have a migration plan written before migration begins. |
| LEG-MIG-02 | P2 | MUST | Legacy migrations MUST be staged: run old and new in parallel until new is verified, then decommission old. No big-bang replacements. |
| LEG-MIG-03 | P2 | SHOULD | Commerce automation and revenue-generating systems SHOULD be migrated last, after lower-risk systems are proven in the new architecture. |
| LEG-MIG-04 | P1 | MUST | Previously built operational assets that already satisfy a Mission Control screen requirement (tasks, calendar, projects, memory, docs) MUST be evaluated for migration before any reimplementation begins. Building from scratch when a working prior system exists is waste and requires explicit justification. |

---

## 14. Phase Acceptance Requirements

These are the summarized acceptance gates per phase, derived from sections 4–13.

### Phase 0 Exit Gate

The following must ALL be true before Phase 0 is declared complete:

| # | Requirement | Source |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mac Mini does not sleep (system sleep disabled) | BRG-AVL-01 |
| 2 | SSH to Mac Mini works from MacBook Air | BRG-AVL-02, BRG-RMT-02 |
| 3 | Screen Sharing works from MacBook Air | BRG-RMT-01 |
| 4 | OpenClaw gateway starts automatically after reboot | BRG-AVL-03, BRG-OCL-02 |
| 5 | Mac Mini auto-restarts after power loss | BRG-AVL-04 |
| 6 | D1.1, D1.2, D1.3 are present and current | MC-STR-02, MC-STR-03, MC-STR-04 |
| 7 | No agent has been granted autonomy above Level 0 | SYS-AUT-01 |
| 8 | MacBook Air has remote access to Mission Control on Mac Mini | MC-ACC-01 |

### Phase 1 Exit Gate

The following must ALL be true before Phase 1 is declared complete:

| # | Requirement | Source |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mac Mini has a stable IP (DHCP reservation or static) | BRG-AVL-05 |
| 2 | Mission Control is version-controlled with a committed baseline | MC-STR-05, MC-STR-06 |
| 3 | OpenClaw `bridge` agent is defined and registered | BRG-OCL-04 |
| 4 | Model API key is configured on Bridge | BRG-OCL-05 |
| 5 | Telegram bot (SEC-002) is active and Bridge can receive a directive via Telegram | COM-TLG-01, COM-TLG-02 |
| 6 | First end-to-end directive (human → Telegram → Bridge → response) has succeeded once | COM-TLG-03 |
| 7 | Legacy asset inventory (D5.3) exists | LEG-INV-01 |

### Phase 2 Exit Gate

The following must ALL be true before Phase 2 is declared complete:

| # | Requirement | Source |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `brain` agent defined on Desktop | BRN-SW-03 |
| 2 | `forge` agent defined on GPU3060 | FRG-SW-03 |
| 3 | Bridge can dispatch a task to Brain and receive a result | COM-IAC-01 |
| 4 | At least one local model running on Forge | FRG-INF-01, INF-CAP-01 |
| 5 | Canonical memory store is operational on Desktop | MEM-CAN-01, MEM-CAN-02 |
| 6 | At least one full multi-agent task has completed and been committed to canonical memory | MEM-CAN-03 |

### Phase 3 and Phase 4 Gates

Phase 3 and Phase 4 acceptance criteria are TBD. They depend on the capability and task scope decisions made during Phase 2. Requirements will be defined and added to this document at the P2→P3 boundary review.

---

## 15. Blocked / TBD Requirements

Summary of all open items that prevent certain requirements from being written definitively.

| ID | Blocked By | What Unblocks It |
|---|---|---|
| BRG-AVL-06 | Basement switch not uplinked to router | Physical cable from switch to router |
| BRG-INF-03 | Mac Mini model and serving stack not chosen | D1.5-MODEL_POLICY.md and D1.6-LOCAL_INFERENCE_PLAN.md authored |
| BRG-OCL-07 | LAN gateway exposure needs auth decision | D1.9-BROKER_POLICY.md and D1.14-SECRETS_POLICY.md authored |
| BRN-AVL-02 | Basement switch not uplinked to router | Physical cable from switch to router |
| BRN-AVL-04 | Desktop sleep/power strategy not decided | Decision at P1 planning checkpoint |
| BRN-SW-01 | Desktop OS not confirmed | Power on Desktop, survey software |
| BRN-MEM-03 | Memory technology not chosen | D1.7-MEMORY_POLICY.md authored |
| FRG-AVL-02 | Basement switch not uplinked to router | Physical cable from switch to router |
| FRG-AVL-03 | Forge power/sleep strategy not decided | Decision at P1 planning checkpoint |
| FRG-SW-01 | GPU3060 OS not confirmed | Power on GPU3060, survey software |
| FRG-INF-02 | Inference stack not chosen | D1.5-MODEL_POLICY.md authored |
| EDG-GEN-02 | Edge comm path not decided | Architecture review at P2 boundary |
| EDG-GEN-04 | Seeker role scope not decided | Architecture review at P2 boundary |
| COM-IAC-03 | Inter-agent protocol not chosen | Architecture review at P1→P2 boundary |
| MEM-CAN-04 | Retention policy not decided | Decision at P2 planning checkpoint |
| INF-CAP-02 | Use cases not defined yet | Defined at P1→P2 boundary |
| MC-ACC-03 | Agent filesystem access policy not decided | D1.5 or dedicated policy doc |
| FRG-BLD-02 | What is being built not yet defined | Emerges from Phase 2 task assignments |

---

## 16. Non-Requirements

The following are explicitly out of scope for this requirements register. They are documented here to prevent scope creep.

| Item | Why Out of Scope |
|---|---|
| How to install OpenClaw | Runbook concern (D2.x), not a requirement |
| Which Discord bot library to use | Implementation detail, not a requirement |
| Agent personality or tone | Policy concern (D1.5+), not a requirement |
| UI for monitoring agents | No UI required — legibility via Mission Control docs and Discord is sufficient |
| Multi-user access to Mission Control | Single operator. Multi-user is not a current requirement. |
| High availability (active-active failover) | Out of phase. Durability (restart on failure) is sufficient for P0–P2. |
| Kubernetes or container orchestration | Not required at this scale. Individual process management is sufficient. |
| External customers or SLAs | The organization serves internal use cases only through P3. |
| Agent self-modification of code | Prohibited until explicit Autonomy Level grant. Not a Phase 0–2 requirement. |
| Automatic scaling | No cloud infra. Fixed hardware. Scaling is not a current concern. |

---

## 17. Immediate Requirement Priorities

These are the requirements that are currently blocking or closest to blocking the next action.

### Right Now (Phase 0 completion)

1. **BRG-AVL-03 / BRG-OCL-02** — Verify OpenClaw gateway actually auto-starts after a cold reboot. This has been configured but not tested with an actual reboot cycle.
2. **BRG-AVL-04** — Verify Mac Mini auto-restarts after power loss. `systemsetup -setrestartpowerfailure on` was run but a test power cycle has not been performed.
3. **MC-STR-04** — This document (D1.3) is the blocking requirement. Creating it completes this item.
4. **MC-ACC-01** — Confirm MacBook Air remote workspace (Cursor remote workspace, SSH-mounted workspace, or equivalent remote editing path) to Mac Mini is working and Mission Control directory is accessible.

### Next (Phase 1 readiness)

5. **MC-STR-05 / MC-STR-06** — Initialize git in Mission Control directory. One command, no blockers.
6. **BRG-OCL-04 / BRG-OCL-05** — Define `bridge` agent in OpenClaw and configure model API key.
7. **COM-TLG-02** — Activate Telegram bot (SEC-002) and verify Bridge can receive a directive via Telegram.
8. **LEG-INV-01** — Create D5.3-LEGACY-ASSET-REGISTER.md by powering on Desktop and surveying what is running.

### Physically Blocked (requires action outside software)

9. **BRG-AVL-05 / BRG-AVL-06 / BRN-AVL-02 / FRG-AVL-02** — All four are blocked by the same physical action: uplink the basement switch to the router with one cable. This single cable unblocks stable IP assignment for Mac Mini, and network access for Desktop and GPU3060.

---

_End of D1.3 — REQUIREMENTS_
_Parent document: D1.1-ROADMAP.md | Architecture reference: D1.2-ARCHITECTURE.md_
_Next in series: D1.4-ORG_MAP.md_

docs.title: D1.4 — ORG_MAP
docs.content:
# D1.4 — ORG_MAP
**Autonomous AI Organization — Ownership, Roles, and Decision Rights**
_Last updated: 2026-03-16 | Status: Active | Parent: D1.1-ROADMAP.md | Architecture: D1.2-ARCHITECTURE.md | Requirements: D1.3-REQUIREMENTS.md_

> ⚠️ **UPDATED 2026-03-16 (CREW-004):** All bridge/brain/forge references updated to Nami/Franky/Chopper.
> Machine inventory and decision rights updated to reflect confirmed crew state. Discord now active.

---

## 1. Purpose

This document defines who owns what in the autonomous AI organization. It maps runtime agents to machines, logical roles to agents, decision rights to people and agents, and responsibility for every declared surface and strategic track.

**What this document answers:**
- Which runtime agent is responsible for each class of work?
- Which logical role inside that agent handles a given task?
- What does the human operator own exclusively?
- Who decides when something escalates?
- Who owns each Mission Control screen?
- Who owns each strategic outcome track?

**What this document is not:**
- Not a policy document — it describes ownership, not the rules agents follow (see D1.5–D1.15)
- Not a runbook — it does not describe how to do work (see D2.x)
- Not a requirements spec — it does not define acceptance criteria (see D1.3)
- Not an architecture description — it does not describe the hardware or network (see D1.2)

When the organizational structure changes — a new agent is added, a role is reassigned, a strategic track changes ownership — this document is updated first. Documents that reference specific ownership must be reviewed when this file changes.

---

## 2. Organizational Structure

The organization has four layers. Each layer has a defined role and a defined relationship to the layers above and below it.

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  CIHAN (Human Operator)                                      │
│  Architect · Director · Judge                                │
│  MacBook Air (Control Hub — not a worker node)               │
└──────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │ intent / directives (Telegram StrawHats HQ)
                       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  MISSION CONTROL                                             │
│  Source of truth — structured markdown on Nami (Mac Mini)    │
│  All tasks, state, docs, and decisions are recorded here     │
└──────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │ task assignments (OpenClaw gateway RPC)
                       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  NAMI  (Mac Mini · M4 · 10.0.0.152)                         │
│  Chief of Staff · Navigator · Orchestrator                   │
│  Always-on gateway · Dispatcher · Scheduler · Comms broker   │
│  Roles: Conductor · Broker · Navigator                       │
└───────────┬──────────────────────────────┬───────────────────┘
            │ ws://10.0.0.251:18789         │ ws://10.0.0.16:18789
            ▼                              ▼
┌───────────────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│  FRANKY (Desktop)     │    │  CHOPPER (GPU3060 · 10.0.0.16)  │
│  10.0.0.251           │    │  Chief QA / Security Manager    │
│  Chief Engineer       │    │  Verification · Health checks   │
│  Build · Deploy       │    │  Security audits · Watchdog     │
│  Roles: Builder       │    │  Roles: Critic · Watchdog       │
│         Engineer      │    │         Researcher              │
└───────────────────────┘    └─────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Key rule:** The organization continues operating autonomously when the MacBook Air is offline. The MacBook Air is the human's interface — it is not infrastructure.

---

## 3. Runtime Agent Ownership

There are exactly three runtime agents. No additional runtime agents are created without an explicit architectural decision documented in D1.2 and approved by the human operator.

### Nami — Mac Mini (bridge)

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Machine | Mac Mini (Apple M4, 16 GB RAM) |
| IP | 10.0.0.152 (Ethernet, DHCP reserved) |
| Runtime | OpenClaw 2026.3.13 — launchd managed, never sleeps |
| Always-on | Yes |
| Primary function | Orchestration, dispatch, scheduling, communications brokering |
| Logical roles hosted | Conductor, Broker, Navigator |
| Owns | Task queue, message routing, inter-crew dispatch, Telegram + Discord I/O, Mission Control sync, sprint management |
| Does NOT own | Build output (Franky), QA verdicts (Chopper) |

### Franky — Desktop (brain)

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Machine | Desktop (Windows, hostname: Cihan) |
| IP | 10.0.0.251 (Ethernet, DHCP reserved) |
| Runtime | OpenClaw 2026.3.13 — Active |
| Always-on | Yes |
| Primary function | Engineering, builds, deployments, code execution |
| Logical roles hosted | Builder, Engineer |
| Agent workspace | `D:\openclaw-brain\` |
| Owns | Code output, build artifacts, deployments, engineering docs |
| Does NOT own | Task routing (Nami), QA verdicts (Chopper), gateway (Nami) |

### Chopper — GPU3060 (forge)

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Machine | GPU3060 Laptop (Windows, hostname: meme) |
| IP | 10.0.0.16 (Ethernet, DHCP reserved) |
| Runtime | OpenClaw 2026.3.13 — Active |
| Model | openai-codex/gpt-5.4 |
| Always-on | Yes |
| Primary function | QA, verification, security checks, health monitoring |
| Logical roles hosted | Critic, Watchdog, Researcher |
| Agent workspace | `C:\Users\memeb\.openclaw\workspace\` |
| Owns | Test results, verification reports, security audits, health checks |
| Does NOT own | Task dispatch (Nami), build output (Franky), gateway (Nami) |

---

## 4. Logical Role Map

Logical roles are labels that describe what a runtime agent is doing in a given context. They are not separate OpenClaw agents. They are not separate processes. They do not have separate credentials. A single agent prompt context shifts between roles depending on the task it has been assigned.

| Role | Hosted In | Crew Agent | Responsibility | Does Not Do |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Conductor** | bridge | Nami | Coordinates task flow. Assigns tasks to Franky or Chopper. Manages cron, sprint, scheduled work. Owns tool lane routing governance. | Does not execute build or QA tasks itself. |
| **Broker** | bridge | Nami | Routes messages. Manages channel I/O (Telegram + Discord). Handles directives. Routes results and digests. | Does not make decisions about task content. Routes only. |
| **Navigator** | bridge | Nami | Sprint management, planning, backlog grooming, roadmap tracking. | Does not execute tasks directly. Plans and tracks only. |
| **Builder** | brain | Franky | Generates code, scaffolds projects, builds deployments. Returns artifacts to Nami or directly to Mission Control. | Does not deploy to production without Chopper QA at current autonomy levels. |
| **Engineer** | brain | Franky | Architecture, system design, infrastructure builds. | Does not override QA verdicts. |
| **Critic** | forge | Chopper | Evaluates build output. Runs tests. Produces pass/fail verdicts. Files QA reports to Discord #qa. | Does not modify code directly. Reviews only. |
| **Watchdog** | forge | Chopper | Monitors gateway health, SSH status, machine health. Flags anomalies. Posts to Discord #ops. | Does not make infrastructure changes. Monitors and flags only. |
| **Researcher** | forge | Chopper | Reads sources, extracts signal, produces research briefs. Also available from Franky for technical research. | Does not make adoption decisions. Surfaces findings only. |

### Role Activation Rule

A runtime agent may operate in multiple logical roles within a single session, but:
- It MUST NOT switch roles without a task boundary or explicit directive
- Each role activation MUST be traceable to a task in Mission Control
- Role scope is bounded by the task packet that triggered it

---

## 5. Human Operator Role

The human operator is the architect, director, and judge of the organization. The human is not a day-to-day operator.

### What the Human Owns Exclusively

| Domain | Human Action Required |
|---|---|
| Organizational structure | Adding, removing, or reassigning runtime agents or logical roles |
| Autonomy level grants | Increasing autonomy level for any task type at any agent |
| Financial authorization | Any transaction above approved routine commerce parameters |
| Infrastructure changes | New machines, new networks, OS changes, OpenClaw upgrades |
| New capability adoption | Approving new models, plugins, or tools from D5.2 SKILL-REGISTRY.md |
| Strategic track direction | Setting or changing objectives for any of the 5 strategic outcome tracks |
| Policy changes | Authoring or modifying any D1.x policy document |
| Phase advancement | Declaring a phase complete and authorizing the next phase to begin |
| Agent permission grants | Write access to Mission Control, deployment rights, external API access |

### What the Human Does NOT Do in Steady State

- Manually route tasks between agents
- Manually move task cards on the Kanban board (agents do this)
- Trigger research sweeps (forge does this on schedule)
- Write memory entries (brain does this)
- Issue detailed sub-task instructions for known task types (agents handle these within delegation specs)

### Human Interface Points

| Interface | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Telegram StrawHats HQ | Primary directive channel, escalation acknowledgement, result review, digest consumption. 7 forum topics: General (4), Planning (6), Briefing (8), Research (10), Finances (12), Life (14), Commerce (16). |
| Discord The Straw Hats | Crew workshop — technical work, build logs, QA results, task assignments. 7 channels: #ops, #dev, #qa, #tasks, #memory, #research-tech, #devops. **Active** as of 2026-03-16. |
| MacBook Air + Cursor | Mission Control authoring, document review, configuration |
| Mission Control (Nami) | Strategic direction, phase management, org oversight. Dashboard at Nami :18800. |

---

## 6. Decision Rights

Decision rights define who can make what category of decision without escalation.

### Decision Authority Table

| Decision Category | Human | Nami (bridge) | Franky (brain) | Chopper (forge) | Escalation Required |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Issue a directive | ✓ | — | — | — | Never |
| Accept and execute a task | — | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | No, within task scope |
| Route a task to another crew | — | ✓ | — | — | No |
| File a memory entry | — | ✓ | ✓ | — | No |
| File a research brief | — | — | ✓ | ✓ | No |
| Move a task card on Kanban | — | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | No |
| Mark a task complete | — | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | No, within task scope |
| Issue QA verdict | — | — | — | ✓ | No |
| Execute a financial transaction | ✓ | — | — | — | Always |
| Increase autonomy level | ✓ | — | — | — | Always |
| Approve new capability | ✓ | — | — | — | Always |
| Modify Mission Control docs | ✓ | Proposed edits only | Proposed edits only | Proposed edits only | Human review required |
| Declare a phase complete | ✓ | — | — | — | Always |
| Add a runtime agent | ✓ | — | — | — | Always |
| Assign a logical role to a task | — | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | No, within agent scope |

### Escalation Triggers

An agent MUST escalate to the human when any of the following occur:
1. A task requires an action outside its currently granted autonomy level
2. A task requires spending money or accessing financial systems beyond approved parameters
3. A task requires modifying infrastructure (machines, network, OS, OpenClaw config)
4. A task produces an ambiguous result that requires a strategic decision to proceed
5. A task has been blocked for more than a defined timeout (TBD in D1.9-BROKER_POLICY.md)
6. Two agents disagree on a decision that affects shared state (Mission Control, memory store)
7. Any unexpected external event occurs that was not anticipated in the task spec

---

## 7. Ownership by Strategic Outcome Track

The five strategic outcome tracks from D1.1 are mapped to owners here. Primary owner does the work. Supporting owner assists. Human approves direction.

### Track 1 — Personal Operating System / Jarvis

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Description | Manages the human's schedule, research intake, task queue, and ambient awareness |
| Primary owner | Nami (Conductor, Broker) |
| Supporting | Franky (archiving and context), Chopper (health monitoring) |
| Human role | Sets direction, reviews digests, makes decisions surfaced by the track |
| Current status | Partially active — daily digest cron running to Telegram Briefing topic |

### Track 2 — Content and Traffic Engine

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Description | Produces written, visual, or structured content from research and directives; publishes and monitors |
| Primary owner | Franky (Builder, Researcher) |
| Supporting | Nami (orchestration and routing) |
| Human role | Sets content direction, approves publishing policy, reviews output quality |
| Current status | Not started. Begins Phase B. |

### Track 3 — Commerce and Sales Engine

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Description | Manages product listings, pricing, fulfillment triggers, and sales channel activity |
| Primary owner | Franky (Builder — commerce logic and automation) |
| Supporting | Nami (Conductor — schedules and monitors commerce tasks), Chopper (QA) |
| Human role | Approves commerce parameters, reviews anomaly flags, authorizes new sales channels |
| Current status | Not started. Prior commerce automation on Franky must be inventoried first (D5.3). |

### Track 4 — Financial Operating System

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Description | Tracks revenue, costs, and margins; produces financial digests; flags threshold breaches |
| Primary owner | Nami (routes to Telegram Finances topic, thread 12) |
| Supporting | Franky (builds financial tooling) |
| Human role | Reviews all financial reports, makes all financial decisions, sets thresholds |
| Current status | Not started. Nami routes cost alerts to Telegram Finances topic. |
| Note | This track provides visibility and recommendations only until explicit autonomy levels are granted. |

### Track 5 — Experimentation and Intelligence Engine

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Description | Continuously runs small experiments; files results; surfaces what works |
| Primary owner | Franky (Builder, Researcher) + Chopper (Critic — verification) |
| Supporting | Nami (knowledge base, experiment log), human (adoption decisions) |
| Human role | Approves all capability adoptions, sets experiment direction, reviews outcomes |
| Current status | Active at low level — CREW sprint tasks constitute early experiment cycles. |

---

## 8. Ownership by Mission Control Surface

Each of the seven declared Mission Control screens has a primary owner (who maintains it) and a primary consumer (who reads it most).

| Screen | Primary Owner | Primary Consumer | Who Can Write | Phase |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tasks / Kanban** | bridge (Conductor) | Human + all agents | All agents (state changes); human (directives) | P1 |
| **Calendar** | bridge (Conductor) | Human | bridge (schedule entries); human (strategic dates) | P1 |
| **Projects** | Human | Human + bridge | Human (creates/closes projects); bridge (status updates) | P1 |
| **Memory** | brain (Archivist) | Human + bridge | brain only (write); all agents (read) | P1 (view), P2 (full) |
| **Docs** | Human (D-series) | Human + all agents | Human (policy/arch docs); bridge (operational docs filed under Broker) | P0 |
| **Office** | TBD | Human | TBD | P3 |
| **Team** | Human | Human + all agents | Human (defines roles); updated at each org change | P1 |

### Notes on Contested Surfaces

- **Docs hub**: The D-series (D1.x–D5.x) is always human-authored or human-reviewed. Agents may propose edits but cannot write directly until Autonomy Level 3 is granted for document editing.
- **Tasks / Kanban**: Agents move task cards within the task flow. They do NOT create new tasks outside an approved task class without human authorization.
- **Memory**: Brain is the only agent with write access to the canonical memory store. Other agents submit entries to brain for filing — they do not write to memory directly.

---

## 9. Delegation and Escalation Model

### Delegation Flow

A task moves through the organization in the following sequence:

```
1. Human issues directive (Telegram or Mission Control)
         ↓
2. bridge (Broker) receives and acknowledges the directive
         ↓
3. bridge (Conductor) creates a task packet in Mission Control:
   - owner assigned
   - scope defined
   - route defined
   - output format defined
   - handoff target defined
         ↓
4. Task dispatched to the owning agent (brain or forge)
         ↓
5. Owning agent executes, produces proof artifact
         ↓
6. Result filed to Mission Control and/or canonical memory
         ↓
7. bridge (Broker) routes result to Telegram (ops-channel) for human review
         ↓
8. Human reviews result, approves, or issues follow-on directive
```

### Task Packet Requirements

A task is not delegated work until it has all of the following:
- **Owner** — which runtime agent is responsible
- **Scope** — what the task does and does not include
- **Route** — where the result goes when complete
- **Output format** — what proof artifact is expected
- **Handoff target** — who receives the result

A directive that skips this structure is chat, not delegation. Bridge (Conductor) is responsible for converting directives into proper task packets.

### Self-Managed Execution Flow

Alongside the human-directive flow above, the swarm operates a self-managed loop for approved task classes. This is not a separate system — it is the same task packet and Mission Control structure, initiated by Conductor rather than by a human directive:

```
1. bridge (Conductor) scans Mission Control backlog for eligible tasks
   (eligible = task class approved, automation level ≥ current maturity,
    no active block, machine within resource budget)
         ↓
2. bridge (Conductor) pulls the task, creates or validates its task packet
         ↓
3. Task dispatched to owning agent (or executed internally by Conductor)
         ↓
4. Owning agent executes, produces proof artifact
         ↓
5. Result filed to Mission Control and/or canonical memory
         ↓
6. bridge (Broker) routes completion notice to Telegram
         ↓
7. Human reviews digest or individual completion notices — no mid-task intervention needed
```

**Backlog hygiene** is Conductor's responsibility. Tasks in backlog that are stale, missing required fields, or blocked without a recorded reason are flagged to the human in the next digest — they are not silently accumulated.

**Workflow reuse.** When a task class has an approved workflow in D5.6-WORKFLOW_LIBRARY.md, Conductor executes using that workflow. When no workflow exists and the pattern repeats, Conductor surfaces it as a workflow candidate for human approval.

**The boundary.** Self-managed execution operates within approved scope, automation class, and autonomy level. Conductor does not pull tasks above its current autonomy level, does not expand task scope mid-execution, and does not initiate new task classes without human approval. Self-managed, but not self-authorizing beyond policy.

### Escalation Flow

When an agent cannot proceed without human input:

```
1. Agent marks task as BLOCKED in Mission Control with reason
2. bridge (Broker) posts escalation to Telegram (ops-channel) with:
   - task ID
   - blocking reason
   - what decision or action is needed
   - options (if applicable)
3. Human reviews and issues a decision via Telegram or Mission Control
4. bridge (Conductor) resumes the task with the human decision recorded
```

---

## 10. Autonomy Ownership Model

Autonomy is tracked per task type, not per agent. A single agent may hold different autonomy levels for different task types.

### Autonomy Level Reference

| Level | Name | Human Involvement |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Manual | Human does the work or explicitly calls the agent each time |
| 1 | Assisted | Agent drafts, human executes |
| 2 | Supervised | Agent executes, human reviews every result |
| 3 | Delegated | Agent executes, human spot-checks on schedule |
| 4 | Trusted | Agent executes, human reviews only flagged items |
| 5 | Autonomous | Agent executes and self-corrects, human reviews digests |

### Current Autonomy State

All task types are currently at Level 0. No autonomy level has been granted to any agent for any task type.

### Autonomy Authority Table

| Action | Authority |
|---|---|
| Propose an autonomy level increase | Any agent or human |
| Approve an autonomy level increase | Human only |
| Grant autonomy for a new task type | Human only, after minimum 3 observed cycles at current level |
| Revoke an autonomy level | Human only, immediate, no agent approval required |
| Track autonomy level per task type | bridge (Conductor) maintains the register in Mission Control |

### Autonomy Register Location

The current autonomy level per task type is maintained in Mission Control. The register is updated by bridge (Conductor) when the human approves a change. The register is read-only for brain and forge.

---

## 11. Capability Ownership

New capabilities — models, plugins, skills, tools, automations — enter the organization through defined lanes. Ownership of each stage is explicit.

| Stage | Owner | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Discovery | forge (Researcher) or human | Identifies a new capability from research, X posts, or experimentation |
| Evaluation | forge (Critic) + human | forge benchmarks or tests the capability; human reviews the evaluation |
| Adoption decision | Human only | Human decides whether to adopt, defer, or reject |
| Registration | bridge (Conductor) | Files the adopted capability in D5.2 SKILL-REGISTRY.md |
| Installation | bridge or human (depending on risk) | Installs and configures the capability on the relevant node |
| Active use | Assigned runtime agent | Uses the capability within task scope |

**Rule:** No capability is used in production before it appears in D5.2 SKILL-REGISTRY.md with an adoption decision recorded.

### Perplexity — Candidate Callable Capability

Perplexity is a candidate external callable capability (D5.7 CAP-006) for live web research, current-information retrieval, and browser/computer-use tasks. It is:

- **A callable service** — agents invoke it for specific task classes; it is not a runtime agent, not a messaging endpoint, and not an orchestration layer
- **Not a subagent** — Perplexity does not hold task state, manage memory, or route messages; it returns outputs that agents process and route normally through bridge (Broker)
- **Not a model routing target** — Perplexity is outside the D1.5 inference routing policy; it is a CAP-SVC capability governed by D1.12 and D1.15
- **Candidate status** — not approved or active at Phase 1; activation requires the full D1.12 adoption process

Ownership of the Perplexity evaluation and adoption decision follows the capability ownership stages above. forge (Researcher / Critic) evaluates; human decides; bridge registers.

---

## 12. Legacy Migration Ownership

Prior systems on Desktop contain real, recoverable value. Migration ownership is explicit so nothing is modified without intent.

| Asset Category | Assessment Owner | Migration Owner | Approval |
|---|---|---|---|
| n8n workflows | brain (Archivist surveys) + human (classifies) | forge (Builder migrates) | Human approves each migration |
| Commerce automation | brain (Merchant surveys) + human (classifies) | brain (Merchant integrates) | Human approves |
| Discord / bot configs | bridge (Broker surveys) + human (classifies) | bridge (Broker integrates) | Human approves |
| SSH configs and runbooks | Human surveys and classifies | Human or bridge migrates | Human approves |
| Dashboard concepts | Human surveys and classifies | forge (Builder implements) | Human approves |
| Prompt packs | brain (Archivist surveys) | brain (Archivist integrates) | Human approves |

### Migration Sequence Rule

1. Survey and inventory first (D5.3-LEGACY-ASSET-REGISTER.md must exist)
2. Classify each asset (keep / adapt / retire) — human has final say
3. Write migration plan before touching anything (LEG-MIG-01)
4. Run old and new in parallel until new is verified (LEG-MIG-02)
5. Commerce and revenue-generating systems migrate last (LEG-MIG-03)
6. Before reimplementing any surface: confirm no prior asset satisfies it (LEG-MIG-04)

---

## 13. Org Guardrails

These rules protect the organizational structure from drift. They apply permanently unless explicitly revised by the human operator and documented in this file.

1. **Three agents, no more.** The organization runs on bridge, brain, and forge. No additional runtime agent is created without an architectural decision in D1.2 and human approval.

2. **Logical roles are not agents.** Conductor, Broker, Archivist, Planner, Merchant, Builder, Critic, and Researcher are context modes — not separate processes. Creating a new OpenClaw agent to represent a logical role is a violation of this rule.

3. **Human owns the top.** Strategic direction, phase advancement, autonomy grants, financial authorization, and policy changes are never delegated. These are permanently in the human domain.

4. **Autonomy is earned, not assumed.** Every new task type starts at Level 0. Promotion requires observed cycles and explicit human approval. No agent self-promotes.

5. **Mission Control is the record.** Agent-to-agent verbal agreements, Telegram or Discord messages, or in-flight context do not constitute organization state. State is only real when it exists in Mission Control.

6. **Bridge is the gateway.** All inter-agent task routing passes through bridge (Conductor). Agents do not call each other directly without bridge orchestrating the exchange.

7. **Memory is brain's domain.** No agent writes to the canonical memory store except brain (Archivist). Other agents file requests; brain writes.

8. **Proof or it didn't happen.** Every delegated task must produce a proof artifact — a file, log, or structured result — before it is considered complete. A channel reply (Telegram, Discord, or other) is not proof.

9. **No unbounded subagent expansion.** Agents may generate child prompts only within approved task classes, with bounded depth and defined handoff rules. Unbounded prompt chains are not delegation — they are drift.

10. **Pixel and Seeker are surfaces, not agents.** Edge devices receive tasks and return results. They do not make decisions. Until a formal edge agent architecture is defined, they operate as execution relays only.

---

## 14. Deferred Org Decisions

These organizational questions are open. They are explicitly deferred and must not be resolved informally.

| Decision | What Is Unknown | When to Decide |
|---|---|---|
| Pixel/Seeker architecture | Whether Seeker runs a full agent or acts as a thin execution relay | Phase 3 planning |
| Edge agent ownership | Who owns edge tasks if a Seeker agent is created | Phase 3 planning, after EDG-GEN-04 is resolved |
| Subagent depth rules | How many levels of sub-task nesting are permitted before escalation | D1.8-AUTOMATION_POLICY.md |
| Memory write delegation | Whether brain can delegate Archivist writes to a subagent process | D1.7-MEMORY_POLICY.md |
| Merchant scope expansion | Whether Merchant handles multi-channel commerce or only current channels | D1.8 or dedicated commerce spec |
| Autonomy register format | Exact structure of the per-task-type autonomy register in Mission Control | Before Phase 1 exit |
| Office screen ownership | Who generates agent presence data and how it is piped to the Office view | Phase 3, D1.11 or dedicated spec |
| Future agent beyond three | Whether a fourth runtime agent is ever warranted (research node, edge agent, etc.) | No sooner than Phase 3 |
| Commerce authorization threshold | The exact dollar or transaction threshold above which Merchant must escalate | D1.8-AUTOMATION_POLICY.md |

---

## 15. Immediate Org Next Steps

These are the org-facing actions that are currently blocking or adjacent to blocking.

### Right Now (Phase 0 / Phase 1 boundary)

1. **Define the `bridge` agent shell in OpenClaw** — Assign it the Conductor and Broker roles. Set workspace path. Set model config reference. This is the first concrete org activation. Do not execute model-backed tasks until D1.5-MODEL_POLICY.md is written and the routing policy is approved. (Source: BRG-OCL-04, BRG-INF-02)

2. **Create the autonomy register in Mission Control** — A simple table of task types and their current autonomy level (all Level 0). This is the record from which all future autonomy grants are tracked.

3. **Create the Team view in Mission Control** — List the three runtime agents, their logical roles, their machines, and their current task types. This is the org chart in practice.

### Phase 1

4. **Define `brain` agent in OpenClaw** — After Desktop is network-reachable. Assign Archivist, Planner, Merchant roles.

5. **Write D1.5-MODEL_POLICY.md** — Model ownership decisions (what runs local vs. cloud, which agent uses which model) must be written before any agent executes a model call. This is an org decision before it is a technical one.

6. **Classify legacy assets** — Survey Desktop. File D5.3-LEGACY-ASSET-REGISTER.md. Human classifies each asset. Assign migration owners per the table in Section 12.

7. **Issue the first real directive** — Human → Telegram → bridge (Broker receives) → bridge (Conductor creates task packet) → result filed to Mission Control → bridge (Broker routes result to Telegram). This validates the org model end-to-end.

### Phase 2

8. **Define `forge` agent in OpenClaw** — After GPU3060 is network-reachable. Assign Builder, Critic, Researcher roles.

9. **Activate first Delegation Spec** — Write D3.1-DELEGATION-SPEC-RESEARCH.md. Assign Researcher role its first real task type. Grant Autonomy Level 1 for research summaries after first observed cycles.

10. **Update Team view** — As each agent comes online and each logical role is activated, the Team view in Mission Control is updated immediately. Stale team views are a guardrail violation.

---

## 16. Crew Identity Mapping (2026-03-14)

The three runtime agents now operate under crew names for daily coordination:

| Agent | Crew Name | Crew Role | Machine |
|---|---|---|---|
| bridge | **Nami** | Chief of Staff / Navigator / Orchestrator | Mac Mini |
| brain | **Franky** | Chief Engineer / Builder | Desktop |
| forge | **Chopper** | QA / Health / Security | 3060 Laptop |

Agent names (bridge/brain/forge) remain canonical in OpenClaw config and D1.x policy. Crew names (Nami/Franky/Chopper) are the operational identity. See `registry/TRANSITION-NOTE.md` and D1.2 §19.

---

_End of D1.4 — ORG_MAP_
_Parent document: D1.1-ROADMAP.md | Architecture: D1.2-ARCHITECTURE.md | Requirements: D1.3-REQUIREMENTS.md_
_Next in series: D1.5-MODEL_POLICY.md_

docs.title: D1.5 — MODEL_POLICY
docs.content:
# D1.5 — MODEL_POLICY
**Autonomous AI Organization — Model Selection, Routing, and Cost Policy**
_Last updated: 2026-03-16 | Status: Active | Parent: D1.1-ROADMAP.md | Org: D1.4-ORG_MAP.md_

> ⚠️ **UPDATED 2026-03-16 (CREW-005):** Model assignments updated to confirmed actual state.
> Nami: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 via setup-token (subscription-backed, no API credit burn).
> Franky: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 via OAuth subscription.
> Chopper: openai-codex/gpt-5.4 via OAuth subscription.
> Local fallback: Ollama qwen2.5:3b + qwen2.5:7b on Nami only.
> bridge/brain/forge terminology updated to Nami/Franky/Chopper throughout.

---

## 1. Purpose

This document defines the policy for model selection and inference routing across the organization. It answers which model runs where, when cloud inference is permitted, how routing decisions are made, and what governs cost and usage.

**What this document does:**
- Defines model classes by capability tier
- States the routing policy (local-first, cloud-fallback)
- Maps task types to appropriate model classes
- Governs cost, usage, and cron/heartbeat behavior
- Sets per-agent model policy for bridge, brain, and forge
- Defines the conditions under which cloud inference is allowed
- Marks decisions that are still open

**What this document is not:**
- Not a setup guide or install script (see D2.x Runbooks)
- Not a local inference plan — hardware constraints and benchmarks are in D1.6-LOCAL_INFERENCE_PLAN.md
- Not a secrets or credentials policy (see D1.14-SECRETS_POLICY.md)
- Not a broker routing spec — inter-agent message routing is in D1.9-BROKER_POLICY.md

When a model is added, changed, or retired for any agent, this document is updated first. Runbooks that reference specific model configurations must be flagged for review when this file changes.

---

## 2. Scope

This policy covers:

| Scope | What It Covers |
|---|---|
| Nami / bridge (Mac Mini, 10.0.0.152) | Model used for task routing, scheduling decisions, comms brokering, orchestration |
| Franky / brain (Desktop) | Model used for engineering, build execution, code generation |
| Chopper / forge (GPU3060) | Model used for QA, verification, security checks, health monitoring |
| Cloud fallback | Conditions under which any agent may use an external API instead of local inference |
| Cron / heartbeat | Rules for model use in recurring or background tasks |
| Experimental | Routing approaches that may be explored but are not dependencies |

This policy does not cover:
- Hardware specs or RAM constraints (see D1.6)
- Which Telegram channels receive model output (see D1.9, D1.11)
- Agent autonomy levels (see D1.4 §10)

---

## 3. Policy Principles

These principles govern every model decision in the organization. They are not preferences — they are rules. Deviations require explicit human approval and must be documented.

1. **Local inference is the default.** Every worker machine (Mac Mini, Desktop, GPU3060) must run at least one local model. Local inference is attempted first for every task. Cloud inference is not the silent default.

2. **Cloud is fallback, not supplement.** Cloud inference is used when local inference cannot satisfy a task — due to capability limits, availability, or context size. It is not used simply because it is convenient or faster.

3. **No model-hopping mid-task.** A task must be assigned a model class before it begins. The model does not change during execution unless the task explicitly fails and falls back. Switching models mid-task without a failure trigger is not permitted.

4. **Recurring low-value work runs local.** Cron jobs, heartbeats, status checks, and recurring summaries MUST use local inference. These tasks MUST NOT burn cloud API tokens on a schedule.

5. **High-value irreversible tasks may use cloud.** Tasks that produce outputs a human will review and act on — a plan, a commerce recommendation, a financial flag — may use a higher-tier model including cloud, if the local model cannot meet the quality bar.

6. **Cost visibility is required.** Cloud token usage must be logged and reviewable. Invisible cost accumulation is not acceptable. Agents do not call cloud APIs in loops or without a defined output target.

7. **Model policy is written before model config.** No agent is configured with a model API key until this policy is approved. The policy comes first; the key is entered after.

8. **Experimental routing is opt-in.** Proxy routing, multi-model ensemble approaches, and similar techniques are permitted only in a designated experimental context (see §13). They are not used in production task execution without explicit policy addition here.

---

## 4. Model Classes

Model classes are capability tiers, not specific models. Specific model selection within a class is decided per agent in §9–11 and in D1.6. Classes allow the policy to remain stable when specific model versions change.

| Class | Tier | Capability Profile | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Local-Nano** | Low | Small models (1–4B parameters). Fast. Low RAM. Limited reasoning depth. | Routing decisions, keyword extraction, simple classification, status checks |
| **Local-Mid** | Medium | Mid-range models (7–14B parameters). Balanced speed and capability. | Task summaries, research extraction, code scaffolding, structured output generation |
| **Local-High** | High | Large local models (30B+ parameters). Slower. Higher RAM requirement. GPU required. | Complex planning, code review, multi-step reasoning, high-quality content generation |
| **Cloud-Standard** | Cloud | Provider API, standard tier (e.g. Anthropic claude-haiku-4-5). Moderate cost. | Overflow from local, tasks requiring recency or web-augmented retrieval |
| **Cloud-Premium** | Cloud | Provider API, premium tier (e.g. Anthropic claude-sonnet-4-6, claude-opus-4-6). Higher cost. | High-stakes planning, financial analysis, escalation-triggered tasks, human-review outputs |
| **Research-External** | External | xAI/Grok or equivalent research lane. Not for production routing. | Research intake only (see §14). Not activated at Phase 1. |

**Rule:** Local-Nano and Local-Mid classes must be available on Mac Mini before Phase 1 exits. Local-High class must be available on GPU3060 before any forge inference task is assigned.

---

## 5. Routing Policy

The routing policy defines the decision tree for model selection at task execution time. This tree is applied by bridge (Conductor) when dispatching a task, or by the executing agent when starting work.

```
Task received
      │
      ▼
Is a model class assigned in the task packet?
      │
      ├── YES → Use the assigned class. Apply agent-specific model within that class (§9–11).
      │
      └── NO → Assign class based on task type map (§6).
                     │
                     ▼
              Is local inference available for the assigned class on this agent's machine?
                     │
                     ├── YES → Execute locally. Log model used and token estimate.
                     │
                     └── NO → Is cloud fallback permitted for this task type? (§12)
                                    │
                                    ├── YES → Use cloud fallback. Log provider, model, and token count.
                                    │          Post escalation note to Telegram if cost threshold exceeded.
                                    │
                                    └── NO → Mark task BLOCKED. Escalate to human.
                                             Do not execute.
```

### Routing Constraints

- A task marked as requiring local-only MUST NOT fall back to cloud even if local is slow or unavailable.
- A task that fails local inference due to context size overflow MAY fall back to cloud if the task type permits it.
- Bridge (Conductor) must log every routing decision: which class was selected, which model was used, and whether it was local or cloud.

---

## 6. Task-to-Model Mapping

This table defines the default model class for each declared task type. Task types not in this table default to Local-Mid until this table is updated.

| Task Type | Default Class | Cloud Fallback Permitted | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Task routing and dispatch | Local-Nano | No | Cron-safe. Must stay local. |
| Status check / health ping | Local-Nano | No | Never cloud. |
| Recurring task summaries | Local-Mid | No | Scheduled. Must stay local. |
| Research brief extraction | Local-Mid | Yes (Standard) | Cloud permitted if source is complex or long |
| Code scaffolding | Local-Mid | Yes (Standard) | Cloud permitted for large context |
| Code review / QA | Local-Mid | Yes (Standard) | Cloud permitted if complexity warrants |
| Knowledge archiving | Local-Mid | No | Archivist writes. Local only. |
| Planning and goal decomposition | Local-High | Yes (Premium) | Cloud permitted for high-stakes plans |
| Commerce monitoring | Local-Mid | No | Merchant logic. Stays local. |
| Commerce anomaly flagging | Local-Mid | Yes (Standard) | Cloud permitted for ambiguous anomalies |
| Financial tracking / digest | Local-Mid | Yes (Premium) | Cloud permitted for human-reviewed outputs |
| Content generation (draft) | Local-High | Yes (Standard) | Cloud as overflow for quality bar |
| Content generation (final/publish) | Local-High | Yes (Premium) | Human reviews before publish; cloud permitted |
| Escalation response draft | Local-High | Yes (Premium) | Cloud preferred for human-reviewed escalations |
| Experiment execution | Local-High | Yes (Standard) | forge task. Cloud as overflow. |
| Ops-channel digest generation | Local-Mid | No | Recurring. Must stay local. |

---

## 7. Cost / Usage Policy

### Approved Cloud Providers

| Provider | Status | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Anthropic (Claude API) | Approved — Phase 1 | Primary cloud fallback lane |
| xAI / Grok | Not activated | Research lane only — see §14 |
| OpenAI / openai-codex | Approved (forge/Chopper only) | openai-codex/gpt-5.4 is the primary model for Chopper (forge). Approved for Chopper's QA, verification, and security tasks via OAuth subscription. Not approved for Nami or Franky without explicit human approval. |
| Other providers | Not approved | Same. Requires human approval and policy update before any use. |

### Cost Controls

1. **No API key is entered on any agent until this policy is approved.** (Source: D1.4 §15 item 1)
2. **Cloud usage is logged per task.** Every cloud API call must emit a log entry with: task ID, model used, estimated token count, and reason for cloud routing.
3. **Recurring tasks MUST NOT use cloud.** Cron jobs, scheduled digests, and heartbeats are local-only. A cron task that silently burns cloud tokens is a policy violation.
4. **A cost threshold alert is sent to Telegram (ops-channel) when accumulated cloud spend in a session exceeds a defined limit.** Threshold TBD — set before any cloud key is activated. Logged in D1.14-SECRETS_POLICY.md alongside key management.
5. **No cloud API call is made in a loop without a defined completion condition.** Unbounded cloud calls are a policy violation regardless of context.
6. **Usage is reviewed at each phase boundary** by the human operator. If cost is disproportionate to output, routing decisions are revised.

### Token Budget Guidance (Non-Binding, Direction Only)

These are not hard limits — they are guidance for task design:

| Task Type | Suggested Budget |
|---|---|
| Routing / classification | < 500 tokens total |
| Research brief | < 4,000 tokens total |
| Code scaffold | < 8,000 tokens total |
| Planning document | < 12,000 tokens total |
| Financial digest | < 6,000 tokens total |

---

## 8. Heartbeat / Cron Policy

Recurring tasks and health checks are the highest-risk category for invisible cost accumulation. These rules apply to all scheduled or triggered recurring model calls.

| Rule | Detail |
|---|---|
| Heartbeats are signal-only | Status pings, health checks, and presence signals MUST NOT invoke a language model. They are system-level calls, not model calls. |
| Recurring summaries use local | Any scheduled summary, digest, or report generation uses Local-Nano or Local-Mid only. Cloud is not permitted on a schedule. |
| Cron tasks must have a defined output | No cron task invokes a model without a defined output target (a file, a Telegram message, a Mission Control entry). |
| Cron frequency is approved by human | New recurring tasks that invoke a model must be approved by the human before the cron is registered. Frequency must be stated in the approval. |
| Cron tasks log every execution | Every cron-triggered model call logs: timestamp, model, task ID, output destination. No silent executions. |
| Escalation on consecutive failures | If a cron task fails 3 consecutive times, bridge (Conductor) marks it BLOCKED and posts an escalation to Telegram. It does not retry indefinitely. |

---

## 9. Nami (Bridge) Model Policy

Nami handles routing, scheduling, comms brokering, and orchestration. It is always-on and must operate even when cloud is unavailable.

| Field | Policy |
|---|---|
| **Primary model** | **anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6** via setup-token auth (SEC-003, subscription-backed — no per-token API billing) |
| Local fallback | Ollama qwen2.5:3b (Local-Nano, routing/dispatch) + qwen2.5:7b (Local-Mid, orchestration) |
| Auth method | Setup-token (anthropic:manual profile in auth-profiles.json). Not API key. Subscription billing. |
| Heartbeat model | ollama/qwen2.5:3b — local only, no cloud |
| Cron model | ollama/qwen2.5:7b — local only, no cloud |
| Cloud permitted | For complex orchestration and human-reviewed outputs (via subscription, not API credits) |
| Cloud forbidden | All cron tasks, all routing decisions, all heartbeats, all status checks |
| Model must be available at boot | Yes — local Ollama models installed and passing stability. Cloud subscription always available. |
| Acceptable failure mode | If local model is unavailable, Nami uses cloud (subscription). If cloud subscription expires, Nami marks itself DEGRADED. |
| Local model status | ✅ Installed — Ollama 0.17.7, qwen2.5:3b + qwen2.5:7b, 20/20 stability PASS. |

---

## 10. Franky (Brain) Model Policy

Franky handles engineering, builds, deployments, and code execution. It runs on Desktop (Windows, 10.0.0.251).

| Field | Policy |
|---|---|
| **Primary model** | **anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6** via OAuth subscription (subscription-backed — no per-token API billing) |
| Auth method | Anthropic OAuth subscription on Franky's OpenClaw instance |
| Local fallback | TBD — GPU available on Desktop; local model to be configured in Phase B |
