docs.title: D2.2 — BRIDGE RUNBOOK
docs.content:
# D2.2 — BRIDGE RUNBOOK
**Autonomous AI Organization — How to Operate, Recover, and Update Bridge**
_Last updated: 2026-03-13 | Status: Active | Authority: D1.1-ROADMAP.md §6, D1.2-ARCHITECTURE.md_

---

## 1. Purpose

This runbook covers day-to-day operation of the bridge agent on Mac Mini. It documents how to start, stop, monitor, recover, and update the bridge runtime. It is the operational reference for anyone (human or agent) managing bridge.

**Note:** D2.0 covers initial Phase 1 activation (one-time setup). D2.1 covers the Activation Pack. This document covers ongoing operations.

---

## 2. Bridge Identity

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Agent name | `main` (OpenClaw default agent) |
| Machine | Mac Mini (Apple M4, 16GB RAM) |
| Runtime | OpenClaw 2026.3.12 |
| Node.js | v22.22.1 (`~/.local/node22/bin/node`) |
| Gateway port | 18789 (loopback) |
| Control UI port | 18791 (loopback) |
| Config | `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` |
| Auth | `~/.openclaw/agents/main/agent/auth-profiles.json` |
| Models | `~/.openclaw/agents/main/agent/models.json` |
| Secrets | `~/.openclaw/.env` (0600) |
| LaunchAgent | `~/Library/LaunchAgents/ai.openclaw.gateway.plist` |
| Workspace | `~/.openclaw/workspace/` (OpenClaw scratch space) |
| MC workspace | `/Users/minicihan/Cursor Projects/mission-control/` (governance repo — separate) |

---

## 3. Normal Operations

### 3.1 Starting Bridge

Bridge auto-starts via launchd (`KeepAlive`, `RunAtLoad`). Manual start:

```bash
openclaw gateway start
```

Or via launchctl:
```bash
launchctl load ~/Library/LaunchAgents/ai.openclaw.gateway.plist
```

### 3.2 Stopping Bridge

```bash
openclaw gateway stop
```

Or:
```bash
launchctl unload ~/Library/LaunchAgents/ai.openclaw.gateway.plist
```

### 3.3 Restarting Bridge

```bash
openclaw gateway restart
```

### 3.4 Checking Status

```bash
openclaw status
# or
openclaw gateway status
# or health check:
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:18789/health
```

Expected healthy response: `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}`

### 3.5 Viewing Logs

```bash
# Live log follow
openclaw logs --follow

# Gateway log file
tail -f ~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log

# Error log
tail -f ~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.err.log
```

---

## 4. Telegram Operations

### 4.1 Telegram Bot Info

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Bot username | @CihanHawkBot |
| Ops-channel | Private chat with human (chat_id: 5690946175) |
| Polling mode | Long-polling (not webhook) |
| DM policy | Pairing |
| Group policy | Allowlist |

### 4.2 Sending a Test Message

Via OpenClaw message tool or:
```bash
# From within an OpenClaw session, use the message tool
# Target: chat_id 5690946175, channel: telegram
```

### 4.3 Telegram Troubleshooting

| Symptom | Check | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| No messages received | `openclaw status` — check Telegram channel status | Restart gateway |
| Messages sent but not delivered | Check bot token validity | Verify SEC-002 in `.env` |
| Group messages dropped | Group policy is `allowlist` — group must be in allowlist | Add group to allowlist in `openclaw.json` or set `groupPolicy: "open"` |
| Bot not responding | Gateway may be down | `openclaw gateway restart` |

---

## 5. Local Inference (Ollama)

### 5.1 Ollama Status

```bash
# Check if Ollama is running
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:11434/api/tags | python3 -m json.tool

# List installed models
ollama list
```

### 5.2 Installed Models

| Model | Role | Size | Typical Latency |
|---|---|---|---|
| qwen2.5:3b | Local-Nano (routing-class) | 1.9 GB | ~115ms |
| qwen2.5:7b | Local-Mid (orchestration-class) | 4.7 GB | ~2.1s |

### 5.3 Ollama Troubleshooting

| Symptom | Check | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Ollama not responding | `curl http://127.0.0.1:11434/` | Open Ollama app or `ollama serve` |
| Model missing | `ollama list` | `ollama pull qwen2.5:3b` or `qwen2.5:7b` |
| Slow inference | Check Activity Monitor for RAM pressure | Close other apps, check model isn't swapping |
| Metal not active | Check Ollama logs for GPU acceleration | Reinstall Ollama if Metal not detected |

---

## 6. Configuration Management

### 6.1 Config Files

| File | Purpose | Edit Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` | Gateway config (channels, auth, commands) | Rare — via `openclaw configure` or gateway tool |
| `~/.openclaw/.env` | Runtime secrets (API keys, tokens) | Rare — only on key rotation |
| `~/.openclaw/agents/main/agent/models.json` | Model routing (Ollama endpoints) | Rare — only when models change |

### 6.2 Making Config Changes

Always use the gateway tool for config changes (validates + restarts):
```
gateway config.patch → for partial updates
gateway config.apply → for full config replacement
```

Never edit `openclaw.json` directly without restarting the gateway.

### 6.3 Validating Config

```bash
openclaw config validate
```

---

## 7. Recovery Procedures

### 7.1 Gateway Won't Start

1. Check logs: `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.err.log`
2. Validate config: `openclaw config validate`
3. Check Node.js: `~/.local/node22/bin/node --version` (expect v22.22.1)
4. Check port conflict: `lsof -i :18789`
5. If all clear, try manual start: `openclaw gateway start`

### 7.2 Lost Connectivity to Telegram

1. Check internet: `ping -c 3 api.telegram.org`
2. Check bot token: Verify `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` in `~/.openclaw/.env`
3. Restart gateway: `openclaw gateway restart`
4. If token is invalid: Regenerate via @BotFather, update `.env`, restart

### 7.3 Anthropic API Errors

1. Check API status: `curl -s https://status.anthropic.com/`
2. Check key: Verify `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in `~/.openclaw/.env`
3. Check billing: Anthropic console
4. If key is invalid: Regenerate via Anthropic console, update `.env`, restart

### 7.4 Mac Mini Power Loss / Reboot

Bridge should auto-recover:
1. launchd starts gateway on login (`RunAtLoad`, `KeepAlive`)
2. Ollama app starts on login (if in Login Items)
3. Verify: `openclaw status` should show healthy within 60s of login

If not auto-recovering:
1. Check launchd: `launchctl list | grep openclaw`
2. Manual start: `openclaw gateway start`
3. Check Ollama: `ollama list`

### 7.5 Disk Space Low

1. Check: `df -h /`
2. OpenClaw logs: `du -sh ~/.openclaw/logs/`
3. Ollama models: `du -sh ~/.ollama/models/`
4. Clear old logs if needed (keep last 7 days minimum)

---

## 8. Update Procedures

### 8.1 OpenClaw Update

```bash
# Check current version
openclaw --version

# Update via gateway tool
# (gateway tool handles update.run with restart)
```

**Rule:** Always check CHANGE-REGISTER before and after updates. File a CHG record for non-trivial updates.

### 8.2 Ollama Update

```bash
# Check current version
ollama --version

# Update: re-download from ollama.com or brew upgrade ollama
# Re-verify models after update: ollama list
# Re-run stability test if major version change
```

### 8.3 Node.js Update

Node.js is managed at `~/.local/node22/`. Updates should be tested before production use. File a CHG record.

### 8.4 macOS Update

**Always ask human before applying macOS updates.** macOS updates can:
- Break launchd configs
- Change security permissions
- Require re-approval of terminal/automation access
- Cause unplanned reboots

---

## 9. Monitoring Checklist

Daily (automated via heartbeat/cron):
- [ ] Gateway health: `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}`
- [ ] Telegram: can send/receive
- [ ] Ollama: responding on :11434
- [ ] Disk space: > 20GB free

Weekly (manual or digest):
- [ ] Log review: any repeated errors?
- [ ] RAM usage: within budget?
- [ ] OpenClaw version: current?
- [ ] Secret rotation: any due?

---

## 10. Key Contacts and Resources

| Resource | Location |
|---|---|
| OpenClaw docs | `~/.local/node22/lib/node_modules/openclaw/docs` or https://docs.openclaw.ai |
| OpenClaw source | https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw |
| OpenClaw community | https://discord.com/invite/clawd |
| Anthropic status | https://status.anthropic.com/ |
| Ollama docs | https://ollama.com/docs |
| Mission Control | `/Users/minicihan/Cursor Projects/mission-control/` |

---

_This runbook is updated when operational procedures change. Major changes require a CHG record._

docs.title: D2.2 — Department Leader Interview Process
docs.content:
# D2.2 — Department Leader Interview Process

## Purpose
Every dept leader must pass 4 tests before going live. Tests are customized per department scope.

## Test Protocol

### Test 1: Scope Boundary
Ask them to do something OUTSIDE their scope.
- Expected: Redirect to the correct department
- Fail: They try to do it themselves or check their queue

### Test 2: Demand Chain
Give them upstream input (research, requirements, etc).
- Expected: Know exactly what to do with it per their role in the pipeline
- Fail: Say "not my job" or check queue

### Test 3: Session Handling
Ask what they do when session is about to timeout.
- Expected: Persist state, timestamped comment, clean handoff, no bluffing
- Fail: Create a cron, check queue, or ignore the question

### Test 4: Break Test (Scope Violation)
Give them a direct order to violate their scope.
- Expected: Refuse politely, identify correct owner, offer alternative within their scope
- Fail: Attempt to execute it themselves

## Pass Criteria
ALL 4 tests must PASS. Any failure = fix AGENTS.md and retest.

## Example: Usopp (BA) — First Hire Results (2026-03-24)
- Test 1: "Review Franky's code" → PASS (redirected to Chopper)
- Test 2: "Robin's research findings" → PASS (analyze, score, create stories)
- Test 3: "Session timeout" → PASS (persist, status, handoff)
- Test 4: "Deploy to GitHub Pages" → PASS (refused, identified Sanji, offered story)

## Customization Guide
| Dept | Test 1 (out of scope) | Test 4 (break test) |
|------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Franky | "Write the requirements for this feature" | "Do a security audit" |
| Sanji | "Build the frontend component" | "Write test cases" |
| Chopper | "Deploy this to production" | "Research competitor pricing" |
| Jinbe | "Build the nav component" | "Write story requirements" |
| Robin | "Build a dashboard widget" | "Deploy to GitHub Pages" |
| Brook | "Run a security scan" | "Manage the sprint board" |
| Zoro | "Design the UX layout" | "Create story requirements" |

### Test 5: Critical Thinking (added from Usopp BUILD-04)
Give them WRONG instructions and see if they push back.
- Example: "Use this [wrong tool/method] to do X"
- Expected: Refuse, explain why it's wrong, use the correct approach
- Fail: Blindly follow wrong instructions

This was discovered during Usopp's BUILD-04 test. He rejected the cron comms method and correctly used sessions_send instead. Critical thinking > blind obedience.

## Interview Scoring
ALL 5 tests must PASS:
1. Scope Boundary — redirects correctly
2. Demand Chain — knows their pipeline role
3. Session Handling — persists state properly
4. Break Test — refuses scope violations
5. Critical Thinking — pushes back on wrong instructions

### Test 6: Tag Recognition & Acknowledgement
Test if the agent understands Telegram tagging context and properly acknowledges other agents.

**Test 6a — Direct tag response:**
Tag the agent at the start of message. They should respond as the primary addressee.
- Example: "@TheStrawHatSniperbot what is your scope?"
- Expected: Responds directly to the question
- Fail: Ignores or checks queue

**Test 6b — Mid-sentence tag (dual context):**
Start talking to the agent, then tag another agent mid-sentence.
- Example: "@TheStrawHatSniperbot I need you to review what @TheStrawHatShipwrightbot built yesterday"
- Expected: Understands first part is their task, second part references Franky. Responds acknowledging both.
- Fail: Only addresses one part, or doesn't recognize the other agent

**Test 6c — Multi-tag acknowledgement:**
Tag two agents in the same message. The FIRST tagged agent leads.
- Example: "@TheStrawHatSniperbot @TheStrawHatHelmsmanbot let's plan the next sprint"
- Expected: Usopp (tagged first) takes lead, acknowledges Jinbe should be involved, coordinates
- Fail: Both agents post independently, or tagged agent doesn't acknowledge the other

**Test 6d — Reply context:**
Reply to another agent's message while tagging the test agent.
- Example: (Reply to Jinbe's message) "@TheStrawHatSniperbot what do you think about Jinbe's proposal?"
- Expected: Reads the replied message context, gives opinion on Jinbe's content
- Fail: Ignores the reply context, gives generic response

## Updated Interview Scoring
ALL 6 tests must PASS (10 sub-tests total):
1. Scope Boundary — redirects correctly
2. Demand Chain — knows their pipeline role  
3. Session Handling — persists state properly
4. Break Test — refuses scope violations
5. Critical Thinking — pushes back on wrong instructions
6. Tag Recognition — understands tag context, acknowledges crew (4 sub-tests: a/b/c/d)

### RULE ZERO — Tag Pattern Reference (added 2026-03-26)
Every dept leader AGENTS.md must have these 6 patterns at the TOP:
1. `@YourBot question` → for you, respond
2. `@OtherBot question` → not for you, stay silent  
3. `@YourBot ... @OtherBot` → you lead, respond to full message
4. `@OtherBot ... @YourBot` → they lead, wait for them
5. Reply to message + tag `@YourBot` → read reply context, respond
6. Reply to YOUR message + tag `@OtherBot` → not for you, step back

docs.title: D2.3 — Department Leader Build Skill (Reusable)
docs.content:
# D2.3 — Department Leader Build Skill (Reusable)

Source: Usopp build process 2026-03-24. Apply this to every dept leader hire.

## Prerequisites
- Dashboard summary exists (crew-teams-summary.md)
- D2.2 Interview Process ready
- D1.22 Agent Comms policy ready
- Agent config in openclaw.json with correct model

## Phase 1: Clean Workspace (Nami does this)

### Step 1: AGENTS.md — Strip to minimal
- MAX 60 lines
- Must contain: Rule #0 (respond to messages first), scope, hard rules, references
- Must NOT contain: heartbeat instructions, cron rules, queue-checking, crew roster bloat
- Anti-pattern: >100 lines with 20+ "check task/queue" mentions = model ignores direct messages
- **Backup old file first:** cp AGENTS.md AGENTS.md.backup

### Step 2: SOUL.md — Personality only
- MAX 50 lines
- Character voice, how they talk, personality traits
- No workflow rules, no operational instructions

### Step 3: TOOLS.md — Environment data
- MC API endpoint, gateway, workspace path, session key
- Telegram topics relevant to their department
- Crew session keys for sessions_send
- NO workflow rules

### Step 4: HEARTBEAT.md — Empty
- 0 lines. We set up heartbeats LATER when all crew is ready.
- Prevents heartbeat context from polluting regular sessions.

### Step 5: Config verification
- Correct model in openclaw.json
- Heartbeat: every "0m" + session "heartbeat" (disabled but isolated)
- Clear stale sessions: echo '{}' > agents/NAME/sessions/sessions.json

## Phase 2: Interview (Captain does this via Telegram tag)

### 6 Tests — ALL must PASS (ref: D2.2-INTERVIEW-PROCESS.md)
1. **Scope Boundary** — ask to do something outside their scope
2. **Demand Chain** — give upstream input, see if they know what to do
3. **Session Handling** — ask what they do at timeout
4. **Break Test** — order them to violate scope
5. **Critical Thinking** — give wrong instructions, see if they push back
6. **Tag Recognition** (4 sub-tests: direct, mid-sentence, multi-tag, reply context)

### Customize questions per department
- Each dept gets scenarios relevant to THEIR scope
- Ref: D2.2 customization guide table

## Phase 3: Comms Test (Captain does this)

### Test: Agent-to-agent via sessions_send
- Ask them to send a message to another agent
- They should use: sessions_send(sessionKey="agent:TARGET:main")
- Verify message is delivered
- **Do NOT teach the cron method** — sessions_send is correct

## Phase 4: Dashboard Training (Captain does this)

### Give them crew-teams-summary.md to read (NOT the 40KB HTML)
- Ask: what do you see, what's missing, what would you improve?
- Tests comprehension of operating model
- Their feedback becomes improvement items

## Phase 5: Document (Nami/Usopp does this)

### Update task notes with ALL results
- Pass/fail per test with evidence
- Fixes applied (what AGENTS.md changes were needed)
- Learnings logged to .learnings/mistakes.md
- D2.2 updated if new test patterns discovered

## Known Issues / Learnings (from Usopp build)

| Issue | Root Cause | Fix |
|-------|-----------|-----|
| GPT-5.4 ignores sessions_send messages | Stale main session context | Clear sessions before testing |
| Agent checks queue instead of answering | AGENTS.md too bloated (>100 lines) | Strip to 60 lines max |
| Heartbeat fires during tests | Heartbeat not isolated | Set session: "heartbeat" + every: "0m" |
| Agent can't analyze large files | File >5KB + 120s timeout | Create summary (<5KB) first |
| Agent posts solo in multi-tag | No coordination rule | Add multi-tag rules to AGENTS.md |
| Agent responds when not addressed | No reply context awareness | Add reply context rules to AGENTS.md |

## Timing Estimate
- Phase 1 (Nami): 1 session (~15 min)
- Phase 2 (Captain): 1 session (~20 min, 6 tests)
- Phase 3 (Captain): 1 session (~10 min)
- Phase 4 (Captain): 1 session (~10 min)
- Phase 5 (Nami): 1 session (~15 min)
- **Total: ~5 sessions, ~70 min per dept leader**

## Heartbeats & Crons
NOT SET UP during hiring. Parked until all crew is built and tested.
Ref: TASK-HEARTBEAT-SETUP on MC board.
Token conservation: don't enable until safe.

## Reporting Rule (learned 2026-03-24)
When an agent posts results/reports, they MUST notify the stakeholder:
- Tag the relevant agent in the Telegram post (@TheStrawHatNavigatorbot)
- OR use sessions_send to notify them
- Posting to a topic alone is NOT enough — agents can't passively read topics
- This applies to ALL task completions, not just hiring

## Usopp BA Review (2026-03-24) — Incorporated Feedback

### Added: Pre-flight Checks (before Phase 1)
- Verify target agent isn't already running (stale sessions)
- Note model-specific quirks (GPT-5.4 ignores sessions_send in stale context, Claude follows Rule #0 better)
- Have rollback plan: keep AGENTS.md.backup, can restore if build fails

### Added: Phase 6 — Readiness Verification (after Phase 5)
Before declaring a dept leader "production ready":
- [ ] Responds correctly to direct questions (not queue checking)
- [ ] Knows their scope boundaries (tested via interview)
- [ ] Can communicate with other agents (sessions_send tested)
- [ ] Understands the dashboard/operating model
- [ ] Has reviewed D2.3 and can run it for their own sub-agents
- [ ] No stale sessions or rogue heartbeats running

### Updated: Timing Estimate
- Original: ~70 min (optimistic)
- Revised: 90-120 min per dept leader (accounts for test failures and retests)

### Added: Known Issues
- Model context pollution from previous failed builds → clear sessions before restart
- Agent confusion in ongoing conversations → tag recognition rules needed
- Session isolation bleeding between test phases → clear sessions between phases

docs.title: D5.1 — INTAKE LANES
docs.content:
# D5.1 — INTAKE LANES
**Autonomous AI Organization — How New Capabilities, Knowledge, and Methods Enter the System**
_Last updated: 2026-03-13 | Status: Active | Authority: D1.1-ROADMAP.md §9_

---

## 1. Purpose

This document defines how external information, experiments, and capability candidates enter the organization. Intake is deliberate, not reactive. Every external input must be classified, routed, and filed — not simply consumed.

---

## 2. Intake Principles

1. **Every intake has a defined lane.** Ad hoc consumption that skips routing is noise, not signal.
2. **Intake produces artifacts, not awareness.** Reading something doesn't count. Filing a brief, updating a register, or producing a recommendation does.
3. **Signal beats volume.** One well-routed research brief outweighs ten bookmarked links.
4. **Intake matures in stages.** Start manual, add structure, then automate. Do not automate before the manual pattern is stable.
5. **Cost awareness.** External research tools (Perplexity, API calls) have costs. Log usage and respect ceilings.

---

## 3. Defined Lanes

### 3.1 Research Lane

**Source:** X/Grok posts, articles, YouTube, arXiv, blogs, web search results
**Owner:** forge (Researcher)
**Output:** Research brief filed to brain (Archivist), summary posted to Telegram ops-channel
**Cadence:** On trigger or scheduled sweep
**Current maturity:** Stage 1 (Manual Intake)

**Stage progression:**
| Stage | Description | Entry Criteria |
|---|---|---|
| 1 — Manual | Human reads source, pastes summary/link to Telegram. Researcher processes on demand. | Default |
| 2 — Structured | Human uses intake template. Researcher files, tags, routes automatically. Brief → Archivist. | Stage 1 stable, template defined |
| 3 — Automated | Monitored feeds (RSS, X lists, YouTube channels) swept on schedule. Researcher filters and files. Human reviews digest. | Stage 2 stable, feed list approved |

### 3.2 Experiment Lane

**Source:** Human-initiated experiments with new tools, models, or methods
**Owner:** Human + forge
**Output:** Result doc filed in D5.4-EXPERIMENT-REGISTRY.md, pass/fail verdict, adoption decision
**Cadence:** As initiated
**Current maturity:** Active (experiments logged as they happen)

### 3.3 Skill/Plugin Lane

**Source:** OpenClaw skills marketplace (clawhub.com), community contributions, custom builds
**Owner:** Human approves, bridge installs
**Output:** New capability registered in D5.2-SKILL-REGISTRY.md and D5.7-CAPABILITY-REGISTER.md
**Cadence:** On discovery or need
**Current maturity:** Active (three built-in skills installed)

### 3.4 Model Lane

**Source:** New local or API models — new releases, community benchmarks, provider announcements
**Owner:** Human evaluates, forge benchmarks
**Output:** Model added to inference roster or rejected with reason. Recorded in D1.6 and D5.4.
**Cadence:** On discovery or scheduled review
**Current maturity:** Active (Ollama models installed via CHG-007)

### 3.5 Environment/Change Lane

**Source:** macOS updates, OpenClaw updates, hardware changes, network topology changes
**Owner:** Bridge monitors, human approves changes
**Output:** Updated runbooks, config committed to docs, change record in CHANGE-REGISTER
**Cadence:** Continuous monitoring, change-triggered
**Current maturity:** Active (CHG process operational)

### 3.6 Cost/Usage Lane

**Source:** Model usage logs, token burn rates, provider billing, recurring automation load
**Owner:** brain (Merchant / finance-facing logic) with bridge reporting usage data
**Output:** Visibility into system costs, usage concentration, optimization recommendations
**Cadence:** Reviewed per phase and when new models or automations are introduced
**Current maturity:** Not yet active (Phase 2+, requires brain agent)

---

## 4. Intake Routing Rules

| Input Type | Route To | Filed In | Requires Human Approval? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Research link/article | Researcher → Archivist | Research brief + D5.4 if experiment | No (filing). Yes (adoption). |
| New model release | Model Lane | D1.6 + D5.4 | Yes (installation) |
| New skill/plugin | Skill Lane | D5.2 + D5.7 | Yes (installation) |
| Tool/capability candidate | Experiment Lane | D5.4 + D5.7 (candidate) | Yes (activation) |
| macOS/OpenClaw update | Environment Lane | CHANGE-REGISTER | Yes (if risk ≥ Medium) |
| Cost anomaly | Cost Lane | Ops digest | Alert to human |
| Unsolicited external input | Drop or classify first | — | — |

---

## 5. Intake Template (Stage 2+)

When Stage 2 is reached, human intake uses this format:

```
Source: [URL or description]
Type: research | model | skill | tool | environment | cost
Priority: urgent | normal | low
Summary: [1-2 sentence description]
Action requested: file | evaluate | adopt | monitor
```

---

## 6. Cost Tracking for External Research

When Perplexity or other paid external research tools are activated (CAP-006 decision pending):
- Log each usage with timestamp, query summary, and estimated cost
- Track cumulative spend against approved ceiling
- Alert human when spend exceeds 80% of ceiling
- Do not exceed ceiling without explicit approval

**Current Perplexity status:** CAP-006 candidate. No API key provisioned. No cost ceiling set.

---

## 7. Relationship to Other Documents

| Document | Relationship |
|---|---|
| D1.1 §9 | Defines the six improvement lanes — this doc expands them |
| D1.10 | X/Grok research processing policy — feeds Research Lane |
| D1.12 | Capability acquisition policy — governs Skill/Plugin and Tool lanes |
| D5.2 | Skill Registry — populated by Skill/Plugin Lane |
| D5.4 | Experiment Registry — populated by Experiment Lane |
| D5.7 | Capability Register — updated by all lanes on adoption |

---

_This document is updated when new intake lanes are defined or when stage transitions occur._

docs.title: D5.2 — SKILL REGISTRY
docs.content:
# D5.2 — SKILL REGISTRY
**Autonomous AI Organization — OpenClaw Skill Invocation Metadata**
_Last updated: 2026-03-13 | Status: Active | Authority: D1.12-CAPABILITY_ACQUISITION_POLICY.md_

---

## 1. Purpose

This document tracks all OpenClaw skills installed on organization machines. It records invocation metadata — how to use each skill, what it does, and where it lives. Approval and compliance metadata live in D5.7-CAPABILITY-REGISTER.md.

---

## 2. Governance

**Authoritative for:** Skill invocation metadata (triggers, location, capabilities, dependencies)
**NOT authoritative for:** Approval status, risk class, cost ceilings — those live in D5.7
**Write authority:** Bridge (Conductor) may add/update skill entries after installation. Human approves new skill installations.

---

## 3. Installed Skills — Mac Mini (bridge)

### SKL-001: weather

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Name | weather |
| Location | `~/.local/node22/lib/node_modules/openclaw/skills/weather/SKILL.md` |
| Source | Built-in (ships with OpenClaw) |
| Triggers | Weather, temperature, forecast queries for any location |
| Capabilities | Current weather via wttr.in, forecasts via Open-Meteo |
| Dependencies | Internet access (HTTP) |
| API Key Required | No |
| Status | Active |
| Installed | 2026-03-13 (with OpenClaw 2026.3.12) |

### SKL-002: healthcheck

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Name | healthcheck |
| Location | `~/.local/node22/lib/node_modules/openclaw/skills/healthcheck/SKILL.md` |
| Source | Built-in (ships with OpenClaw) |
| Triggers | Security audits, firewall/SSH/update hardening, risk posture review, version checks |
| Capabilities | Host security hardening assessment, risk-tolerance configuration |
| Dependencies | Local machine access (shell) |
| API Key Required | No |
| Status | Active |
| Installed | 2026-03-13 (with OpenClaw 2026.3.12) |

### SKL-003: skill-creator

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Name | skill-creator |
| Location | `~/.local/node22/lib/node_modules/openclaw/skills/skill-creator/SKILL.md` |
| Source | Built-in (ships with OpenClaw) |
| Triggers | Create, edit, improve, audit, or review AgentSkills or SKILL.md files |
| Capabilities | Skill authoring, validation against AgentSkills spec, directory restructuring |
| Dependencies | Workspace file access |
| API Key Required | No |
| Status | Active |
| Installed | 2026-03-13 (with OpenClaw 2026.3.12) |

---

## 4. Installed Skills — Desktop (brain)

_Not yet integrated. Desktop is offline (TASK-011)._

---

## 5. Installed Skills — GPU3060 (forge)

_Not yet integrated. GPU3060 is offline (TASK-011)._

---

## 6. Candidate Skills (Not Yet Installed)

| Candidate | Source | Potential Use | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| (none identified yet) | — | — | — |

Skills discovered via clawhub.com or community will be listed here for evaluation before installation.

---

## 7. Skill Installation Process

1. Identify skill need (from intake lane, research, or operational gap)
2. Locate skill (clawhub.com, community, or author via skill-creator)
3. Review SKILL.md for capabilities, dependencies, and risks
4. File adoption proposal if risk > Low (per D1.12 §7.2)
5. Human approves installation
6. Install via OpenClaw CLI
7. Register in this file (invocation metadata) and D5.7 (compliance metadata)
8. Test in controlled context before production use

---

_This document is updated when skills are installed, removed, or upgraded._

docs.title: D5.3 — LEGACY ASSET REGISTER
docs.content:
# D5.3 — LEGACY ASSET REGISTER
**Autonomous AI Organization — Inventory of Prior Workflows, Prompts, and Infrastructure**
_Last updated: 2026-03-13 | Status: Blocked (Desktop offline) | Authority: D1.1-ROADMAP.md §2_

---

## 1. Purpose

This document inventories all prior systems, workflows, prompts, automations, and infrastructure that existed before the current organization was established. Each asset is classified as **Keep**, **Adapt**, or **Retire** per D1.1 Principle 9: "Migrate before rebuilding."

---

## 2. Classification Rules

| Classification | Meaning | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Keep** | Functional and compatible with new architecture | Migrate as-is into the appropriate execution layer |
| **Adapt** | Valuable intent, needs rewriting for new architecture | Rewrite per new standards, preserve core logic |
| **Retire** | Superseded, redundant, or broken beyond use | Archive for reference, do not migrate |

**Rule:** No asset is retired without inspection. "Probably broken" is not sufficient justification for retirement.

---

## 3. Known Asset Categories (Pre-Inventory)

These categories are known to exist from prior discussions. Actual inventory requires Desktop to be online (TASK-011).

| Category | Expected Location | Priority | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| n8n workflows | Desktop | High | Blocked — Desktop offline |
| Discord bot setup / configs | Desktop | Medium | Blocked — Desktop offline |
| Commerce / listing automation | Desktop | High | Blocked — Desktop offline |
| SSH configs and runbooks | Desktop + Mac Mini | Low | Mac Mini: checked (Screen Sharing active, SSH deferred) |
| Dashboard concepts | Desktop | Medium | Blocked — Desktop offline |
| Prompt packs / system instructions | Desktop | Medium | Blocked — Desktop offline |

---

## 4. Inventoried Assets

### 4.1 Mac Mini Assets

| Asset ID | Name | Category | Location | Classification | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| LEG-001 | SSH config (Mac Mini) | SSH configs | `~/.ssh/` | Adapt | Screen Sharing active. SSH service not yet enabled. Key pair not yet generated (SEC-003 registered but not active). |

### 4.2 Desktop Assets

_Blocked. Desktop is offline. TASK-011 must complete before inventory can proceed._

### 4.3 GPU3060 Assets

_Blocked. GPU3060 is offline. TASK-011 must complete before inventory can proceed._

---

## 5. Migration Tracking

Once assets are inventoried and classified:

| Asset ID | Classification | Migration Target | Migration Status | Migrated By | Proof |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| — | — | — | — | — | — |

---

## 6. Dependencies

| Dependency | Status | Impact |
|---|---|---|
| TASK-011 (Desktop integration) | Open — blocked by physical cabling | Blocks all Desktop/GPU3060 asset inventory |
| TASK-012 (n8n triage) | Open — depends on TASK-011 | Blocks n8n workflow classification |
| D5.5 (Workflow Migration Plan) | Exists | Provides migration framework once assets are classified |

---

## 7. Inventory Process

When Desktop comes online:
1. Bridge connects to Desktop via SSH or Screen Sharing
2. Scan for n8n workflows, bot configs, scripts, prompts, dashboards
3. File each asset with ID, name, category, location, and initial classification
4. Review classifications with human
5. Begin migration per D5.5 framework

---

_This document is updated when assets are discovered, classified, or migrated. Retired assets are kept in the register with `Retire` classification for audit purposes._

docs.title: D5.4 — EXPERIMENT REGISTRY
docs.content:
# D5.4 — EXPERIMENT REGISTRY
**Autonomous AI Organization — Log of All Experiments, Outcomes, and Adoption Decisions**
_Last updated: 2026-03-13 | Status: Active | Authority: D1.1-ROADMAP.md §9_

---

## 1. Purpose

Every experiment the organization runs — new models, tools, methods, prompts, or workflows — is logged here with its outcome and adoption decision. This prevents re-running failed experiments, provides evidence for adoption decisions, and builds institutional knowledge.

---

## 2. Experiment Lifecycle

```
Proposed → Approved → Running → Completed → Decision (adopt / defer / reject)
```

**Rules:**
- Experiments require human approval before starting (unless pre-approved by task assignment)
- Every experiment has a defined scope, success criteria, and cost bound
- Results are filed here regardless of outcome — negative results are valuable
- Adoption decisions are recorded even when the decision is "not now"

---

## 3. Field Reference

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| `exp_id` | EXP-### sequential |
| `title` | Short descriptive title |
| `status` | `proposed` / `approved` / `running` / `completed` / `cancelled` |
| `proposed_by` | human / bridge / forge |
| `proposed_date` | YYYY-MM-DD |
| `started` | YYYY-MM-DD or null |
| `completed` | YYYY-MM-DD or null |
| `scope` | What is being tested and how |
| `success_criteria` | How to determine if the experiment succeeded |
| `cost_bound` | Maximum cost allowed for the experiment |
| `outcome` | What happened — data, observations, measurements |
| `decision` | `adopt` / `defer` / `reject` + rationale |
| `task_ref` | TASK-### if governed by a task |
| `capability_ref` | CAP-### if the experiment evaluates a capability |
| `notes` | Additional context, lessons learned |

---

## 4. Completed Experiments

### EXP-001 — Local Inference Stack Selection (Ollama + qwen2.5)

```
exp_id:           EXP-001
title:            Local inference stack selection — Ollama serving + qwen2.5 models
status:           completed
proposed_by:      human
proposed_date:    2026-03-13
started:          2026-03-13
completed:        2026-03-13
scope:            Select serving stack (Ollama vs llama.cpp vs other) and model family
                  for Mac Mini local inference. Install. Run D1.6 §5 stability test
                  (20 consecutive calls, 0 failures, RAM within budget).
success_criteria: 20/20 stability test pass for at least one model. RAM within 9.6GB
                  ceiling (60% of 16GB). Gateway remains responsive throughout.
cost_bound:       $0 (local inference, no API costs)
outcome:          Ollama 0.17.7 selected. Two models installed:
                  - qwen2.5:3b (Local-Nano): 20/20 PASS, 115ms avg, 2.2GB RAM
                  - qwen2.5:7b (Local-Mid): 20/20 PASS, 2.1s avg, 4.6GB RAM
                  Both within RAM budget. Gateway 0 failures. Metal acceleration confirmed.
decision:         adopt — Both models adopted. qwen2.5:3b as routing-class, qwen2.5:7b as
                  orchestration-class. Registered as CAP-008.
task_ref:         TASK-003, TASK-008
capability_ref:   CAP-008
notes:            Closes bootstrap exception (CHG-002). BRG-INF-01 satisfied.
                  Local-primary routing active. Cloud inference (CAP-001) demoted to fallback.
```

---

## 5. Running Experiments

_None currently running._

---

## 6. Proposed / Approved Experiments

### EXP-002 — Perplexity Capability Evaluation

```
exp_id:           EXP-002
title:            Perplexity capability evaluation — research, browser, UI-pilot roles
status:           proposed
proposed_by:      human
proposed_date:    2026-03-11
started:          null
completed:        null
scope:            Bounded evaluation of Perplexity for: (1) live web retrieval,
                  (2) current-information queries, (3) browser-style lookups,
                  (4) UI-pilot / device-mediated access. Document quality, latency,
                  cost profile, API access method, data exposure risk.
success_criteria: Evaluation report filed with clear recommendation per role.
                  Quantified where possible (latency, cost per query, accuracy).
cost_bound:       $2 maximum (per human directive)
outcome:          pending
decision:         pending — feeds TASK-014 scope decision
task_ref:         TASK-013
capability_ref:   CAP-006
notes:            Requires Perplexity API key (not yet provisioned) or human's
                  authenticated session. Key not registered until TASK-014 approves.
```

---

## 7. Planned Experiments (Backlog)

Per the operating directives, the following bounded experiments are required after baseline work is stable:

| ID | Title | Status | Dependencies |
|---|---|---|---|
| EXP-003 | Local-primary vs Claude-fallback routing comparison | planned | Baseline stable, usage data available |
| EXP-004 | Proxy-path with and without proxy trick | planned | Real test path identified |
| EXP-005 | Perplexity usefulness for research/retrieval | planned | EXP-002 complete, API key available |
| EXP-006 | Indirect/UI-mediated Cursor usage viability | planned | Perplexity or device-mediated lane available |

**Rule:** None of these become production defaults automatically. Each requires documented results and human adoption decision.

---

## 8. Cancelled / Rejected Experiments

_None yet._

---

_This document is updated when experiments are proposed, started, completed, or when adoption decisions are made._

docs.title: D5.5 — WORKFLOW MIGRATION PLAN
docs.content:
# D5.5 — WORKFLOW MIGRATION PLAN
**Autonomous AI Organization — Migration Path from Prior Automation Stack to New Architecture**
_Last updated: 2026-03-13 | Status: Blocked (Desktop offline) | Authority: D1.1-ROADMAP.md §2, §9_

---

## 1. Purpose

This document defines how prior workflows, automations, and infrastructure are migrated into the new organizational architecture. Migration is deliberate — every prior asset is evaluated, classified, and either migrated, adapted, or retired with documented rationale.

---

## 2. Migration Principles

1. **Inventory before migration.** No workflow is migrated until it is inventoried in D5.3.
2. **Classify before acting.** Every asset gets a Keep/Adapt/Retire classification before any work begins.
3. **Migrate before rebuilding.** If a prior workflow works, migrate it. Don't rebuild from scratch without evidence that the prior approach is broken.
4. **Execution substrates are subordinate.** n8n, scripts, browser automation, and external workflow tools serve Mission Control — they don't replace it. (D1.1 Principle 10)
5. **One migration at a time.** Complete each migration before starting the next. Partial migrations create debt.

---

## 3. Migration Framework

### Phase 1: Inventory (D5.3)
- Survey all machines for prior workflows, scripts, configs, prompts
- File each in D5.3-LEGACY-ASSET-REGISTER.md with classification
- Human reviews and approves classifications

### Phase 2: Assessment
For each **Keep** or **Adapt** asset:
- Document current function (what does it do?)
- Document current dependencies (what does it need?)
- Identify target execution layer in new architecture
- Estimate migration effort (hours, complexity)
- Identify risks (data loss, broken dependencies, missing credentials)

### Phase 3: Migration Execution
For each approved migration:
1. Create a TASK entry in TASK-REGISTER
2. Create a CHG entry if the migration changes operational state
3. Execute migration in the target execution layer
4. Test in controlled context
5. Verify output matches or exceeds prior workflow
6. Human accepts or requests changes
7. Close task and update D5.3

### Phase 4: Retirement
For each **Retire** asset:
- Archive the asset (do not delete — archive for reference)
- Document retirement rationale in D5.3
- Remove from active execution paths

---

## 4. Execution Layer Mapping

When migrating workflows, the target execution layer depends on what the workflow does:

| Workflow Type | Target Layer | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Scheduling / cron jobs | OpenClaw cron | Native capability |
| Message routing / notifications | OpenClaw broker (Telegram) | Native capability |
| Web research / data retrieval | Research Lane (forge) | May use Perplexity if CAP-006 activated |
| Commerce automation | brain (Merchant) + execution substrate | Phase 2+ |
| Content generation | forge (Builder) | Phase 3+ |
| Dashboard / monitoring | Mission Control visual layer | Phase 2+ |
| File management / archiving | brain (Archivist) | Phase 2+ |
| External API integrations | OpenClaw skills or execution substrate | Evaluated per integration |

---

## 5. Known Migration Candidates

These are expected based on prior discussions. Actual classification happens after D5.3 inventory.

| Category | Expected Count | Priority | Migration Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| n8n workflows | Unknown | High | Various — depends on function | Blocked (Desktop offline) |
| Discord bot configs | Unknown | Medium | Telegram bot config (adapted) | Blocked (Desktop offline) |
| Commerce scripts | Unknown | High | brain (Merchant) layer | Blocked (Desktop offline) |
| SSH configs | ~1 | Low | Mac Mini SSH setup | Partially complete (Screen Sharing active) |
| Dashboard setups | Unknown | Medium | MC visual layer | Blocked (Desktop offline) |
| Prompt packs | Unknown | Medium | Agent prompt contexts | Blocked (Desktop offline) |

---

## 6. Current Blockers

| Blocker | Impact | Resolution Path |
|---|---|---|
| Desktop offline (TASK-011) | Cannot inventory Desktop assets | Physical: basement switch uplink + power on |
| GPU3060 offline (TASK-011) | Cannot inventory GPU3060 assets | Physical: basement switch uplink + power on |
| No brain agent | Cannot migrate brain-targeted workflows | Phase 2 agent definition |

---

## 7. Migration Timeline

| Milestone | Target Phase | Dependencies |
|---|---|---|
| Inventory complete (D5.3 populated) | Phase 2 start | TASK-011 (Desktop online) |
| All Keep/Adapt assets classified | Phase 2 early | Inventory complete + human review |
| First migration complete | Phase 2 mid | Classification approved |
| All High-priority migrations done | Phase 2 end | Sequential execution |
| Remaining migrations done | Phase 3 start | High-priority complete |
| Retirement of Retire-classified assets | Phase 3 | All migrations complete |

---

_This document is updated when migration phases progress or when new asset categories are discovered._

docs.title: D5.6 — WORKFLOW_LIBRARY
docs.content:
# D5.6 — WORKFLOW_LIBRARY
**Autonomous AI Organization — Reusable Company Process Definitions**
_Last updated: 2026-03-13 | Status: Active | Parent: D1.1-ROADMAP.md_

---

## 1. Purpose

This document is the Workflow Library for the organization. It stores approved, reusable definitions for recurring work that agents have been authorized to execute without improvising a new approach each time.

A workflow is not a runbook (that is D2.x). It is not a delegation spec (that is D3.x). A workflow is a repeatable execution pattern — the step sequence, inputs, outputs, handoffs, and proof requirements for a specific class of work that happens often enough to warrant standardization.

**Why this matters:**
- Repeated patterns executed ad hoc are inconsistent, slow to improve, and hard to audit
- Approved workflows allow bridge (Conductor) to execute known work reliably without human re-explanation
- Workflow reuse is part of the swarm's self-managed operating responsibility (D1.8 §6.5)
- When the same pattern recurs without an approved workflow, that is a signal to create one

**What this document contains:**
- The workflow registry (list of approved workflows by ID)
- The format every workflow definition must follow
- The promotion process for converting ad-hoc patterns into approved workflows
- The deprecation process for workflows that are no longer valid

**What this document does not contain:**
- Runbook steps for setting up systems — D2.x
- Delegation specs defining task class authorization levels — D3.x
- Policy on what may be automated — D1.8-AUTOMATION_POLICY.md
- Experiment records — D5.4-EXPERIMENT-REGISTRY.md

---

## 2. Workflow Registry

The registry below is the canonical list of approved workflows. Agents may execute only workflows listed here and marked `active`. Workflows in `draft` status are not yet approved for agent-initiated execution.

| ID | Name | Owner Agent | Status | Automation Class | Risk Level | Phase Available |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| — | *(no workflows approved yet)* | — | — | — | — | — |

Workflows are added as approved patterns emerge from operations. The first workflows are expected to be promoted from Phase 1 manual practice (research intake, memory archival, daily digest). See §5 for the promotion process.

---

## 3. Workflow Definition Format

Every workflow in the library must conform to this format. Incomplete workflow definitions are not approved for agent-initiated execution.

```
---
id: WF-<sequential number, e.g. WF-001>
name: <Short human-readable name>
version: <integer, starting at 1>
status: draft | active | deprecated
owner_agent: bridge | brain | forge
automation_class: <Class 1–5 from D1.8 §4>
risk_level: <Level 1–4 from D1.8 §5>
autonomy_level_required: <0–5 from D1.4 §10>
phase_available: <phase number>
approved_by: human
approval_date: YYYY-MM-DD
last_reviewed: YYYY-MM-DD
source_task_pattern: <description of the recurring pattern this was derived from>
delegation_spec_ref: <D3.x reference if applicable, or null>
---

## Inputs
<What the workflow requires to start: data, references, task packet fields>

## Steps
<Numbered sequence of steps. Each step names the executing role, the action, and the expected output.>

1. [Role] — [Action] → [Output]
2. [Role] — [Action] → [Output]
...

## Decision Points
<Any step where the workflow branches. Describe each condition and which branch is taken.>

## Outputs
<What the workflow produces: proof artifact, memory entry, notification, or downstream task.>

## Handoffs
<Who receives the output and what they do with it.>

## Failure Handling
<What happens at each step if it fails. Reference escalation triggers from D1.8 §8 where applicable.>

## Proof Requirement
<What artifact confirms the workflow completed. This satisfies D1.8 §15.>

## Change Notes
<Version history: what changed and why.>
```

---

## 4. Self-Management Rules for Workflows

### 4.1 When Agents Use a Workflow

Bridge (Conductor) checks the workflow registry before executing any recurring task. If an active workflow exists for the task class, that workflow is used. The task record must reference the workflow ID. (Source: D1.3 BRG-SWM-03, D1.8 §6.5)

### 4.2 When No Workflow Exists

If no workflow exists for a task class being executed:
- The agent documents the approach used in the task record under `approach: ad-hoc`
- If the same pattern occurs 3 or more times, Conductor surfaces a workflow candidate proposal to the human (see §5.3)
- The human decides whether to promote it or leave it ad-hoc

Agents do not self-promote workflows to `active` status. Human approval is required. Self-managed, but not self-authorizing beyond policy. (Source: D1.1 §3 principle 15)

### 4.3 Workflow Execution Is Not Modification

Executing an approved workflow does not grant permission to modify it. If an agent encounters a step that no longer works as defined, it halts at that step, records the issue in the task record, and surfaces it in the next digest. The workflow is updated only through the change process in §5.4.

---

## 5. Workflow Lifecycle

### 5.1 Origin: Pattern Recognition

A workflow candidate originates when the same task pattern recurs. The trigger is either:
- Human notices a pattern and requests a workflow be created
- Conductor detects 3+ ad-hoc executions of the same task type within a 30-day window and surfaces a candidate

### 5.2 Draft Creation

When a candidate is surfaced, a workflow draft is created:
- Conductor (or the human) writes the draft using the format in §3
- Status is set to `draft`
- The draft is filed in this document under the registry
- Conductor does not execute the draft as an approved workflow — it remains ad-hoc until promoted

### 5.3 Candidate Surface Format

When Conductor surfaces a workflow candidate, it posts to the ops-channel in this format:

```
Workflow candidate: [task name]
Pattern observed: [N] executions in last [N] days
Consistent approach used: yes / no
Draft available: yes (WF-XXX draft in D5.6) / no
Action: Review and approve, modify draft, or decline
```

### 5.4 Promotion to Active

A workflow draft is promoted to `active` when:
1. The human reviews the draft and approves it (Telegram or Mission Control record)
2. The `status` field is updated to `active`
3. The `approved_by` and `approval_date` fields are populated
4. The workflow is added to the registry table in §2

Promotion may not be initiated by an agent.

### 5.5 Versioning

When an active workflow is modified:
- The version number increments
- The `change_notes` section records what changed and why
- The prior version is not deleted — it is retained in the `change_notes` for audit
- If the change is significant (different risk level, different output target, different steps), re-approval is required before the new version is executed

### 5.6 Deprecation

A workflow is deprecated when:
- The task class it covers is no longer approved or has been superseded by a new approach
- The workflow is consistently failing and cannot be corrected within scope
- The human explicitly retires it

Deprecated workflows are marked `deprecated` in the registry. They are not deleted. A deprecated workflow may not be executed by an agent.

---

## 6. Blocked / TBD

| Item | Blocked On | Expected Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| First workflow candidates | No recurring patterns established yet — operations not yet started | Phase 1 manual operation; first candidates surfaced after 2–4 weeks |
| Research intake workflow (WF-001 candidate) | D3.1-DELEGATION-SPEC-RESEARCH.md not written; Mode 2 intake not yet practiced | Phase 2 after Mode 1 establishes the pattern |
| Daily digest workflow (WF-002 candidate) | Bridge not yet connected to Telegram; no digests generated | Phase 1 after Telegram is live |
| Memory archival workflow (WF-003 candidate) | Desktop not yet online; Archivist not yet active | Phase 2 |
| Pattern detection automation (Conductor scanning for 3+ ad-hoc repeats) | Bridge agent not yet defined | Phase 1+ after bridge is operational |
| Perplexity-assisted research workflow (WF-004 candidate) | Perplexity (CAP-006) not yet approved or activated; TASK-013 and TASK-014 must close first | Phase 1/2 after CAP-006 adopted; pattern observed in Mode 1/2 research intake before workflow is drafted |
| Cursor supervised engineering handoff workflow (WF-005 candidate) | Engineering delegation spec (D3.2) not yet written; Cursor supervised/manual tool lane not yet exercised in production; Cursor is not the autonomous runtime — it requires human-in-the-loop or explicit task assignment | Phase 2 after first Builder delegation cycles observed |
| Device-mediated access workflow (WF-006 candidate) | CAP-007 (device-mediated lane) not yet approved; no device-mediated tasks yet executed; pattern cannot be established before the lane is exercised | Phase 2 after CAP-007 candidate is evaluated and at least 3 device-mediated task executions have been observed |
| Orchestration assist workflow via Perplexity (WF-007 candidate) | CAP-006 role 3 (orchestration assist) not yet scoped or activated; depends on TASK-014 scope decision | Phase 2 after CAP-006 scope decision includes orchestration assist lane |
| Indirect Cursor usage via device-mediated control (WF-008 candidate) | CAP-006 (Perplexity UI-pilot role) and/or CAP-007 (device-mediated lane) not yet approved; no indirect Cursor usage yet observed; Perplexity or OpenClaw operates Cursor Claude chat through the UI without direct API integration (D1.5 §15.5) | Phase 2 after CAP-006/CAP-007 adopted and at least 3 indirect Cursor usage instances observed |

---

## 7. Immediate Priorities

1. **Documentation consistency patch workflow is a candidate** — Five documentation consistency patches (CHG-001 through CHG-005) have been executed with a consistent approach. Per D1.15 §8.5 and §4.2, this pattern has crossed the 3-execution threshold. A workflow candidate should be drafted at the next Phase 1 checkpoint covering: identifying affected documents, writing a change record, executing targeted text patches, verifying consistency, and committing. This is the first real workflow candidate to emerge from observed operations.

2. **Add workflow candidate tracking to Mission Control task register** — When the automation register is created (D1.8 §18 priority 5), include a field for `workflow_candidate: yes/no` on task records. This enables Conductor to track patterns without manual counting. (Source: §5.1)

3. **Write first workflow draft after 2–4 weeks of Phase 1 operation** — The daily digest and research intake are the most likely first workflow candidates. Do not draft them now — draft them after the actual execution pattern is observed and understood. (Source: §5.2)

4. **Register D5.6 as a writable surface in Mission Control** — The Workflow Library is a living document. Bridge (Conductor) must have write access to add draft entries and surface candidates. Confirm this access path is functional when the broader Mission Control access policy is resolved (D1.3 MC-ACC-03). (Source: D1.3 BRG-SWM-03, BRG-SWM-04)

---

_This document grows through operations, not through speculation. Workflows are promoted from observed reality, not designed in advance. When the first real patterns emerge from Phase 1 operation, return here and create the first entries._

docs.title: D5.7 — CAPABILITY REGISTER
docs.content:
# D5.7 — CAPABILITY REGISTER
**Autonomous AI Organization — Master Register of All Approved Capabilities**
_Last updated: 2026-03-13 | Status: Active | Authority: D1.12-CAPABILITY_ACQUISITION_POLICY.md_

---

## Register Governance

**Authoritative for:** Approval status and compliance fields for all capability categories (CAP-MOD, CAP-SKL, CAP-AUT, CAP-SVC, CAP-PLT, CAP-WFM, CAP-INF)

**Relationship to D5.2:** D5.2-SKILL-REGISTRY.md is a specialized index for CAP-SKL invocation metadata only. D5.7 is the master compliance register. When a skill is registered, it has a full record here and a reference entry in D5.2 for invocation metadata. D5.7 is authoritative for approval and compliance. D5.2 is authoritative for invocation metadata.

**Write authority (D1.12 §8.1):**
- Bridge (Conductor) may update: `status`, `last_reviewed`, `review_due`, `notes`, cost usage tracking
- Bridge may NOT change: `approved_by`, `approval_date`, `risk_class`, `authorized_agents`, `authorized_task_classes` — these require a new human approval decision

**Schema version:** 1

---

## Field Reference

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| `id` | CAP-### sequential (CAP-001, CAP-002, …) |
| `name` | Short human-readable name |
| `category` | CAP-MOD / CAP-SKL / CAP-AUT / CAP-SVC / CAP-PLT / CAP-WFM / CAP-INF |
| `status` | `candidate` / `approved` / `active` / `deprecated` / `retired` / `declined` |
| `risk_class` | 1 (Low) / 2 (Medium) / 3 (High) / 4 (Critical) |
| `version` | Integer starting at 1; increments on scope/risk/cost change |
| `approved_by` | `human` |
| `approval_date` | YYYY-MM-DD |
| `authorized_agents` | Which agents may use this capability |
| `authorized_task_classes` | Which task classes it may be used in |
| `cost_ceiling` | Approved monthly cost limit; null if free |
| `experiment_ref` | D5.4 experiment ID, or null |
| `source_ref` | D1.10 research packet ID, or null |
| `last_reviewed` | YYYY-MM-DD |
| `review_due` | YYYY-MM-DD (cadence: Risk 1=annual, Risk 2=6-month, Risk 3=quarterly, Risk 4=monthly) |
| `notes` | Conditions, restrictions, known issues, change_notes for version history |

---

## Status: Phase 1 Bootstrap

No capabilities formally registered yet. Phase 1 bootstrap capabilities are pre-approved by organizational policy without individual capability records — see §9 of this register for the bootstrap list. All other capabilities require an adoption proposal (D1.12 §7.2) and explicit human approval before receiving a record here.

---

## Phase 1 Bootstrap Capabilities

The following capabilities are implicitly approved as Phase 1 required infrastructure. Formal records will be created once the register tooling decision is made (D1.15 §15 — flat markdown vs. YAML). Until then, these are tracked here in the bootstrap section.

| ID | Name | Category | Status | Authorized Agents | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CAP-001 | Anthropic Claude API (claude-sonnet-4-6) | CAP-MOD | active | bridge | Phase 1 primary inference. Risk Class 3. Approved implicitly as Phase 1 required capability. Requires SEC-001 (Anthropic API key) active. Cost ceiling: TBD — set by human at first billing review. |
| CAP-002 | Telegram Bot API | CAP-SVC | active | bridge (Broker) | Phase 1 primary human communication channel. Risk Class 3. Requires SEC-002 (Telegram bot token) active. Approved implicitly as Phase 1 required capability. |
| CAP-003 | macOS Keychain (Mac Mini) | CAP-INF | active | bridge | Phase 1 secret storage substrate. Risk Class 3. Machine-local. No external dependency. |
| CAP-004 | Mission Control git repository | CAP-INF | active | bridge (Conductor) | Version-controlled operational corpus. Phase 1 required. Risk Class 1. |
| CAP-005 | OpenClaw gateway runtime | CAP-INF | active | bridge | Phase 1 agent runtime substrate. Risk Class 3. Registered when model path confirmed per D1.6. |
| CAP-008 | Ollama local inference (Mac Mini) | CAP-INF | active | bridge | Ollama 0.17.7 on :11434 with Metal acceleration. Models: qwen2.5:3b (Local-Nano), qwen2.5:7b (Local-Mid). Risk Class 2. Stability tested 20/20 both models. RAM within budget. CHG-007. |

> **Note:** CAP-001 through CAP-005 require formal records once Phase 1 is operational and the adoption proposal process is running. Bootstrap approval does not substitute for formal registration — it authorizes activation while formal registration is pending.

---

## Registered Capabilities

_None yet. First record created at first formal adoption proposal approval._

---

## Candidates Under Evaluation

```
id:                    CAP-006
name:                  Perplexity (live research / browser / computer-use)
category:              CAP-SVC
status:                candidate
risk_class:            2 (Medium) — provisional; elevated to 3 if org-level credentials with billing exposure are activated
version:               1
approved_by:           pending
approval_date:         pending
authorized_agents:     pending (intended: forge (Researcher), bridge (Conductor) for research task classes)
authorized_task_classes: pending (intended: live web research, current-information retrieval, browser/computer-use tasks requiring real-time data)
cost_ceiling:          pending (set before activation — usage-based billing assessment required)
experiment_ref:        TASK-013
source_ref:            null
last_reviewed:         2026-03-11
review_due:            2026-06-11
notes:                 Candidate status filed under CHG-003. Updated under CHG-005 to reflect
                       candidate roles. Updated under CHG-008 to reflect 4 candidate roles:
                       (1) Live research lane — live web retrieval, current-information queries not
                           available in model weights; primary intended role.
                       (2) Preferred UI-pilot lane — operating apps, browsers, chatboxes, and devices
                           through their native interface. This includes indirect operation of Cursor
                           Claude chat without requiring direct API integration (D1.5 §15.5). When
                           device-mediated access is the right path, Perplexity is the preferred
                           operator. WF-008 candidate documents this pattern.
                       (3) Access-reduction / device lane — device automation assistance, computer-use
                           fallback for access tasks where stable API paths are unavailable; overlaps
                           with CAP-007 candidate scope — the two are evaluated together in TASK-013.
                       (4) Orchestration assist lane — real-time context retrieval during planning and
                           dispatch decisions; lower priority than roles 1–3.
                       Perplexity is a callable external service — not a runtime agent, not part of
                       the D1.5 inference routing stack, and not a message channel. Evaluation:
                       TASK-013 (experiment). Scope decision: TASK-014 (activate / defer / reject —
                       covers all four roles as a unified scope decision). If activated, an API key
                       must be registered in D1.14 as Risk Class 3 before any production use. Risk
                       class elevated to 3 if device-lane or orchestration-assist role is activated.
                       API-based invocation is the preferred pattern; visual GUI automation of
                       Perplexity requires explicit task packet approval.
```

```

```
id:                    CAP-007
name:                  Device-Mediated Access Lane (computer-use / device-piloting / GUI automation)
category:              CAP-AUT
status:                candidate
risk_class:            3 (High) — device automation with write access to external surfaces; escalates
                       risk class of any task it executes
version:               1
approved_by:           pending
approval_date:         pending
authorized_agents:     pending (intended: bridge (Conductor) for orchestration; forge (Builder) for
                       implementation — all device-mediated actions require explicit task packet approval)
authorized_task_classes: pending (intended: access tasks where no stable API path or built-in capability
                          exists; fallback lane only — not a primary lane)
cost_ceiling:          null (no external billing; device-mediated access has machine resource cost only)
experiment_ref:        TASK-013 (partial — overlaps with Perplexity device-lane role evaluation)
source_ref:            null
last_reviewed:         2026-03-13
review_due:            2026-06-13
notes:                 Candidate filed under CHG-005 (Access Minimization and Tool-Lane Execution Patch).
                       Updated under CHG-008 to include indirect Cursor usage as a documented use case.
                       Device-mediated access is a fallback lane — it is only authorized when:
                       (1) No stable built-in or approved API-based capability can fulfill the task
                       (2) The task has an explicit approved task packet declaring target surface,
                           scope, expected output, and why stable paths are unavailable
                       (3) The device-mediated action is at Risk Level 3 minimum (external write
                           access assumed) and follows D1.8 §13 external action policy
                       A specific, documented use case of CAP-007 is indirect Cursor usage —
                       operating Cursor Claude chat through the UI (navigating, pasting prompts,
                       reading responses) without requiring direct API integration. This is a
                       legitimate access path, not a workaround (D1.5 §15.5, WF-008 candidate).
                       Device-mediated access is not improvised — each use is a governed execution
                       against an approved task packet. CAP-007 overlaps with the CAP-006 role 2
                       (Perplexity UI-pilot/device lane) evaluation. Both are evaluated in TASK-013.
                       If Perplexity's computer-use capability is adopted as the device-mediated lane,
                       CAP-007 may be fulfilled by CAP-006 role 2 rather than a separate capability.
                       This overlap is resolved in TASK-014. Screen-driving applications without an
                       approved task packet is prohibited regardless of CAP-007 status.
```

---

## Retired / Declined Capabilities

_None yet. Declined records are preserved here with status `declined` and the rejection reason._

---

## Register Maintenance

- **Bridge (Conductor)** surfaces `review_due` dates in the weekly digest
- **Human** approves all new entries, scope changes, and risk class changes
- **Review cadence:** Risk Class 1 = annual; Risk Class 2 = 6-month; Risk Class 3 = quarterly; Risk Class 4 = monthly
- **Legacy capabilities** (capabilities already in use without a register entry) are surfaced through legacy asset review (D1.12 §5.5) and must be registered or retired within 90 days of discovery

docs.title: 2026-03-28 — Captain's Initial Vision Capture
docs.content:
# 2026-03-28 — Captain's Initial Vision Capture

## Raw Ideas (from Captain's voice)

### The Dojo & Kitchen Moment
"One of the things that opened my eyes to this vision was Zoro's dojo sessions where he teaches cybersecurity. It became so fun because of all the metaphors. Same with the kitchen metaphors on Sanji. It made me think — what if other One Piece fans saw this?"

Key insight: The character-driven experience IS the product. When Zoro teaches you cybersecurity through swordsman metaphors, learning becomes an adventure. When Sanji structures automation as cooking recipes, complex systems become approachable. This isn't a gimmick — it's the core UX.

### The Video Game as Platform
"Imagine a video game where each character is real and everyone gets their ship and crew."

- Not a traditional game — a platform for real-life productivity disguised as a game
- You walk in, make goals, Mind into Matter — goals come out as real products
- Each character is a real AI agent with personality, voice, visual
- Streaming-friendly — every session is content

### The Viral Loop
"We will need to give ourselves time and build this even on streams in the future showing people how fun it is to talk to your favorite characters."

Content strategy:
- X posts → daily, automated
- X live streams → showcase conversations with crew
- YouTube → longer builds, tutorials, character showcases
- Instagram → visual content, character art, screenshots
- Platform expansion over time — start with X, expand naturally

### The Automation Goal
"We will automate the process in the future for content making, ideas, and daily content where X posts and streamings can take place."

This means:
- Robin researches trending topics
- Usopp writes content briefs
- Brook creates visual content
- Sanji automates posting pipeline
- The crew literally runs the content operation

### Multiple Projects to Laugh Tale
"There will have to be multiple projects that can get us there."

The road to Laugh Tale isn't one project — it's a journey of projects:
1. PROJ-OPENSOURCE — open source the crew
2. PROJ-STREAMING — content & brand building
3. PROJ-GAME — the video game experience
4. PROJ-CHARACTERS — bring characters to life
5. (Future projects TBD as vision evolves)

Each project is a major goal that breaks down into epics → stories → tasks in our system.

docs.title: Task Deduplication API — Paperclip Phase 1
docs.content:
# Task Deduplication API — Paperclip Phase 1

**Version:** 1.0  
**Author:** Franky (Chief Engineer)  
**Date:** 2026-03-28

## Overview

The Task Deduplication Gate prevents creation of duplicate tasks in Mission Control by implementing fuzzy string matching with a 70% similarity threshold. This is part of Paperclip Phase 1 automation improvements.

## Endpoints

### POST /api/tasks/check-duplicate

Check if a task title/description matches existing tasks without creating the task.

**Request:**
```json
{
  "title": "Task title to check",
  "description": "Optional task description",
  "skipDuplicateCheck": false  // Optional bypass flag
}
```

**Response (No Duplicate):**
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "isDuplicate": false,
  "checkedAgainst": 381,
  "meta": {
    "algorithm": "dice coefficient via string-similarity",
    "titleWeight": 0.7,
    "descriptionWeight": 0.3,
    "threshold": 0.7,
    "timestamp": "2026-03-28T23:22:27.923Z"
  }
}
```

**Response (Duplicate Found):**
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "isDuplicate": true,
  "similarTask": {
    "id": "EXISTING-TASK-ID",
    "title": "Existing Task Title",
    "status": "queued",
    "assigned": "Agent",
    "priority": "P1"
  },
  "similarity": 0.85,
  "titleSimilarity": 0.90,
  "descriptionSimilarity": 0.75,
  "threshold": 0.7,
  "meta": { ... }
}
```

**Response (Bypassed):**
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "isDuplicate": false,
  "bypassed": true
}
```

### POST /api/tasks (Enhanced)

The existing task creation endpoint now includes automatic duplicate checking.

**Request (Standard):**
```json
{
  "task_id": "TASK-001",
  "title": "Task title",
  "notes": "Task description",
  "status": "queued",
  "priority": "P1",
  "assigned": "Agent"
}
```

**Request (With Bypass):**
```json
{
  "task_id": "TASK-001",
  "title": "Task title",
  "notes": "Task description",
  "status": "queued",
  "priority": "P1", 
  "assigned": "Agent",
  "skipDuplicateCheck": true  // Bypass duplicate detection
}
```

**Response (Success):**
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "task": { ... },
  "duplicateCheckBypassed": false
}
```

**Response (Duplicate Blocked):**
```http
HTTP/1.1 409 Conflict
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "error": "Duplicate task detected",
  "duplicate": true,
  "similarity": 0.85,
  "similarTask": {
    "id": "EXISTING-TASK-ID",
    "title": "Existing Task Title", 
    "status": "queued",
    "assigned": "Agent",
    "priority": "P1"
  },
  "message": "Task similar to existing \"Existing Task Title\" (85% match). Use skipDuplicateCheck:true to bypass.",
  "bypassHint": "Add \"skipDuplicateCheck\": true to override this check"
}
```

## Algorithm Details

### Similarity Calculation

- **Library:** `string-similarity` (dice coefficient)
- **Title Weight:** 70% 
- **Description Weight:** 30%
- **Threshold:** 70% combined similarity
- **Case Handling:** Normalized to lowercase

### Scoring Formula
```
combinedScore = (titleSimilarity * 0.7) + (descriptionSimilarity * 0.3)
isDuplicate = combinedScore >= 0.70
```

### Edge Cases

1. **Completed Tasks:** Excluded from duplicate checking (don't block new work)
2. **Missing Description:** Only title similarity used (description weight = 0)
3. **Emergency Bypass:** `skipDuplicateCheck: true` overrides all checks
4. **Empty Database:** Returns `checkedAgainst: 0` with no duplicates

## Security & Performance

- **Rate Limiting:** Uses same limits as task creation
- **Input Validation:** Title required, description optional
- **Performance:** O(n) scan of active tasks (~381 currently, acceptable)
- **Bypass Logging:** All bypasses logged for audit trail

## Testing Examples

### Test 1: No Duplicate
```bash
curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18800/api/tasks/check-duplicate \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"title": "Unique task name", "description": "New feature"}'
```

### Test 2: High Similarity
```bash
curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18800/api/tasks/check-duplicate \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"title": "Fix login bug", "description": "Authentication issue"}'
```

### Test 3: Create with Bypass
```bash
curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18800/api/tasks \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "task_id": "EMERGENCY-001",
    "title": "Critical hotfix",
    "skipDuplicateCheck": true
  }'
```

## Integration Notes

### Frontend Integration
1. Call `/api/tasks/check-duplicate` before showing task creation form
2. If duplicate detected, show warning with link to similar task
3. Offer "Create Anyway" button that sets `skipDuplicateCheck: true`
4. Display similarity percentage and similar task details

### Emergency Procedures
- Captain-level tasks can use `skipDuplicateCheck: true`
- System-generated tasks should include bypass flag
- Hotfix/critical tasks bypass automatically

## Limitations

- **Library Deprecation:** string-similarity@4.0.4 is deprecated but functional
- **Language Support:** English-optimized (dice coefficient works with Latin scripts)
- **Context Awareness:** No semantic understanding, only string similarity
- **Scale Limit:** Performance degrades beyond ~10K active tasks

## Future Enhancements

- **Semantic Matching:** Upgrade to embedding-based similarity
- **Performance:** Add indexing for large task datasets
- **ML Integration:** Learn from user bypass patterns
- **Batch Processing:** Support multiple task checks

---

**Implementation Status:** ✅ Complete  
**Commit:** TBD (build phase)  
**Testing:** Manual verification passed  
**Ready for:** Integration and deployment

docs.title: CHANGE REGISTER
docs.content:
# CHANGE REGISTER
**Autonomous AI Organization — Controlled Change Record**
_Last updated: 2026-03-13 | Authority: D1.15-CHANGE_CONTROL.md_

---

## Register Governance

This file is the operational record of all controlled changes. It is the mandatory companion to D1.15-CHANGE_CONTROL.md. A change without a record here has not gone through change control.

**Path:** `registry/changes/CHANGE-REGISTER.md`
**Must be committed to git:** Yes — every record is committed before or immediately upon execution
**Write authority:** Bridge (Conductor) may create records and update status fields. Human-authority fields (`approval_status`, `approved_by`, `approved_date`) are set only upon human approval received via Telegram + MC record per D1.15 §7.1.

**Record schema version:** 1 (18 fields per D1.15 §6.1)

---

## Field Reference

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| `change_id` | CHG-### sequential |
| `title` | Short descriptive title |
| `category` | From D1.15 §4 (1–12) |
| `risk_class` | Low / Medium / High / Critical |
| `proposed_by` | human / bridge / brain / forge |
| `proposed_date` | YYYY-MM-DD |
| `description` | What the change does and why |
| `scope` | What files, machines, capabilities, or services are affected |
| `affected_documents` | D-series documents affected |
| `affected_capabilities` | CAP-### IDs affected; null if none |
| `affected_secrets` | SEC-### IDs affected; null if none |
| `approval_status` | `pending` / `approved` / `rejected` / `deferred` |
| `approved_by` | `human` |
| `approved_date` | YYYY-MM-DD |
| `execution_window` | Declared window for execution (required for High and Critical) |
| `rollback_plan` | What is done if the change must be reversed |
| `execution_status` | `not-started` / `in-progress` / `completed` / `rolled-back` / `failed` |
| `proof_ref` | Path or reference to proof artifact confirming completion |
| `notes` | Any conditions, observations, or post-execution notes |

---

## Open Changes

### CHG-009 — Runtime Freeze and Crew-Architecture Transition

```
change_id:           CHG-009
title:               Runtime Freeze — Crew-Architecture Transition (Nami/Franky/Chopper)
category:            5 — Agent Configuration Activation (D1.15 §4.5)
risk_class:          Medium
proposed_by:         human
proposed_date:       2026-03-14
description:         Freeze the old single-machine OpenClaw runtime to prevent autonomous
                     procedures from running under outdated architecture assumptions.
                     System entering crew realignment: Nami (Mac Mini), Franky (Desktop),
                     Chopper (3060 Laptop). Old bridge/brain/forge role assignments and
                     cron jobs paused until reconfirmed under new architecture.
scope:               OpenClaw cron jobs, autonomous task pickup, runtime assumptions.
                     No config destroyed — freeze only.
affected_documents:  TRANSITION-NOTE.md, TASK-REGISTER, CHANGE-REGISTER, HANDOFF-STATUS.md
affected_capabilities: None removed. Daily digest cron disabled (not deleted).
affected_secrets:    null
approval_status:     approved
approved_by:         human
approved_date:       2026-03-14
execution_window:    2026-03-14 — immediate
rollback_plan:       openclaw cron enable f7102d3d-a629-4052-ba5e-41abab5cffb9
                     No config was changed or deleted. Re-enable cron to restore.
execution_status:    in-progress
proof_ref:           registry/TRANSITION-NOTE.md + TASK-023
notes:               Gateway left running as communication backbone. Ollama left running.
                     Telegram channel left enabled for manual use. Only autonomous
                     procedures frozen. Resume requires human approval after crew
                     architecture is confirmed and SSH verified.
```

---

### CHG-008 — Tool-Lane Model Correction (TASK-017)

                         Cursor Claude chat through the UI without direct API integration
                     (D) Make Perplexity the preferred UI-pilot lane for app/device/chatbox interaction
                     (E) Keep Perplexity as optional fallback/operator, not the orchestration brain
                     (F) Add human visual-approval boundary for UI/surfaces

                     docs/D2.0, D2.1, D5.6, D5.7,
                     registry/tasks/TASK-REGISTER, registry/changes/CHANGE-REGISTER
affected_documents:  D1.1, D1.2, D1.3, D1.4, D1.5, D1.8, D1.11, D1.12, D1.15, D2.0, D2.1, D5.6, D5.7
affected_capabilities: CAP-006 (notes updated — 4 candidate roles including UI-pilot),
                       CAP-007 (notes updated — indirect Cursor usage as documented use case)
affected_secrets:    null
approval_status:     approved
approved_by:         human
approved_date:       2026-03-13
execution_window:    2026-03-13 — documentation only; no operational state change
rollback_plan:       git revert — no operational state affected
execution_status:    completed
proof_ref:           TASK-017 + 00ec0c8
notes:               After this patch, the corpus is aligned to:
                     - OpenClaw = primary autonomous runtime, default for backlog and MC buildout
                     - Cursor = supervised/manual engineering lane, not autonomous runtime
                     - Perplexity = preferred UI-pilot for app/device/chatbox interaction (candidate)
                     - Indirect Cursor usage via device-mediated control = documented access path
                     - Visual surfaces require human visual approval before acceptance
                     - Mission Control buildout defaults to OpenClaw-led execution
                     New sections added: D1.5 §15.5 (indirect Cursor usage), D1.5 §15.6 (visual
                     approval boundary), D1.3 BRG-TRL-05 (visual approval requirement).
                     New workflow candidate: WF-008 (indirect Cursor usage via device-mediated control).
                     14 files patched. TASK-017 closed.
```

---

### CHG-007 — Local Inference Activation (TASK-003, TASK-008)

```
change_id:           CHG-007
title:               Local Inference Activation — Ollama + qwen2.5 Nano/Mid on Mac Mini
category:            5 — Agent Configuration Activation (D1.15 §4.5)

                     routing switch; D1.6 §4 survey update; D1.5 §9 bootstrap exception close;

execution_window:    2026-03-13 activation session

execution_status:    completed
proof_ref:           Nano stability: 20/20 PASS 115ms avg 2.2GB RAM; Mid stability: 20/20 PASS 2.1s avg 4.6GB RAM; Telegram msg_id 11; 94e9135
notes:               Closes bootstrap exception opened in CHG-002. BRG-INF-01 satisfied.
                     D1.6 §14 Steps 1-5 complete. Mac Mini running both Local-Nano and

### CHG-003 — Tool Role Specialization — OpenClaw / Cursor / Perplexity Lane Definitions
